1
Phase 01
Network+ Fundamentals
Days 01–07 · 7 sessions
DAY 1
What is Networking? OSI Model Explained
Do you know how your data actually travels across the internet?
#NetworkPlus#OSIModel#Day1of90
dark What is Networking? Definition
The System That Connects Everything
DevicesProtocolsData Sharing

Networking is the system of interconnected devices sharing data — computers, phones, servers, routers — all communicating over wires, Wi-Fi, or fibre.

Think of it as a global postal system: your data is a letter, IP addresses are house numbers, routers are sorting offices, and protocols are the delivery rules.

Every single cyber attack travels over a network. You cannot hack — or defend — what you do not understand.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Video 1
red Why OSI Matters Importance
Why OSI is Your Hacker's Bible

The OSI model is a 7-layer framework that describes exactly how data moves from device to device.

For a hacker, each layer = a different attack surface. Every protocol, exploit, and defence tool maps to a specific OSI layer.

When you hear 'Layer 7 DDoS attack' or 'Layer 3 routing issue' — that is the OSI model in real professional use.

Knowing OSI = speaking the language of networking. Without it you are guessing. With it you know exactly where to attack and defend.

30 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Playlist 1–3
layers OSI Layers 1–3 OSI Model
Physical · Data Link · Network
1PhysicalCables, Wi-Fi signals, electrical impulses
2Data LinkMAC addresses · Ethernet frames · ARP
3NetworkIP routing · Logical addressing · Packets
Layer 2 → target of ARP spoofing (MITM attacks). Layer 3 → target of IP spoofing and routing attacks.
white OSI Layers 4–5 Transport & Session
Transport & Session Layers

Layer 4 — Transport: TCP vs UDP. Port numbers live here. Nmap port scanning, SYN flood attacks, and TCP handshake exploits all operate at this layer.

TCP — reliable, ordered, connection-based. Used by HTTP, SSH, FTP. Vulnerable to SYN flood DoS attacks.

UDP — fast, connectionless. Used by DNS, VoIP. Target of UDP amplification DDoS attacks.

Layer 5 — Session: Manages connection states. Session hijacking attacks target this layer.

20 min · TryHackMe 'Intro to Networking'
dark OSI Layers 6–7 Top Layers
Presentation & Application

Layer 6 — Presentation: Encryption (SSL/TLS), data encoding, and format conversion. HTTPS protects your data here.

Layer 7 — Application: HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP — everything you touch daily. This is where most attacks happen: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, and phishing all live here.

Web application hacking (later in this series) operates almost entirely at Layer 7 — the richest attack surface in cybersecurity.

20 min · Cloudflare OSI Learning Center
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 1 Checklist
  • 1Name all 7 OSI layers in order — Physical to Application
  • 2Know which protocols belong to each layer — HTTP=L7, TCP=L4, IP=L3
  • 3Understand TCP vs UDP and why it matters for hacking
  • 4Memorise the mnemonic: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away (layers 1→7)
  • 5Map one attack type to each layer — this is how pros think
Total Day 1 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Learn From The Best — All Free
📺 Primary Video Prof. Messer — CompTIA N10-009 Playlist 1–3 professormesser.com/network-plus/n10-009
🧪 Hands-On Lab TryHackMe — 'What is Networking?' Room tryhackme.com/room/whatisnetworking
📖 Deep Dive Cloudflare Learning Center — OSI Model Guide cloudflare.com/learning/ddos/glossary/osi-model
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 1
  • 1Watch Prof. Messer N10-009 Playlist 1–3 (60 min) — take notes as you go
  • 2Draw the OSI model from memory — 7 layers, without looking
  • 3Label each layer with a real protocol: HTTP (L7), TCP (L4), IP (L3), Ethernet (L2)
  • 4Complete TryHackMe 'What is Networking?' room — apply everything you just learned
Hands-On Task: 30 – 45 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 1
"Master OSI first — every tool, attack, and protocol you will learn for the next 89 days maps directly back to these 7 layers."
The OSI model is not just theory — it is the mental map professionals use to diagnose problems in seconds. Network+ and OSCP examiners expect you to think in layers.
⚠ Common Mistake
Jumping straight to TCP/IP without mastering OSI. TCP/IP is a simplified 4-layer version — skip OSI and you will hit a wall when debugging complex attacks.
DAY 2
TCP/IP Model & IP Addressing
What's your IP address — and why should every hacker know this?
#NetworkPlus#TCPIP#Day2of90
dark TCP/IP vs OSI Foundation
TCP/IP: The Internet's Real Blueprint
TCP/IPIPv4Subnetting

While OSI has 7 layers, the real internet runs on TCP/IP — a practical 4-layer model used by every device on earth.

OSI is conceptual. TCP/IP is operational. It combines OSI's top 3 into Application, keeps Transport, merges Network+Internet, and collapses Physical+Data Link into Network Access.

For hacking: Every exploit, payload, and reverse shell travels through TCP/IP. Understanding how packets move is non-negotiable for any penetration tester.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Playlist
red IPv4 Basics Critical Concept
IPv4: The Address System of the Internet

IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses written in dotted decimal notation — for example: 192.168.1.1. Each of the 4 octets ranges from 0 to 255.

Public vs Private IPs: Public IPs are routable on the internet. Private IPs are used inside local networks and never appear on the public internet.

Why IPv4 is running out: Only ~4.3 billion addresses exist. With billions of devices online, address exhaustion is a real problem — solved by NAT and IPv6.

For pentesters: Knowing public vs private ranges is essential — private IPs reveal internal network structure during assessments.

25 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 IP Addressing Section
dark IPv6 Basics Modern Addressing
IPv6: The Future of Addressing

IPv6 uses 128-bit hex addresses — written as 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334. Colons separate 8 groups of 4 hex digits.

Why it matters: IPv6 provides 340 undecillion unique addresses — solving IPv4 exhaustion permanently. Most modern networks run dual-stack (both IPv4 and IPv6).

Security implication: Many firewalls and IDS rules are written for IPv4 only. IPv6 traffic on a network is sometimes unfiltered — an attack surface pentesters exploit.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 IPv6 Section
white Private IP Ranges Reference
Private IP Ranges — Memorise These
10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255 /8 (Class A)
172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255 /12 (Class B)
192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255 /16 (Class C)
127.0.0.1 = Loopback (localhost)
169.254.x.x = APIPA (no DHCP assigned)
15 min · Flashcard drill — memorise all 3 ranges
yellow Subnetting Intro What to Learn
Day 2 Checklist
  • 1Understand the 4-layer TCP/IP model and how it maps to OSI
  • 2Memorise IPv4 dotted decimal notation and the 3 private ranges
  • 3Understand CIDR notation — /24 = 256 addresses, /16 = 65,536 addresses
  • 4Run `ipconfig` (Windows) or `ip a` (Linux) — identify your IP, subnet, gateway
  • 5Understand why subnetting matters for network segmentation and pentest scoping
Total Day 2 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer N10-009 — IP Addressing Section professormesser.com/network-plus/n10-009
🧪 Practice SubnettingPractice.com — Free Subnetting Drills subnettingpractice.com
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'What is Networking?' Room tryhackme.com/room/whatisnetworking
📖 Visual Practical Networking — Subnetting Mastery Guide practicalnetworking.net/stand-alone/subnetting-mastery
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 2
  • 1Open terminal — run `ipconfig` (Windows) or `ip a` (Linux) — document your IP and subnet
  • 2Identify whether each IP is public or private using the 3 private ranges
  • 3Practice 10 subnetting questions at subnettingpractice.com — use binary, not shortcuts
  • 4Write a cheat sheet: 5 private ranges, loopback, APIPA — with descriptions
Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes
stats By The Numbers Why This Matters
Why IP Addressing Matters
4.3B
Total IPv4 addresses — nearly exhausted
3
Private IP ranges every hacker must know
340T+
Undecillion IPv6 addresses available
Day 2
Of your 90-day journey
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 2
"Learn subnetting with binary — not just formulas. When you understand the binary math, subnetting becomes instant and permanent."
Every Network+ exam has subnetting questions. Every pentest scope document uses CIDR notation. Every internal network runs on private ranges. This is foundational knowledge with zero expiry.
⚠ Common Mistake
Memorising IP ranges without understanding binary math behind them. Formulas break under pressure. Binary understanding never does — and it transfers directly to real-world network analysis.
DAY 3
DNS, DHCP & Core Protocols
DNS is the phonebook of the internet — and hackers exploit it daily.
#NetworkPlus#DNS#Day3of90
dark DNS Explained Foundation
DNS: The Internet's Phonebook
DNSDHCPProtocols

DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable names like google.com into IP addresses machines understand — for example: 142.250.80.46.

4 types of DNS servers: Recursive Resolver (your ISP) → Root Server → TLD Server (.com/.org) → Authoritative Server (the actual answer).

Without DNS, the internet breaks. Every website visit, every email sent, every API call starts with a DNS lookup. Compromising DNS = redirecting all traffic.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 DNS Section
red DNS Attack Types Attack Surface
DNS Attacks: 3 You Must Know
DNS Spoofing: Attacker injects fake DNS records — your query for bank.com returns the attacker's IP. You visit a fake site without knowing.
DNS Cache Poisoning: Corrupt the resolver's cache so ALL users on that server get redirected — not just you. Affects thousands simultaneously.
DNS Hijacking: Attacker changes your router's DNS server setting — all your DNS queries now go through the attacker's controlled server.

Defence: DNSSEC digitally signs DNS records. HTTPS ensures you're on the real site even if DNS is poisoned.

25 min · TryHackMe 'DNS in Detail' Room
dark DHCP Explained Protocol
DHCP: Automatic IP Assignment

DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS server to every device that connects to a network.

DORA Process: Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge. Your device broadcasts, the server responds, you request it, server confirms.

Attack angle — Rogue DHCP: An attacker sets up a fake DHCP server on the network. All new devices get assigned the attacker's machine as their default gateway — full traffic interception.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 DHCP Section
white Critical Ports Reference Must Memorise
Critical Ports Every Hacker Knows
21 — FTP (File Transfer — plaintext, insecure)
22 — SSH (Secure Shell — encrypted remote access)
25 — SMTP (Email sending — often abused for spam)
53 — DNS (Domain resolution — UDP + TCP)
80 — HTTP (Web — plaintext, interceptable)
443 — HTTPS (Web — TLS encrypted)
3389— RDP (Remote Desktop — common attack target)
30 min · Flashcard drill — all 20 top ports
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 3 Checklist
  • 1Explain how DNS works end-to-end — the full 4-server lookup chain
  • 2Understand DNS spoofing vs cache poisoning — the difference and impact
  • 3Know the DORA process for DHCP — and how a rogue DHCP attack works
  • 4Memorise top 20 protocol ports — they appear in every CTF and pentest
  • 5Run `nslookup google.com` and `nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8` — compare results
Total Day 3 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'DNS in Detail' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/dnsindetail
📺 Video Prof. Messer N10-009 — DNS & DHCP Section professormesser.com/network-plus/n10-009
🛠️ Tool Cisco Packet Tracer — Free Network Simulator netacad.com
📖 Reference ExamCompass — Free Protocol Port Quiz examcompass.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 3
  • 1Run `nslookup google.com` — see which DNS server resolved the query
  • 2Try `nslookup facebook.com 8.8.8.8` — use Google's public DNS instead
  • 3Run `ipconfig /all` (Windows) or `cat /etc/resolv.conf` (Linux) — find your DHCP server
  • 4Complete TryHackMe 'DNS in Detail' room — apply everything learned today
Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 3
"Memorise the top 20 port numbers cold. They appear in 80% of exam questions and every single professional port scan output you will ever read."
Build a physical cheat sheet — write each port and protocol by hand, not just typed. Handwriting encodes memory differently. Review it every morning for one week. You will never forget them.
⚠ Common Mistake
Confusing IMAP (143) with POP3 (110) — both receive email but differently. IMAP keeps email on server and syncs. POP3 downloads and deletes. This distinction appears in both exams and phishing attack analysis.
DAY 4
Network Topologies & Hardware
The shape of your network determines how easy it is to hack.
#NetworkPlus#NetworkHardware#Day4of90
dark Network Topologies Foundation
Network Topology: Shape = Attack Surface
TopologyHardwareSecurity

A network topology defines how devices are physically and logically connected. Different shapes create different security vulnerabilities and failure points.

Star — all devices connect to a central switch. Most common. Single point of failure = the switch.
Bus — all devices on one cable. Legacy. One break = entire network down.
Ring — data travels in a loop. Token Ring (legacy). Predictable but fragile.
Mesh — every device connects to every other. Redundant, resilient. Used in critical infrastructure.
Hybrid — combination of topologies. Used in all real enterprise environments.
20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Topology Section
red Routers vs Switches Critical Concept
Routers vs Switches: Critical Difference

Switches (Layer 2): Connect devices within the same network. Forward frames based on MAC addresses. A compromised switch enables ARP spoofing and VLAN hopping attacks.

Routers (Layer 3): Connect different networks together. Forward packets based on IP addresses. A misconfigured router leaks routing tables and enables man-in-the-middle attacks.

Hubs (Legacy — Layer 1): Broadcast every packet to every port. No intelligence. Any device connected can capture all traffic with Wireshark — attackers love finding them.

25 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Hardware Section
dark Firewalls & VLANs Security Controls
Firewalls & VLANs: Defence in Depth

Firewall Types: Packet-filtering (Layer 3/4 rules only), Stateful Inspection (tracks connection state), NGFW/Next-Gen (deep packet inspection, application-aware).

VLANs (Virtual LANs): Logically segment a network without physical separation. Finance, HR, and IT can share the same switches but never see each other's traffic.

VLAN Hopping Attack: If a switch port is misconfigured as a trunk, an attacker can tag frames to jump between VLANs — bypassing all logical segmentation.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Firewall Section
white Wireless Basics Wi-Fi Security
Wireless Standards and Attacks
WEP — Broken. Never use. Cracked in minutes with aircrack-ng.
WPA — Weak. TKIP encryption. Vulnerable to dictionary attacks.
WPA2 — Standard. AES encryption. Vulnerable to PMKID/handshake capture.
WPA3 — Current best. SAE (dragonfly handshake). Resists offline cracking.
802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) — Fastest current standard. WPA3 mandatory.
20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Wireless Section
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 4 Checklist
  • 1Draw and label all 5 network topologies — identify failure points of each
  • 2Explain the difference between router, switch, and hub at OSI layer level
  • 3Understand VLAN segmentation and how VLAN hopping attacks bypass it
  • 4Know WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3 — why WEP is broken and WPA3 is secure
  • 5Build a simple star topology in Cisco Packet Tracer — assign IPs, ping between PCs
Total Day 4 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer N10-009 — Topology & Hardware professormesser.com/network-plus/n10-009
🛠️ Lab Cisco Packet Tracer — Free Network Simulation netacad.com
🛠️ Lab GNS3 — Advanced Free Network Emulator gns3.com
🧪 Practice TryHackMe — 'Pre-Security' Path (Network section) tryhackme.com/path/outline/presecurity
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 4
  • 1Download Cisco Packet Tracer (free at netacad.com) — install it today
  • 2Build a star topology: 1 router, 1 switch, 3 PCs — assign IPs and ping between all PCs
  • 3Draw each topology type from memory — label failure points in red
  • 4Research your home router — is it also a switch? What SSID security does it use?
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 4
"Real enterprise networks are always hybrid topologies. Learn each type individually — only then can you understand and attack a combined real-world network."
In a pentest, topology knowledge directly impacts your lateral movement strategy. Star networks have one critical choke point — the core switch. Compromise it and you own the segment. Know the shape, own the network.
⚠ Common Mistake
Confusing router and switch roles. Routers separate networks. Switches connect devices within a network. Getting this wrong in an exam or client report is an immediate credibility killer.
DAY 5
Ports, Protocols & Firewall Rules
65,535 ports. Hackers know them all. Do you?
#NetworkPlus#Ports#Day5of90
dark What Are Ports? Foundation
Ports: Virtual Doors Into Every System
PortsTCP/UDPFirewall

Ports are virtual endpoints that allow a single IP address to run multiple services simultaneously. Your computer can serve web pages on port 80, SSH on port 22, and email on port 25 — all at once.

Three port ranges: 0–1023 (Well-known, reserved for standard services) · 1024–49151 (Registered, assigned to applications) · 49152–65535 (Dynamic/ephemeral, temporary connections).

For hackers: Open ports = attack surface. Every open port is a potential entry point. Nmap reveals them all in seconds.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Ports Section
red Top Ports to Memorise Must Know
Ports Every Hacker Memorises
21 — FTP · 22 — SSH · 23 — Telnet
25 — SMTP · 53 — DNS · 80 — HTTP
110 — POP3 · 143 — IMAP · 443 — HTTPS
445 — SMB · 3306— MySQL · 3389— RDP
8080 — Alt HTTP · 8443— Alt HTTPS· 27017— MongoDB
30 min · Flashcard drill — all ports + protocols
dark TCP vs UDP Protocol Comparison
TCP vs UDP: Know the Difference

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol): Connection-based. Uses 3-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK). Guaranteed delivery. Used by: HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, FTP, SMTP.

UDP (User Datagram Protocol): Connectionless. Fire-and-forget. No delivery confirmation. Used by: DNS, VoIP, DHCP, TFTP, video streaming.

Attack implications: TCP's handshake enables SYN flood DoS. UDP's lack of authentication enables amplification DDoS attacks using DNS or NTP servers as reflectors.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Transport Layer
white Firewall ACLs Security Control
Firewall ACLs and Port Filtering

ACLs (Access Control Lists) define which traffic is allowed or denied based on source IP, destination IP, port, and protocol.

ALLOW TCP any → 10.0.0.1:443 (HTTPS in)
ALLOW TCP any → 10.0.0.1:80 (HTTP in)
DENY TCP any → 10.0.0.1:3389 (Block RDP)
DENY ALL any → any (Implicit deny)

Implicit deny at the end is critical — anything not explicitly allowed is blocked. A missing rule is NOT a permission grant.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 Firewall Section
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 5 Checklist
  • 1Memorise top 15 port numbers cold — without referencing a cheat sheet
  • 2Explain TCP 3-way handshake and exactly why SYN floods exploit it
  • 3Understand firewall ACL implicit deny — what happens to unlisted traffic
  • 4Run `nmap -sV localhost` — identify every open port on your own machine
  • 5Build a port number cheat sheet — review it daily for one week
Total Day 5 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer N10-009 — Ports & Protocols professormesser.com/network-plus/n10-009
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Nmap' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/furthernmap
📖 Quiz ExamCompass — Free Port Number Practice Quiz examcompass.com
🛠️ Tool Nmap — Official Download and Documentation nmap.org
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 5
  • 1Install Nmap (nmap.org) — run `nmap -sV localhost` and document every open port
  • 2Identify what service is running on each open port — is any unexpected port open?
  • 3Take ExamCompass port quiz — score yourself and note every wrong answer
  • 4Write a firewall ACL from scratch — allow HTTPS, block Telnet, implicit deny all
Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 5
"Build a cheat sheet of the top 30 ports with protocol and service name. Review it every morning for one week. After that you will never need to look them up again."
In a real pentest, the first Nmap scan result you read will have 20+ open ports. You must recognise each one instantly — what service it is, what version matters, and what attacks target it. Speed of recognition is the skill.
⚠ Common Mistake
Scanning any network you do not own or have written permission to scan. Practice only on TryHackMe, HackTheBox, your own VM lab, or localhost. Unauthorised scanning is illegal in most countries — even from your own home.
DAY 6
VPNs, NAT & Network Security
VPNs hide your traffic — but do you actually know HOW?
#NetworkPlus#VPN#Day6of90
dark VPN Types Foundation
VPN: Encrypted Tunnels Explained
VPNNATZero Trust

A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates an encrypted tunnel between your device and a remote server — making your traffic appear to originate from the VPN server, not your real IP.

Remote Access VPN: Individual user → corporate network. Work from home securely.
Site-to-Site VPN: Office A ↔ Office B. Both LANs appear as one seamless network.
SSL VPN: Browser-based. No client software needed. Common for contractor access.

VPN Protocols: IPSec (fastest, most secure), OpenVPN (open source, reliable), WireGuard (modern, ultra-fast), L2TP/IPSec (legacy, widely supported).

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 VPN Section
red NAT Explained Critical Concept
NAT: How Your Router Shares One IP

NAT (Network Address Translation) maps your private IP addresses to a single public IP address. Your router does this for every device on your home network.

Static NAT: One private IP → one public IP. 1:1 mapping. Used for servers needing a fixed public address.
Dynamic NAT: Pool of public IPs shared across private addresses. Assigned on demand.
PAT (Port Address Translation): Many private IPs → one public IP using unique port numbers. What your home router actually does.

Security benefit of NAT: Internal hosts are not directly reachable from the internet — providing implicit protection (though not a replacement for a firewall).

25 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 NAT Section
dark IDS vs IPS Security Tools
IDS vs IPS: Detect vs Prevent

IDS (Intrusion Detection System): Monitors network traffic, detects suspicious patterns, and alerts security teams. It does NOT block traffic. Think of it as a burglar alarm.

IPS (Intrusion Prevention System): Everything an IDS does, plus it actively blocks and drops malicious traffic in real-time. Think of it as a security guard with a lock.

Placement matters: IDS is typically placed after the firewall (out-of-band monitoring). IPS must be inline — between the internet and your network — to block traffic before it arrives.

20 min · Prof. Messer N10-009 IDS/IPS Section
white DMZ & Zero Trust Architecture
DMZ & Zero Trust Architecture

DMZ (Demilitarized Zone): A network segment that sits between the public internet and the internal LAN. Web servers, email servers, and DNS servers live here — publicly accessible but isolated from internal systems.

Architecture: Internet → Firewall → DMZ (Web/Email/DNS Server) → Internal Firewall → Internal LAN. Two firewalls = two barriers an attacker must breach.

Zero Trust Model: 'Never trust, always verify.' Every user, every device, every request must authenticate — even inside the network. No implicit trust based on network location.

20 min · NIST Zero Trust Architecture Guide
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 6 Checklist
  • 1Explain the difference between Remote Access and Site-to-Site VPN
  • 2Understand PAT vs Static NAT — how your home router uses PAT daily
  • 3Know the difference between IDS and IPS — placement and function
  • 4Draw a DMZ network diagram — Internet → Firewall → DMZ → Internal Firewall → LAN
  • 5Explain Zero Trust in 2 sentences — this appears in every senior security interview
Total Day 6 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer N10-009 — VPN, NAT & Security professormesser.com/network-plus/n10-009
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Pre-Security' Path (free) tryhackme.com/path/outline/presecurity

📺 Video NetworkChuck — VPN Explained (YouTube) youtube.com/c/NetworkChuck
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 6
  • 1Draw a full network diagram: Internet → Firewall → DMZ (Web Server) → Internal Firewall → LAN
  • 2Label each zone: public, DMZ, internal — and which services belong in each
  • 3Research your home router's NAT settings — what type of NAT is it using?
  • 4Explain Zero Trust to someone (or write it down) in your own words — no notes
Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 6
"In every job interview for any security role, expect to explain DMZ and IDS vs IPS. These two concepts are asked constantly — get them cold."
Zero Trust is not just a buzzword — it is the current direction of enterprise security architecture. Every major cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) builds Zero Trust products. Understanding the model now puts you ahead of 90% of candidates.
⚠ Common Mistake
Thinking VPN = full anonymity. A VPN hides your traffic from your ISP and shifts trust to the VPN provider. It does NOT make you anonymous, does not protect against malware, and does not hide you from determined nation-state adversaries.
DAY 7
Network+ Week Recap & Exam Tips
7 Days of Networking done. Here's everything you need to PASS Network+.
#NetworkPlus#ExamPrep#Day7of90
dark Week 1 Recap Summary
Week 1: Everything You Learned
RecapExam TipsN10-009
Day 1: OSI 7-Layer Model — physical to application, attack surface per layer
Day 2: TCP/IP 4-Layer Model — IPv4/IPv6, subnetting, CIDR notation
Day 3: DNS, DHCP, Core Protocols — top 20 ports, DORA process, DNS attacks
Day 4: Topologies & Hardware — routers, switches, firewalls, VLANs, wireless security
Day 5: Ports, Protocols, Firewall ACLs — TCP vs UDP, implicit deny, Nmap scanning
Day 6: VPN, NAT, IDS/IPS, DMZ, Zero Trust — modern security architecture
30 min · Review all 6 days of notes
red N10-009 Exam Format Exam Info
CompTIA Network+ N10-009 Exam
Questions: Up to 90 questions — multiple choice + performance-based simulations
Time limit: 90 minutes — approximately 1 minute per question
Passing score: 720 out of 900 — approximately 80%
Key domains: Networking Concepts (23%) · Network Implementation (20%) · Network Operations (17%) · Network Security (20%) · Network Troubleshooting (20%)

Performance-based questions (PBQs) simulate real-world tasks — configuring firewalls, subnetting, troubleshooting connectivity. Do NOT skip them — they are worth more points.

15 min · Read CompTIA official exam objectives
dark Top Exam Topics Must Study
Top Exam Topics You Must Master
Subnetting calculations — CIDR notation, usable hosts, network/broadcast addresses. Always on the exam.
Port numbers — top 20 ports cold. TCP vs UDP per protocol. Appears in 3–5 questions minimum.
Cable types — Cat5e vs Cat6 vs Cat6a. Max length: 100m (Ethernet). Fiber types: single-mode vs multi-mode.
Wireless standards — 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax speeds, frequencies, WPA2 vs WPA3.
Troubleshooting methodology — Top-down (L7→L1) vs Bottom-up (L1→L7). Know both approaches.
45 min · Review CompTIA N10-009 Exam Objectives PDF
white Study Resources Free Study Tools
Best Free Network+ Prep Resources
  • 1Professor Messer — Free N10-009 video course + study guide PDF at professormesser.com
  • 2ExamCompass — Free practice quizzes by domain topic at examcompass.com
  • 3Jason Dion — Udemy course (frequently on sale for $15) — best paid option
  • 4Mike Meyers — All-in-One Network+ textbook — comprehensive physical reference
  • 5TryHackMe Pre-Security Path — free hands-on lab practice for networking fundamentals
Take a full practice exam today — 90 questions, timed
yellow Exam Study Strategy Study Plan
Day 7 Checklist
  • 1Take a full 90-question practice test at ExamCompass — timed, no cheating
  • 2Write down every wrong answer topic — that becomes your targeted study list
  • 3Review your notes from Days 1–6 — fill any gaps identified by the practice test
  • 4Understand the troubleshooting methodology — top-down vs bottom-up OSI approach
  • 5Schedule your exam if you feel ready — commit to a date, it drives urgency
Total Day 7 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources

white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 7
  • 1Take a full ExamCompass N10-009 practice test — 90 questions, 90 minutes, no notes
  • 2Score yourself honestly — anything below 75% means a topic needs more attention
  • 3Review all 6 days of notes — create a single one-page master reference sheet
  • 4Post your progress online with #Day7of90 — accountability accelerates learning
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
stats Week 1 Achievement Progress Stats
Week 1 Complete — Your Progress
7
Days of consistent learning completed
6
Core networking topics mastered
20+
Port numbers in your memory
83
Days remaining on your journey
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 7
"Network+ is the foundation for EVERYTHING in cybersecurity. Do not rush it. Every advanced topic — pentesting, cloud security, threat hunting — builds on what you learned this week."
The study method that works: Watch video → Take notes by hand → Practice questions → Review wrong answers → Repeat. Do NOT just watch. Active recall through practice questions is what makes knowledge stick permanently.
⚠ Common Mistake
Skipping subnetting practice because it seems hard. Subnetting questions appear on every Network+ exam. Students who skip it fail — without exception. Do 20 subnetting problems a day until it feels automatic.
2
Phase 02
Security+ Concepts
Days 08–14 · 7 sessions
DAY 8
Intro to Security+ & CIA Triad
Security+ is the #1 entry-level security cert. Week 2 starts NOW.
#SecurityPlus#CIATriad#Day8of90
dark CIA Triad Foundation
CIA Triad: The Core of Security
ConfidentialityIntegrityAvailability

The CIA Triad is the foundational model of information security. Every security control, policy, and technology maps to one or more of its three pillars.

Confidentiality — only authorised users can access the data. Enforced by encryption, access controls, and classification.
Integrity — data has not been tampered with or altered. Enforced by hashing, digital signatures, and checksums.
Availability — systems and data are accessible when needed. Enforced by redundancy, backups, and failover systems.

Every security decision starts here. If you cannot map a control to C, I, or A — question whether it is actually a security control.

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Playlist
red CIA Attack Mapping Critical Concept
Every Attack Maps to CIA

Every cyberattack violates at least one CIA principle. Knowing the mapping is essential for Security+ scenario questions — and for thinking like a security professional.

Ransomware → Availability violation. Encrypts files — users cannot access data until ransom is paid.
Data breach → Confidentiality violation. Sensitive data exposed to unauthorised parties.
Man-in-the-Middle → Integrity violation. Attacker intercepts and modifies data in transit.
DDoS attack → Availability violation. Floods a server until it cannot respond to legitimate users.

Exam tip: Security+ scenario questions will describe an attack — you must identify which CIA principle is violated. Practice this mapping until it is automatic.

25 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 — Threats Section
dark Authentication vs Authorization AAA Framework
AAA Framework: Never Confuse These

Authentication (AuthN): Verifying identity — 'Who are you?' Proved by passwords, biometrics, tokens, or certificates.

Authorisation (AuthZ): Verifying permissions — 'What can you do?' Granted by access control lists, roles, and policies after authentication.

Accounting: Tracking what you did — 'What did you do?' Logs, audit trails, and SIEM events record all activity for forensics and compliance.

NEVER confuse AuthN and AuthZ on the exam. A user can be authenticated (we know who they are) but not authorised (they cannot access that resource).

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 AAA Section
white Non-Repudiation Key Concept
Non-Repudiation: You Cannot Deny It

Non-repudiation ensures that a party cannot deny having sent a message or performed an action. It is the digital equivalent of a signed contract.

How it works: Digital signatures use the sender's private key to sign a message. Anyone can verify the signature with the sender's public key — proving the sender cannot deny it.

Why it matters: Critical for legal evidence, financial transactions, software code signing, and forensic investigations. Without non-repudiation, attackers can claim 'I didn't do it' — and you cannot prove otherwise.

15 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Cryptography Section
dark SY0-701 Exam Format Exam Info
CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 Exam
Questions: Up to 90 questions — multiple choice and performance-based simulations (PBQs)
Time limit: 90 minutes — approximately 1 minute per question
Passing score: 750 out of 900 — approximately 83%
Domains: Threats/Attacks (22%) · Architecture (18%) · Implementation (28%) · Operations & IR (17%) · Governance (15%)

Security+ is scenario-based. Questions do not ask 'what is AES?' — they ask 'a company needs to encrypt data at rest — which algorithm is MOST appropriate?' Practice applying concepts.

15 min · Read CompTIA SY0-701 Official Exam Objectives
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 8 Checklist
  • 1Memorise the CIA Triad — define each pillar in one sentence without notes
  • 2Map 5 real-world attacks to C, I, or A — ransomware, DDoS, MITM, breach, spoofing
  • 3Explain AuthN vs AuthZ vs Accounting — the 3 pillars of AAA framework
  • 4Understand non-repudiation and how digital signatures provide it
  • 5Read the SY0-701 exam objectives PDF — highlight the top 3 domains by weight
Total Day 8 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Primary Prof. Messer — SY0-701 Full Free Playlist professormesser.com/security-plus/sy0-701
📺 Alternative Pete Zerger — SY0-701 Security+ Playlist youtube.com/@insidecloudandsecurity
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Pre-Security' Path (free) tryhackme.com/path/outline/presecurity
🎯 Objectives CompTIA SY0-701 Official Exam Objectives PDF comptia.org/certifications/security
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 8
  • 1Research 5 real-world breaches on Google — classify each as C, I, or A violation
  • 2Create a table: Attack Type | CIA Principle Violated | Why — this trains exam thinking
  • 3Watch Prof. Messer SY0-701 Playlist 1 — CIA Triad and AAA sections (45 min)
  • 4Download the SY0-701 exam objectives PDF — read domain weights and key topics
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 8
"Security+ questions are scenario-based — not definition-based. Practice applying the CIA Triad to real situations, not just reciting what each letter stands for."
The exam will describe a company scenario and ask 'which security principle is MOST at risk?' You must think in context, not just theory. Every practice question you do this week should be mapped to CIA automatically.
⚠ Common Mistake
Treating Security+ as pure memorisation. Students who only memorise definitions fail scenario questions. Students who practice applying concepts to real situations pass on the first attempt.
DAY 9
Threats, Attacks & Malware Types
Know your enemy. 8 malware types every security pro must memorise.
#SecurityPlus#Malware#Day9of90
dark Malware Overview Foundation
Malware: 8 Types You Must Know
VirusWormRansomware

Malware (Malicious Software) is any software intentionally designed to damage systems, gain unauthorised access, steal data, or disrupt operations.

The SY0-701 exam tests your ability to identify malware type from a scenario description — not just recall definitions. You must recognise behaviour, not just names.

The 8 malware types you must master: Virus, Worm, Trojan, RAT, Rootkit, Ransomware, Spyware/Keylogger, and Adware. Each has distinct behaviour, spread mechanism, and impact.

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Threats Section
red Virus vs Worm Critical Distinction
Virus vs Worm: Critical Difference

Virus: Requires a host file to attach to. Spreads only when a user takes action — opening an infected file, running a program, or inserting infected media. Cannot spread on its own.

Worm: Self-replicating. Spreads automatically across networks without user interaction. Exploits vulnerabilities in services or protocols to move machine-to-machine.

Real example — Worm: WannaCry (2017) — exploited EternalBlue (SMB vulnerability) and spread to 200,000 systems across 150 countries in 72 hours. Zero user interaction required.
Real example — Virus: ILOVEYOU / Love Letter (2000) — spread via email attachment. Activated only when the victim opened the attached file.
25 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Malware Section
dark Trojans, RATs & Rootkits Stealth Malware
Trojans, RATs & Rootkits
Trojan: Disguised as legitimate software. User willingly installs it. Once active, it performs malicious actions in the background — downloading additional malware, opening backdoors.
RAT (Remote Access Trojan): A Trojan that gives the attacker full remote control of the victim's machine — screen capture, file access, webcam access, keylogging, and command execution.
Rootkit: Hides itself and other malware at the OS or kernel level. Extremely difficult to detect because it manipulates the operating system to hide its own presence from antivirus tools.

Key exam distinction: A Trojan deceives. A RAT controls. A Rootkit hides. All three often work together in advanced attacks.

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Malware Section
white Ransomware Deep Dive Most Dangerous
Ransomware: The Most Devastating Threat

Ransomware encrypts all files on a victim's system and demands payment — typically in cryptocurrency — for the decryption key. Modern ransomware also exfiltrates data before encrypting (double extortion).

Common attack vectors: Phishing email attachments, malicious macros in Word documents, exposed RDP (port 3389), and unpatched vulnerabilities in internet-facing systems.

CIA mapping: Ransomware primarily violates Availability (you cannot access your files) and often Confidentiality (data is stolen in double extortion attacks).

Defence: Offline backups (3-2-1 rule), network segmentation, patching, MFA on RDP, email filtering, and endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools.

20 min · Read Verizon DBIR ransomware section (free)
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 9 Checklist
  • 1Define all 8 malware types from memory — no notes — one sentence each
  • 2Explain the exact difference between Virus and Worm — spread mechanism is the key
  • 3Identify the distinction between Trojan, RAT, and Rootkit — deceive vs control vs hide
  • 4Map each malware type to its CIA violation — ransomware = availability, keylogger = confidentiality
  • 5Create a detection/prevention table — 8 malware types, how to detect each, how to prevent each
Total Day 9 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer SY0-701 — Threats & Malware Section professormesser.com/security-plus/sy0-701
📊 Stats AV-TEST — Real Malware Statistics & Research av-test.org
🔬 Samples MalwareBazaar — Safe Malware Sample Repository bazaar.abuse.ch
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Intro to Malware Analysis' Room tryhackme.com/room/intromalwareanalysis
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 9
  • 1Create a 2-column table: Malware Type | How to Detect / How to Prevent — all 8 types
  • 2Research WannaCry — understand exactly how it spread and why it was so effective
  • 3Watch Prof. Messer SY0-701 Threats section (45 min) — pause on each malware type
  • 4Quiz yourself — cover the table and recite all 8 types with behaviour from memory
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 9
"Exam questions describe a scenario without naming the malware type. You must identify it from behaviour alone. Practice scenario-to-type mapping — not definition recall."
The exam will say: 'A user installs what appears to be a free video player. Afterwards, an attacker begins accessing the user's files remotely.' You must recognise this as a RAT delivered via Trojan — without being told either term.
⚠ Common Mistake
Confusing Trojan and RAT. A Trojan is the delivery mechanism — disguised software. A RAT is the payload — remote control capability. Many RATs are delivered via Trojans, but they are distinct concepts on the exam.
DAY 10
Social Engineering & Phishing Attacks
90% of cyberattacks start with a single email. Learn to spot them.
#SecurityPlus#Phishing#Day10of90
dark Social Engineering Foundation
Social Engineering: Hacking Humans
PhishingPretextingVishing

Social engineering is the art of manipulating people into revealing confidential information or performing actions that benefit an attacker — without any technical exploit.

It works because it bypasses all technical security controls by targeting the one vulnerability that cannot be patched: human psychology. Trust, authority, urgency, and fear are the attack vectors.

Kevin Mitnick, the world's most famous hacker, stated that the majority of his successful attacks relied on social engineering — not technical exploitation. The human is always the weakest link.

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Social Engineering Section
red Phishing Types Attack Types
6 Phishing Variants You Must Know
Phishing — mass email campaign impersonating trusted brands (PayPal, Microsoft, your bank) to steal credentials or deliver malware via malicious attachments.
Spear Phishing — targeted phishing using personal information about the victim. References their name, job title, or recent activity. Far more convincing than generic phishing.
Whaling — spear phishing that specifically targets executives (CEOs, CFOs). High-value targets, highly personalised, often involves wire transfer fraud.
Vishing — voice phishing via phone call. Attacker impersonates IT support, bank, or government agency to extract credentials or payment.
Smishing — phishing via SMS text message. 'Your package is delayed — click to reschedule delivery.' Link leads to credential harvester.
Pharming — DNS-based attack that redirects users from a legitimate domain to a fake site without them clicking any link. Harder to detect than standard phishing.
25 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Social Engineering
dark Pretexting & Baiting Techniques
Pretexting, Baiting & Physical Attacks
Pretexting: Attacker creates a fabricated, believable scenario to gain trust. 'I am from IT — we need your credentials to fix your account.' The pretext is the story that makes the request seem legitimate.
Baiting: Leaving infected USB drives in car parks, lobbies, or public areas. Human curiosity does the rest — someone plugs it in and the malware executes automatically.
Tailgating: Following an authorised person through a secured door without using credentials. Exploits politeness — most people hold doors open for others.
Piggybacking: Like tailgating, but the authorised person is aware and consents to letting the attacker through — often deceived into thinking they have legitimate access.
20 min · social-engineer.org resources
white Defence Strategies Defence
Defending Against Social Engineering
  • 1Security awareness training — the most effective defence. Regular phishing simulations reduce click rates from ~30% to ~5%.
  • 2Email filtering — block suspicious domains, scan attachments, flag external senders with banners.
  • 3DMARC / SPF / DKIM — email authentication protocols that prevent domain spoofing and sender impersonation.
  • 4Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) — even if credentials are stolen via phishing, MFA prevents account access without the second factor.
  • 5Verify before acting — call back on a known number to verify any unusual request. Never trust caller ID — it can be spoofed.
20 min · Read CISA phishing guidance (free)
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 10 Checklist
  • 1Name and define all 6 phishing types — phishing, spear, whaling, vishing, smishing, pharming
  • 2Explain pretexting, baiting, tailgating, and piggybacking with a real scenario for each
  • 3Understand how SPF, DKIM, and DMARC protect against email spoofing at a conceptual level
  • 4Know why MFA is the single best technical control against phishing credential theft
  • 5Analyse 5 real phishing email examples — identify the manipulation technique used in each
Total Day 10 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer SY0-701 — Social Engineering Section professormesser.com/security-plus/sy0-701
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Phishing' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/phishingyl
🔧 Tool GoPhish — Open Source Phishing Simulation Framework getgophish.com
🎯 Quiz Google Phishing Quiz — Test Your Detection Skills phishingquiz.withgoogle.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 10
  • 1Take Google's phishing quiz at phishingquiz.withgoogle.com — score yourself honestly
  • 2Analyse 5 phishing email examples from PhishTank.com — list every red flag you find
  • 3Research the 2020 Twitter Hack — identify every social engineering technique used
  • 4Analyse an email header using mxtoolbox.com — find the real sending server behind a spoofed address
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 10
"Security+ performance-based tasks often simulate phishing email analysis. Practice identifying red flags: sender domain mismatch, urgency language, mismatched hover URLs."
On the exam, you will be shown an email and asked to identify whether it is a phishing attempt and which type. Practise analysing real phishing examples from PhishTank — that direct experience transfers directly to exam performance.
⚠ Common Mistake
Never test social engineering or phishing simulations on real people or systems without explicit written authorisation. Even sending a test phishing email to a colleague without permission is a disciplinary — and potentially legal — offence.
DAY 11
Cryptography & PKI Basics
Without cryptography, the internet does not exist. Here is how it works.
#SecurityPlus#Cryptography#Day11of90
dark Symmetric vs Asymmetric Foundation
Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption
AESRSAPKI

Symmetric encryption uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt data. It is fast and efficient — ideal for encrypting large amounts of data. The challenge: how do you securely share the key?

Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair — a public key (shared with anyone) to encrypt, and a private key (kept secret) to decrypt. Slower than symmetric, but solves the key exchange problem.

Symmetric algorithms: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard — current gold standard), DES (broken), 3DES (deprecated), RC4 (broken).
Asymmetric algorithms: RSA (most common), ECC (Elliptic Curve — smaller keys, stronger), Diffie-Hellman (key exchange), DSA (digital signatures).
25 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Cryptography Section
red Hashing Critical Concept
Hashing: Integrity, Not Secrecy

Hashing is a one-way mathematical function that converts any input into a fixed-length output (the hash or digest). It cannot be reversed — you cannot get the original data from a hash.

Purpose: Verify data integrity, not encrypt data. If two files produce the same hash, they are identical. If the hash changes — the file was tampered with.

MD5 — 128-bit. Broken. Cracked in seconds with a GPU. Still found in legacy systems — always flag as a vulnerability.
SHA-1 — 160-bit. Deprecated. Weak. Browsers no longer accept SHA-1 certificates.
SHA-256 — 256-bit. Current standard. Used in TLS, digital signatures, blockchain.
bcrypt — Designed specifically for password hashing. Intentionally slow. Resistant to GPU-based cracking.
20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Hashing Section
dark PKI Explained Infrastructure
PKI: The Trust System of the Internet

PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is the system of Certificate Authorities (CAs), digital certificates, and policies that enables trusted encryption across the internet.

How it works: A CA (like DigiCert or Let's Encrypt) issues a digital certificate that binds a public key to an identity (a domain, a person, or a company). Browsers trust CAs by default.

Digital certificate = public key + identity + CA signature. When you see the padlock on HTTPS, your browser has verified the site's certificate was signed by a trusted CA.

Certificate chain of trust: Root CA → Intermediate CA → End-entity certificate. If any certificate in the chain is revoked or expired — the connection fails.

25 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 PKI Section
white Digital Signatures & Key Exchange Applications
Digital Signatures & Key Exchange

Digital Signatures: Sender hashes the message, then encrypts the hash with their private key — creating a signature. Recipient decrypts with sender's public key and compares hashes. Provides authenticity + non-repudiation.

Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange: Allows two parties to generate a shared secret key over an unsecured channel — without ever transmitting the key itself. Powers the TLS handshake for every HTTPS connection.

TLS Handshake (simplified): 1. Client sends supported cipher suites 2. Server sends certificate (public key) 3. Key exchange (Diffie-Hellman) 4. Shared session key generated 5. Encrypted communication begins
20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 TLS Section
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 11 Checklist
  • 1Explain symmetric vs asymmetric — key difference, use case, and one algorithm each
  • 2Know which algorithms are broken — MD5, DES, SHA-1, RC4 — and why to avoid them
  • 3Understand hashing vs encryption — this distinction appears directly on the exam
  • 4Explain PKI and certificate chain of trust — Root CA → Intermediate → End-entity
  • 5Hash 'password' with MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 on CyberChef — observe the difference
Total Day 11 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer SY0-701 — Cryptography Section professormesser.com/security-plus/sy0-701
🔧 Tool CyberChef — Free Online Cryptography Toolkit gchq.github.io/CyberChef
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Cryptography for Beginners' Room tryhackme.com/room/cryptographyintro
📖 Reference NIST — Approved Algorithm List (FIPS Standards) csrc.nist.gov/projects/cryptographic-standards-and-guidelines
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 11
  • 1Go to CyberChef.io — hash 'password' with MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 — compare all three outputs
  • 2Then hash 'password1' with each — notice how a tiny change creates a completely different hash
  • 3Complete TryHackMe Cryptography for Beginners room — hands-on practice (60 min)
  • 4Write from memory: 3 symmetric algorithms, 3 asymmetric algorithms, 4 hash algorithms — with status (safe/broken)
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 11
"Know which cryptographic algorithms are broken. The exam will test whether you can identify weak cryptography in a scenario — MD5, DES, SHA-1, and RC4 are always wrong answers."
The single most tested distinction in the cryptography domain: hashing is NOT encryption. Hashing is one-way — it verifies integrity. Encryption is two-way — it protects confidentiality. Getting this wrong on the exam costs points you cannot afford.
⚠ Common Mistake
Confusing hashing with encryption. Hashing cannot be reversed — it produces a digest for integrity verification. Encryption can be reversed with the correct key — it protects data confidentiality. These are fundamentally different operations with different purposes.
DAY 12
Identity & Access Management (IAM)
The #1 rule of security: give the LEAST access possible. Here is why.
#SecurityPlus#IAM#Day12of90
dark Least Privilege Foundation
Least Privilege: The #1 IAM Rule
Least PrivilegeMFARBAC

Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP): Users, applications, and systems should be granted only the minimum level of access required to perform their job function — nothing more.

Why it matters: If an attacker compromises a low-privilege account, the blast radius is limited. If they compromise an admin account with no privilege restrictions — they own everything.

Related concept — Need to Know: Users should only access information directly relevant to their role, even if they technically have permission to view more. Least privilege is about access. Need to know is about information.

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 IAM Section
red MFA Explained Critical Control
MFA: The Single Best Security Control

MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) requires two or more different types of authentication factors before granting access. Compromising one factor is not enough.

Something you know — password, PIN, security question. The weakest factor — can be stolen, guessed, or phished.
Something you have — smartphone authenticator app, hardware token, smart card. Requires physical possession.
Something you are — fingerprint, facial recognition, retina scan, voice recognition. Biometric — hardest to replicate.

Exam distinction: Two factors from the SAME category = NOT MFA. A PIN + a password are both 'something you know' — that is single-factor with two instances, not true MFA.

25 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Authentication Section
dark Access Control Models Models
4 Access Control Models
DAC (Discretionary Access Control): The owner of a resource controls who can access it. Most flexible, least secure. Common in personal file systems — you control sharing on your own files.
MAC (Mandatory Access Control): Labels and clearance levels control access. Government/military classification systems. Users cannot override — the system enforces access based on labels (Top Secret, Secret, Unclassified).
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control): Access granted based on job role. Most common in enterprise environments. HR gets HR data. Finance gets finance data. Easy to manage at scale.
ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control): Most flexible. Access based on multiple attributes — user role + time of day + location + device type. Used in Zero Trust architectures.
20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Access Control Section
white SSO & Privileged Accounts Advanced IAM
SSO & Privileged Account Management

SSO (Single Sign-On): One set of credentials grants access to multiple applications. Protocols: SAML (enterprise federated identity), OAuth 2.0 (third-party app authorisation), OpenID Connect (authentication layer on OAuth).

SSO trade-off: Massive convenience improvement — one login for everything. One massive risk — if that one account is compromised, every connected application is breached simultaneously.

PAM (Privileged Access Management): Controls and monitors admin accounts. Admin credentials stored in a secure vault, checked out when needed, all activity recorded. Never use admin accounts for daily tasks.

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Identity Management
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 12 Checklist
  • 1Explain Least Privilege — define it and give a real enterprise example of applying it
  • 2Know all 3 MFA factor categories — and why two passwords do NOT equal MFA
  • 3Compare all 4 access control models — DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC — use case for each
  • 4Understand SSO protocols — SAML vs OAuth 2.0 vs OpenID Connect at a conceptual level
  • 5Run `net user` (Windows) or `cat /etc/passwd` (Linux) — identify all accounts on your machine
Total Day 12 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer SY0-701 — Identity & Access Management professormesser.com/security-plus/sy0-701
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'IAM' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/iam
☁️ Tool Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) — Free Tier Available microsoft.com/en-us/security/business/identity-access/microsoft-entra-id
📖 Reference NIST SP 800-63 — Digital Identity Guidelines pages.nist.gov/800-63-3
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 12
  • 1Run `net user` (Windows) or `cat /etc/passwd` (Linux) — list all accounts and identify admin accounts
  • 2Audit your own online accounts — which ones use MFA? Enable it on any that do not
  • 3Create a comparison table: DAC vs MAC vs RBAC vs ABAC — real use case for each
  • 4Complete TryHackMe IAM room — apply access control concepts in a lab environment
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 12
"RBAC is the most tested IAM concept on Security+. Know how roles are assigned, inherited, and revoked — and why role explosion is a real enterprise problem."
Role explosion occurs when an organisation creates too many fine-grained roles over time — making the access control system unmanageable. The exam tests whether you recognise this and know that role reviews and ABAC can solve it.
⚠ Common Mistake
Confusing authentication token types. A hardware token (physical device like a YubiKey) and a software token (authenticator app) are both 'something you have' — but they have very different security profiles. Hardware tokens cannot be phished. Software tokens can be via SIM swapping or malware.
DAY 13
Risk Management & Incident Response
Every company will be breached. The question is: do they have a plan?
#SecurityPlus#IncidentResponse#Day13of90
dark Risk Management Foundation
Risk Management: Calculated Decisions
RiskNIST IRSIEM

Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Asset Value. A threat with no vulnerability to exploit = no risk. A vulnerability with no threat actor = no risk. Both must exist for risk to be present.

4 Risk Response Strategies:

Accept — risk is too low or mitigation too expensive. Document it and move on.
Transfer — shift financial impact to another party. Cyber insurance transfers risk.
Avoid — eliminate the activity that creates the risk entirely.
Mitigate — implement controls to reduce probability or impact of the risk.
20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Risk Management Section
red Business Continuity Critical Concepts
BCP · DRP · RTO · RPO
BCP (Business Continuity Plan): The overall strategy for keeping business operations running during and after a disaster or major incident.
DRP (Disaster Recovery Plan): The specific technical plan for restoring IT systems, data, and infrastructure after a disaster. A subset of BCP.
RTO (Recovery Time Objective): Maximum acceptable time to restore a system after an outage. 'We must restore email within 4 hours.' The speed target.
RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time. 'We can afford to lose no more than 1 hour of data.' Drives backup frequency decisions.

Exam tip: RTO is about time to restore. RPO is about data loss tolerance. Know both cold — they appear in scenario questions regularly.

25 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Business Continuity
dark NIST IR Framework Incident Response
NIST IR Framework: 6 Phases
1. Preparation — Build the IR team, create runbooks, deploy detection tools, conduct tabletop exercises before an incident occurs.
2. Detection & Analysis — Identify and confirm an incident has occurred. Analyse indicators, determine scope and severity.
3. Containment — Stop the spread. Isolate affected systems. Short-term containment (disconnect from network) then long-term containment (patch, rebuild).
4. Eradication — Remove the threat completely. Delete malware, close backdoors, revoke compromised credentials.
5. Recovery — Restore systems to normal operation. Verify functionality. Monitor for reinfection.
6. Post-Incident Activity — Lessons learned meeting. Update IR plan. Document timeline and evidence for legal or compliance purposes.
30 min · NIST SP 800-61 (free PDF)
white SIEM & Vulnerability Management Tools
SIEM, VA & Pentest — Key Differences

SIEM (Security Information & Event Management): Aggregates and correlates logs from firewalls, servers, endpoints, and applications. Detects patterns that indicate incidents. Tools: Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel.

Vulnerability Assessment (VA): Scans systems to identify known vulnerabilities. Non-disruptive. Does not exploit — only identifies. Produces a list of findings with CVSS scores.

Penetration Test: Goes further than VA by actively attempting to exploit vulnerabilities — with written permission. Proves that a vulnerability is actually exploitable, not just theoretically present.

Key exam distinction: VA finds weaknesses. A pentest proves they are exploitable. Different purpose, different authorisation requirement, different deliverable.

20 min · Prof. Messer SY0-701 Security Operations
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 13 Checklist
  • 1Explain all 4 risk responses — Accept, Transfer, Avoid, Mitigate — with a real scenario for each
  • 2Define BCP vs DRP — and explain the difference between RTO and RPO with numbers
  • 3Memorise the 6 NIST IR phases in order — write them from memory 3 times
  • 4Explain the difference between VA and Penetration Testing — purpose and authorisation
  • 5Understand what a SIEM does — log aggregation, correlation, alert generation
Total Day 13 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Prof. Messer SY0-701 — Risk & Incident Response professormesser.com/security-plus/sy0-701
📖 Guide NIST SP 800-61 — Incident Handling Guide (free PDF) nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-61r2.pdf
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'SIEM' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/introtosiem
📖 Reference NIST SP 800-30 — Risk Assessment Guide (free) nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/Legacy/SP/nistspecialpublication800-30r1.pdf
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 13
  • 1Download NIST SP 800-61 — skim Section 3 (Handling Process) — 30 minutes
  • 2Write the 6 IR phases from memory 3 times — until you can recite them without hesitation
  • 3Create a risk register for your personal digital life — 5 risks, response strategy for each
  • 4Complete TryHackMe SIEM room — understand how log correlation detects incidents
Hands-On Task: 60 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 13
"Incident Response order is heavily tested on Security+. The sequence is always: Containment before Eradication before Recovery. Never skip steps — this is the exam answer every time."
Memorise the NIST IR phases as: P-D-C-E-R-P — Preparation, Detection, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Post-Incident. Exam scenario questions will describe an IR situation and ask what the NEXT step should be. Knowing the order cold means instant correct answers.
⚠ Common Mistake
Skipping the Post-Incident Activity phase. Students think IR ends at Recovery — it does not. The Lessons Learned meeting is where the IR plan gets updated, where legal documentation is finalised, and where the organisation improves. The exam tests this phase specifically.
DAY 14
Security+ Week Recap & Exam Strategy
2 Weeks Down. Security+ week complete. Here is your full exam strategy.
#SecurityPlus#ExamPrep#Day14of90
dark Week 2 Recap Summary
Week 2: Everything You Learned
RecapSY0-701Exam Strategy
Day 8: CIA Triad — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. AAA Framework. Non-Repudiation.
Day 9: Malware Types — Virus, Worm, Trojan, RAT, Rootkit, Ransomware, Spyware, Keylogger.
Day 10: Social Engineering — 6 phishing types, pretexting, baiting, tailgating, defence strategies.
Day 11: Cryptography — Symmetric vs Asymmetric, Hashing, PKI, Digital Signatures, TLS.
Day 12: IAM — Least Privilege, MFA, Access Control Models (DAC/MAC/RBAC/ABAC), SSO.
Day 13: Risk Management — 4 responses, BCP/DRP/RTO/RPO, NIST IR 6 phases, SIEM, VA vs Pentest.
30 min · Review all 6 days of notes
red SY0-701 Domain Weights Exam Priority
SY0-701 Domain Weights: Study Smart
Implementation (28%) — highest weight. Cryptography, identity, access management, network security controls.
Threats, Attacks & Vulnerabilities (22%) — malware, social engineering, application attacks, threat intelligence.
Architecture & Design (18%) — security models, cloud, virtualisation, Zero Trust, network segmentation.
Operations & Incident Response (17%) — NIST IR phases, digital forensics, SIEM, vulnerability scanning.
Governance, Risk & Compliance (15%) — risk management, frameworks (NIST, ISO), policies, regulations.

Strategy: Focus the most time on Implementation and Threats — they represent 50% of the total exam score.

15 min · Read CompTIA SY0-701 Exam Objectives
dark Performance-Based Questions PBQ Strategy
Performance-Based Questions: PBQ Strategy

PBQs (Performance-Based Questions) simulate real-world security tasks — configuring firewalls, identifying phishing emails, ordering IR steps, dragging and dropping ACL rules. They appear at the START of the exam.

Strategy: Skip PBQs on first pass. They are time-consuming and may take 10–15 minutes each. Answer all multiple-choice questions first, then return to PBQs with remaining time.

How to practice PBQs: Professor Messer sells PBQ practice kits. Mike Chapple on LinkedIn Learning includes PBQ simulations. TryHackMe rooms simulate the hands-on component in a live lab environment.

20 min · Professor Messer PBQ practice kit
white Exam Strategy Test-Taking
Security+ Exam Day Strategy
  • 1Skip PBQs on first pass — flag them, answer all multiple choice first, return with remaining time
  • 2Eliminate wrong answers first — Security+ always has 2 obviously wrong options, leaving 2 plausible ones
  • 3Read the scenario carefully — the correct answer depends on context, not just the definition
  • 4Never leave blanks — there is no penalty for wrong answers. Guess intelligently on anything uncertain
  • 5Time management — 90 questions in 90 minutes = 1 minute per question. Track your pace every 20 questions
Take a full 90-question timed mock exam today
yellow Top Free Study Resources Study Resources
Day 14 Checklist
  • 1Take a full 90-question Security+ mock exam under timed conditions — no notes, no pausing
  • 2Score yourself honestly — write down every domain where you scored below 75%
  • 3Review those weak domains from your Days 8–13 notes — targeted review, not re-reading everything
  • 4Understand the exam strategy — PBQ skip technique, elimination method, time pacing
  • 5You do not need to pass Security+ before Day 15 — the learning matters more than the cert right now
Total Day 14 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes

  • 1Take a full SY0-701 mock exam at ExamCompass — 90 questions, 90 minutes, no notes
  • 2Score every domain separately — identify your weakest two domains specifically
  • 3Review your Days 8–13 cheat sheets — create one master reference page covering all 6 days

stats Week 2 Achievement Progress Stats
Week 2 Complete — Your Progress
14
Days of consistent learning completed

Core Security+ domains covered this week

Days remaining on your 90-day journey
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 14
"You do not need to pass Security+ before Day 15. Use these two weeks as a study foundation, then certify when ready. The learning matters most — the cert follows the knowledge."
The study method that produces first-attempt passes: Watch → Notes → Practice questions → Review wrong answers → Repeat. Students who only watch videos fail. Students who practice questions under timed conditions consistently pass on the first attempt.
⚠ Common Mistake
Skipping mock exams because you feel unprepared. Mock exams are how you find out what you do not know — not a test of what you do know. A bad mock exam score today is infinitely more valuable than a bad real exam score on exam day.
3
Phase 03
Linux Fundamentals
Days 15–28 · 14 sessions
DAY 15
Linux Intro: Why Hackers Love Linux
Every hacker, every server, every SOC runs Linux. Week 3 starts now.
#Linux#KaliLinux#Day15of90
dark What is Linux? Foundation
Linux: The Operating System of Security
Kali LinuxOpen SourceCLI

Linux is an open-source, Unix-like operating system built on the Linux kernel combined with GNU tools. It powers 90%+ of internet servers, cloud infrastructure, supercomputers, and virtually all enterprise security tools.

Unlike Windows, Linux is completely open source — no hidden telemetry, full access to every system component, and the ability to modify and rebuild anything. Security professionals need this level of transparency and control.

Every major attack tool and defence platform runs on Linux. Kali, Metasploit, Nmap, Wireshark, Burp Suite, Splunk — all built for Linux first. Windows support is always secondary.

20 min · linuxjourney.com — Intro Lesson
red Why Linux for Security? Importance
Why Linux is the Security Standard
Kali Linux — the industry-standard penetration testing distribution. Ships with 600+ pre-installed security tools ready to use from first boot.
Parrot OS — lightweight alternative to Kali. Better for digital forensics and OSINT work. Popular in SOC analyst roles.
Ubuntu Server — the default OS for most SOC infrastructure, SIEM deployments, and cloud security workloads.
96% of web servers run Linux. To attack a target or defend it, you must understand its operating system from the inside out.

Shell scripting lets you automate entire attack workflows, reconnaissance pipelines, and incident response procedures — saving hours on every engagement.

20 min · kali.org/docs — Official Kali Documentation
dark Linux Distributions Distros
Linux Distributions: Which One?
Debian-based (Ubuntu, Kali, Parrot): Uses apt package manager. Ubuntu = best for beginners. Kali = best for security and pentesting. Most common in security roles.
Red Hat-based (RHEL, CentOS, Fedora): Uses yum/dnf package manager. RHEL and CentOS dominate enterprise production environments. RHCSA certification targets these.
Arch-based (Arch, Manjaro, BlackArch): Bleeding-edge packages, maximum control. BlackArch has 2,800+ security tools. For advanced users only.

Recommendation: Start with Ubuntu for learning. Install Kali as your security lab VM. Know that enterprise work will likely involve RHEL/CentOS.

15 min · distrowatch.com — Compare all major distributions
white Linux Filesystem Structure
Linux Filesystem Structure
/ — Root of the entire filesystem
/home — User home directories (SSH keys, bash history)
/etc — Config files (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/ssh)
/var/log — System logs (auth.log, syslog, apache2/access.log)
/tmp — Temp files — world-writable — attackers stage here
/bin /usr/bin — Executables and system binaries
/root — Root user's home — the ultimate pentest target
20 min · TryHackMe 'Linux Fundamentals Part 1'
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 15 Checklist
  • 1Understand why Linux dominates security — server share, tool ecosystem, open-source control
  • 2Know the 3 main distro families — Debian, Red Hat, Arch — and which to use when
  • 3Memorise the 7 critical Linux directories — /etc, /home, /var/log, /tmp, /root, /bin, /proc
  • 4Set up Linux access today — VirtualBox + Ubuntu, WSL2, or TryHackMe AttackBox
  • 5Run `ls /` to explore the root filesystem — identify what lives in each directory
Total Day 15 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Interactive LinuxJourney.com — Free Interactive Linux Course linuxjourney.com
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Linux Fundamentals Part 1' (free) tryhackme.com/room/linuxfundamentalspart1
📖 Course Cisco NetAcad — Linux Unhatched (free) netacad.com/courses/linux-unhatched
🛠️ Download Kali Linux — Official ISO Download kali.org/get-kali
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 15
  • 1Install Ubuntu in VirtualBox OR enable WSL2 on Windows — do this today, not tomorrow
  • 2Boot Linux and run `ls /` — explore the root filesystem directory by directory
  • 3Complete linuxjourney.com Intro lesson — interactive, browser-based, no setup needed
  • 4Take a screenshot of your terminal showing `ls /` — you are now a Linux user
Hands-On Task: 60 – 90 minutes (including setup)
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 15
"Use Linux daily — even just for file operations and browsing. Muscle memory with commands is built through daily repetition, not weekly reading."
The single biggest differentiator between junior and senior security professionals is Linux fluency. Not knowing — fluency. The commands should feel natural, not requiring thought. That only comes from daily use for months, starting today.
⚠ Common Mistake
Only using Linux inside guided tutorials. Tutorial Linux is not real Linux. Open a terminal and use it for your actual daily tasks — writing notes, managing files, browsing the web. Forced daily exposure builds the muscle memory that tutorials never can.
DAY 16
Essential Linux Commands: Navigation
Master these 15 commands and you can navigate any Linux system.
#Linux#CLI#Day16of90
dark Navigation Core Foundation
Navigation: 6 Commands to Master First
pwd # Print working directory — where am I?
ls # List files in current directory
ls -la # Detailed list — includes hidden files (dot files)
cd /var/log # Change to absolute path
cd .. # Go up one directory level
cd ~ # Jump to your home directory instantly
20 min · linuxjourney.com — Command Line Lesson
red File Operations Critical Commands
File Operations: Create, Move, Delete
touch file.txt # Create empty file
mkdir reports # Create directory
mkdir -p a/b/c # Create nested directories
cp source.txt dest/ # Copy file to directory
mv file.txt newname # Rename OR move file
rm file.txt # Delete file (permanent — no recycle bin)
rm -rf directory/ # Force delete directory and ALL contents
20 min · Practice in your terminal — create, move, delete files
dark Viewing Files File Reading
Viewing Files: 4 Essential Commands
cat /etc/passwd # Print entire file to terminal
less /var/log/syslog # Paginate — q to quit, / to search
head -n 10 file.txt # Show first 10 lines only
tail -n 20 file.txt # Show last 20 lines only
tail -f /var/log/syslog # Follow live — new lines appear in real-time
20 min · Run each command against real system files
white Finding Files Search Commands
Finding Files: Search the Entire System
find / -name '*.conf' 2>/dev/null # Find all .conf files
find /home -name '*.txt' -type f # Files only, not dirs
find / -user root -perm -4000 # SUID files owned by root
locate passwd # Fast search using database
which python3 # Find executable location
whereis nmap # Binary + manual page paths
25 min · Run find commands against your system — explore results
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 16 Checklist
  • 1Navigate using only pwd, ls -la, cd — no mouse, no file manager, CLI only
  • 2Create a directory structure — mkdir -p nested folders — then delete it with rm -rf
  • 3View file contents with cat, less, head, tail — use each one on a real system file
  • 4Use tail -f /var/log/syslog — watch live system events for 2 minutes
  • 5Find all hidden files in your home directory with ls -la ~ — document what you find
Total Day 16 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Interactive LinuxJourney.com — Command Line Section linuxjourney.com
🎮 Wargame OverTheWire Bandit — Linux CLI Wargame (free) overthewire.org/wargames/bandit
🧪 Lab LabEx — Free Browser-Based Linux Labs labex.io/free-labs/linux
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Linux Fundamentals Part 1' (free) tryhackme.com/room/linuxfundamentalspart1
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 16
  • 1Navigate to /var/log — run `ls -la` — view the last 20 lines of syslog with `tail -n 20 syslog`
  • 2Create a directory structure: mkdir -p ~/practice/security/logs — then navigate through it
  • 3Find all .conf files on your system: `find /etc -name '*.conf' 2>/dev/null | head -20`
  • 4Write a cheat sheet of today's 15 commands — what each does, one real example per command
Hands-On Task: 45 – 60 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 16
"`rm -rf` is one of the most dangerous commands in Linux. Never run it as root without being absolutely certain of the exact path. There is no undo."
Before any `rm -rf` command, use `ls` or `echo` to verify the path first. The command `echo rm -rf /path/` will print what would be deleted without actually deleting it — use this safety check every single time.
⚠ Common Mistake
Running all commands as root for convenience. Root access means any mistake, any typo, any malicious command has system-wide impact. Always work as a regular user. Only use sudo when explicitly required. This is professional discipline.
DAY 17
File Permissions & User Management
Linux permissions protect every file. Know them or get exploited.
#Linux#Permissions#Day17of90
dark Permission Structure Foundation
Permission Structure: rwx Explained
chmodSUIDsudo

Every Linux file has a 10-character permission string. Example: `-rwxr-xr--` breaks down as: file type (1) + owner permissions (3) + group permissions (3) + others permissions (3).

-rwxr-xr-- │├──┤├──┤├──┤ │owner group others
r (read=4) — view file contents or list directory
w (write=2) — modify file contents or add/delete in directory
x (execute=1) — run file as program or enter directory

Octal notation: Add values together. 7=rwx, 6=rw-, 5=r-x, 4=r--. chmod 755 = rwxr-xr-x. chmod 644 = rw-r--r--.

25 min · linuxjourney.com — Permissions Lesson
red Special Permissions Critical Concept
SUID & Special Permissions: PrivEsc Path
SUID (Set User ID): File runs with the permissions of its OWNER rather than the user executing it. SUID bit on a root-owned binary = that binary runs as root for anyone who executes it.
SGID (Set Group ID): Similar to SUID but applies group ownership. Files created inside a SGID directory inherit the directory's group — used for collaborative directories.
Sticky Bit: On directories — only the file owner can delete their own files, even if others have write access. Used on /tmp to prevent users deleting each other's temp files.

Pentest use: SUID binaries on root-owned files are a classic privilege escalation vector. Finding a SUID bash binary or a custom SUID program = potential instant root access.

25 min · GTFOBins — SUID binary exploitation reference
dark chmod & chown Commands
chmod & chown: Change Permissions
chmod 644 file.txt # rw-r--r-- (standard file)
chmod 755 script.sh # rwxr-xr-x (executable script)
chmod +x script.sh # Add execute for all (symbolic)
chmod o-w file.txt # Remove write from others
chown alice:developers f # Change owner and group
chown -R www-data /var/www # Recursive ownership change
20 min · Practice chmod/chown on test files in your lab
white User Management Users & Groups
User Management: Add, Modify, Delete
useradd -m alice # Create user with home directory
passwd alice # Set password for user
usermod -aG sudo alice # Add alice to sudo group
userdel -r alice # Delete user + home directory
id alice # Show UID, GID, and groups
whoami # Current user name
sudo -l # List your sudo permissions
20 min · TryHackMe 'Linux Fundamentals Part 2'
