resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Guide Real Python — requests Library Guide realpython.com/python-requests
🧪 API HTTPBin.org — Safe API for Testing HTTP Methods httpbin.org
🏋️ Labs PortSwigger Web Academy — Free Web Security Labs portswigger.net/web-security
🔧 Target WebGoat — Intentionally Vulnerable Web App github.com/WebGoat/WebGoat
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 33
  • 1Build the directory scanner — load SecLists common.txt wordlist from GitHub
  • 2Test against DVWA in Docker or WebGoat — find at least 3 hidden directories
  • 3Print only responses with status 200, 301, 302, or 403 — filter out 404s
  • 4Add a summary at the end: total paths checked, total found, elapsed time
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 33
"Rate limiting your requests is critical — add `time.sleep(0.1)` between requests to avoid crashing servers or triggering WAF blocks. Responsible scanning is a professional and legal requirement."
The `requests.Session()` object is the most underused feature for security testing. It automatically manages cookies, connection pooling, and headers across all requests — which is essential for testing authenticated web application workflows. Gobuster, dirsearch, and every professional web scanner uses sessions internally.
⚠ Common Mistake
Testing web applications you do not own or have written permission to test. Even a simple directory scan against a production website without authorisation can be classified as unauthorised access in most jurisdictions. Always use DVWA, WebGoat, TryHackMe targets, or applications you personally own.
DAY 34
Scapy: Packet Crafting & Network Analysis
Build custom network packets with Python. This is next-level security.
#Python#Scapy#Day34of90
dark What is Scapy? Foundation
Scapy: Build Any Network Packet
IP/TCPARPsniff

Scapy is a Python library for creating, sending, sniffing, and dissecting raw network packets. It operates at Layer 2 and above — you craft the exact bytes that go onto the wire.

pip install scapy from scapy.all import * # Layer stacking mirrors OSI model: # IP layer / TCP layer / payload pkt = IP(dst='192.168.1.1') / TCP(dport=80, flags='S') # Send and receive: response = sr1(pkt, timeout=2, verbose=0) if response: print(f'Response from {response.src}')
25 min · scapy.net · realpython.com/python-scapy
red SYN Scanner with Scapy Stealth Scanning
SYN Scanner: How Nmap -sS Works
from scapy.all import * def syn_scan(target, port): '''Send SYN, analyse response — half-open scan.''' pkt = IP(dst=target) / TCP(dport=port, flags='S') response = sr1(pkt, timeout=1, verbose=0) if response is None: return 'FILTERED' # No response = firewall elif response.haslayer(TCP): if response[TCP].flags == 0x12: # SYN-ACK # Send RST to avoid completing handshake rst = IP(dst=target) / TCP(dport=port, flags='R') send(rst, verbose=0) return 'OPEN' elif response[TCP].flags == 0x14: # RST-ACK return 'CLOSED'

This is exactly how Nmap -sS (stealth scan) works — send SYN, never complete the 3-way handshake, analyse the response. Evades some IDS signatures that only log completed connections.

30 min · Test in your lab VM only — requires root
dark ARP & Packet Sniffing Network Discovery
ARP Discovery & Packet Sniffing
# ARP host discovery on local network: from scapy.all import ARP, Ether, srp arp = ARP(pdst='192.168.1.0/24') ether = Ether(dst='ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff') # Broadcast packet = ether / arp result = srp(packet, timeout=2, verbose=0)[0] for sent, received in result: print(f'IP: {received.psrc} MAC: {received.hwsrc}')
# Packet sniffing — capture HTTP traffic: def packet_callback(pkt): if pkt.haslayer('Raw'): payload = pkt['Raw'].load.decode('utf-8', errors='ignore') if 'HTTP' in payload or 'GET' in payload: print(payload[:200]) # Capture 10 TCP port 80 packets: sniff(filter='tcp port 80', prn=packet_callback, count=10, iface='eth0')
25 min · Run in your local lab VM as root
white ICMP Ping Connectivity Testing
ICMP Ping: Custom Implementation
from scapy.all import IP, ICMP, sr1 def ping(target): '''Custom ICMP ping — analyse TTL for OS detection.''' pkt = IP(dst=target) / ICMP() response = sr1(pkt, timeout=2, verbose=0) if response: ttl = response[IP].ttl if ttl <= 64: os_guess = 'Linux/Unix' elif ttl <= 128: os_guess = 'Windows' else: os_guess = 'Network device' print(f'{target} is UP — TTL={ttl} → {os_guess}') else: print(f'{target} is DOWN or blocking ICMP') ping('8.8.8.8')
20 min · Test ping TTL against 8.8.8.8 and compare to a Windows machine
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 34 Checklist
  • 1Install Scapy: `pip install scapy` — understand why it requires root/admin privileges
  • 2Build a custom ICMP ping — check the TTL response and guess the target OS
  • 3Understand SYN scan mechanics — why half-open is stealthier than full TCP connect
  • 4Run ARP discovery in your local lab network — list all hosts with IP and MAC
  • 5Use sniff() to capture 10 packets — print any HTTP content found in payloads
Total Day 34 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources

📖 Guide Real Python — Python Scapy Guide realpython.com/python-scapy
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Intro to Networking' Room (packet analysis) tryhackme.com/room/introtonetworking
📺 Video TCM Security — Practical Python Networking (YouTube) youtube.com/watch?v=egg-GoT5iVk
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 34
  • 1Run as root on your VM: `sudo python3 -c "from scapy.all import *; print('Scapy ready')"`
  • 2Build the ICMP ping — test against 8.8.8.8 — what TTL and OS does it suggest?
  • 3Run ARP discovery on your local /24 subnet — list all responding hosts
  • 4Sniff 10 packets on your interface — print the summary of each one with `.summary()`
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 34
"Understanding Scapy at the packet level means understanding every network attack and defence mechanism. When you understand how SYN flood works in Scapy, you understand every firewall rule designed to stop it."
Scapy is the tool security researchers use to prototype new attack techniques before they appear in tools like Nmap or Metasploit. When a new network vulnerability is discovered, the first proof-of-concept is almost always a Scapy script. Learning Scapy puts you at the research level of networking.
⚠ Common Mistake
Using Scapy on networks you do not own or control. Packet injection and ARP manipulation disrupt network traffic for ALL devices on the segment — not just your target. Running ARP spoofing on a production corporate network, even accidentally, can cause network-wide outages and is a serious legal issue.
DAY 35
Python Cryptography & Hashing
Implement real encryption and hashing in Python. Security from the code up.
#Python#Cryptography#Day35of90
dark hashlib Foundation
hashlib: Hashing in Pure Python
hashlibbcryptFernet
import hashlib data = b'password123' # Must be bytes (b prefix) md5 = hashlib.md5(data).hexdigest() # 128-bit — BROKEN sha1 = hashlib.sha1(data).hexdigest() # 160-bit — weak sha256 = hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest() # 256-bit — STRONG sha512 = hashlib.sha512(data).hexdigest() # 512-bit — strongest print(f'MD5: {md5}') print(f'SHA256: {sha256}')
# File integrity check — malware detection: def hash_file(filepath): h = hashlib.sha256() with open(filepath, 'rb') as f: while chunk := f.read(8192): # Read in chunks h.update(chunk) return h.hexdigest() hash_val = hash_file('/usr/bin/python3') print(f'SHA256: {hash_val}') # Compare to known good
25 min · python.org docs hashlib · realpython.com
red Password Hashing with bcrypt Secure Password Storage
bcrypt: How Passwords Should Be Stored
import bcrypt password = b'supersecret123' # Hash password with salt (salt generated automatically): hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password, bcrypt.gensalt()) print(hashed) # $2b$12$... — includes salt and cost factor
# Verify password at login: def verify_password(plain, hashed): return bcrypt.checkpw(plain, hashed) if verify_password(b'supersecret123', hashed): print('Password correct — access granted') else: print('Wrong password — access denied')

Why bcrypt beats MD5/SHA: bcrypt is intentionally slow — designed to take milliseconds per hash. This makes brute-forcing 10,000 times slower than MD5. The cost factor (default 12) can be increased as hardware gets faster.

20 min · Implement bcrypt password hashing from scratch
dark Symmetric Encryption Data Protection
Fernet: Symmetric Encryption Made Simple
from cryptography.fernet import Fernet # Generate and store key securely (never hardcode!): key = Fernet.generate_key() print(f'Key: {key}') # Keep this safe — loss = data loss # Create Fernet instance with key: f = Fernet(key) # Encrypt: secret = b'Target: 192.168.1.1, Creds: admin/pass123' encrypted = f.encrypt(secret) print(f'Encrypted: {encrypted}')
# Decrypt (requires same key): decrypted = f.decrypt(encrypted) print(f'Decrypted: {decrypted.decode()}') # Fernet uses AES-128-CBC under the hood + # HMAC-SHA256 for authentication. # This is production-grade symmetric encryption.
25 min · Build an encrypted credential storage tool
white Hash Cracker Project Offensive Crypto
Hash Cracker: How Offline Attacks Work
import hashlib target_hash = '5e884898da28047151d0e56f8dc6292773603d0d6aabbdd62a11ef721d1542d8' def crack_hash(wordlist_path, target, algorithm='sha256'): with open(wordlist_path, errors='ignore') as f: for line in f: word = line.strip() attempt = hashlib.new(algorithm, word.encode()).hexdigest() if attempt == target: print(f'[CRACKED] Hash = {word}') return word print('Not found in wordlist') return None crack_hash('rockyou.txt', target_hash) # Will find 'password'
25 min · Build and run the hash cracker against rockyou.txt
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 35 Checklist
  • 1Hash the string 'password' with MD5, SHA1, and SHA256 using hashlib — compare the outputs
  • 2Write a file integrity checker — hash a file, save the hash, re-hash and compare to detect tampering
  • 3Implement bcrypt password hashing — hash and verify a password with proper salting
  • 4Encrypt and decrypt a string using Fernet — understand what key management means
  • 5Build the hash cracker — run it against the SHA256 challenge hash using rockyou.txt first 1000 lines
Total Day 35 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

Free Resources for Today
📖 Official cryptography.io — Python Cryptography Library Docs cryptography.io
🔧 Tool CyberChef — Free Online Crypto/Hash Testing Tool gchq.github.io/CyberChef
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Cryptography for Beginners' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/cryptographyintro
📖 Reference Python hashlib — Official Documentation docs.python.org/3/library/hashlib.html
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 35
  • 1Hash 'password' with MD5, SHA1, SHA256, SHA512 — save all 4 outputs and compare lengths
  • 2Build the hash cracker — download rockyou.txt first 1000 lines from GitHub
  • 3Run it against the target: `5e884898da28047151d0e56f8dc6292773603d0d6aabbdd62a11ef721d1542d8`
  • 4Implement Fernet encryption — build a simple tool that encrypts a string to a file and decrypts it back
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 35
"Know which cryptographic algorithms are broken. MD5, DES, SHA-1, and RC4 are always the wrong answer — in exams, in code reviews, and in real security assessments."
Building a hash cracker teaches you exactly why database breaches are so catastrophic. When an attacker gets a database dump with MD5 password hashes, rockyou.txt + hashlib cracks 70% of them in minutes. That is why bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 — not SHA256 — must be used for password storage.
⚠ Common Mistake
Never crack hashes you do not own or have authorisation to crack. Even if someone emails you their own hash and asks you to crack it, get that authorisation in writing first. Cracking password hashes without authorisation — even as a test — can have legal consequences in many jurisdictions.
DAY 36
Python for OSINT & Reconnaissance
Automate intelligence gathering. Python + OSINT = next-level recon.
#Python#OSINT#Day36of90
dark OSINT Automation Foundation
OSINT: Automate Intelligence Gathering
WHOISDNSShodan

OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) is gathering information from publicly available sources — legal, no hacking required. Python automates what takes hours manually into seconds.

Professional recon workflow: Domain → WHOIS → DNS records → Subdomain enumeration → Shodan scan → Have I Been Pwned check → Compile report. Manually this takes hours. Automated Python does it in 60 seconds.

Everything in this section is legal when run against public data and domains you have permission to test. OSINT is the first phase of every authorised penetration test engagement.

20 min · osintframework.com · realpython.com
red WHOIS & DNS in Python Domain Intelligence
WHOIS & DNS: Domain Intelligence
import whois # pip install python-whois domain = whois.whois('example.com') print(f'Registrar: {domain.registrar}') print(f'Created: {domain.creation_date}') print(f'Expires: {domain.expiration_date}') print(f'Name servers:{domain.name_servers}')
import dns.resolver # pip install dnspython def query_dns(domain, record_type): try: answers = dns.resolver.resolve(domain, record_type) return [str(r) for r in answers] except: return [] print('A: ', query_dns('google.com', 'A')) print('MX: ', query_dns('google.com', 'MX')) print('NS: ', query_dns('google.com', 'NS')) print('TXT:', query_dns('google.com', 'TXT'))
25 min · Run WHOIS and DNS queries against domains you own
dark Subdomain Enumeration Attack Surface Discovery
Subdomain Enumeration: Expand Attack Surface
import dns.resolver import concurrent.futures common_subs = ['www','mail','vpn','dev','api','staging', 'admin','portal','remote','ftp','smtp'] def check_subdomain(sub, domain): full = f'{sub}.{domain}' try: ip = dns.resolver.resolve(full, 'A') return (full, str(ip[0])) except: return None target = 'example.com' with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) as ex: futures = [ex.submit(check_subdomain, s, target) for s in common_subs] for f in concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures): result = f.result() if result: print(f'[FOUND] {result[0]} → {result[1]}')
25 min · Run against a domain you own — expand wordlist
white Shodan & HIBP APIs Intelligence APIs
Shodan & HIBP: Threat Intelligence
import shodan # pip install shodan — free API key at shodan.io api = shodan.Shodan('YOUR_API_KEY') results = api.search('apache country:US port:80') for r in results['matches'][:5]: print(f"IP: {r['ip_str']} | Port: {r['port']}") print(f"Banner: {r.get('data', '')[:100]}")
import requests # Check email in breach databases: def check_hibp(email, api_key): url = f'https://haveibeenpwned.com/api/v3/breachedaccount/{email}' headers = {'hibp-api-key': api_key, 'user-agent': 'SecurityAuditTool'} r = requests.get(url, headers=headers) if r.status_code == 200: breaches = [b['Name'] for b in r.json()] return breaches return []
20 min · Create free accounts at shodan.io and haveibeenpwned.com

  • 2Query A, MX, NS, and TXT DNS records using dnspython for a domain you own
  • 3Run subdomain enumeration against your own domain with the common subdomains list
  • 4Sign up for a free Shodan account and make your first API query
  • 5Build the domain recon tool — WHOIS + all DNS records + subdomain check → output to report
Total Day 36 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

Free Resources for Today
🔍 Tool Shodan.io — Internet Device Search Engine (free tier) shodan.io
🔒 Tool HaveIBeenPwned — Breach Database API haveibeenpwned.com/API/v3
🕸️ Map OSINT Framework — Complete Tool Directory osintframework.com

white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 36
  • 1Build a domain recon tool: given a domain, collect WHOIS + all DNS record types + subdomain check
  • 2Run it against a domain you own or have permission to test — not random third-party domains
  • 3Output results to a file: `recon_report_{domain}_{date}.txt` with clear sections
  • 4Sign up for Shodan free account — make one API search query and print the results
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 36
"Ethical hackers begin every engagement with OSINT. It is free, legal, and reveals surprising details about targets. Companies expose far more than they realise through public DNS, WHOIS, and job postings."
Job postings are goldmine OSINT. A company posting for 'AWS Administrator with Splunk experience and Palo Alto firewall management' just told every attacker exactly what their infrastructure looks like. Add job posting scraping to your OSINT toolkit — it is 100% legal and extremely revealing.
⚠ Common Mistake
Running OSINT tools against domains you do not own or have permission to test. WHOIS and DNS queries are passive and legal on any domain. Shodan searches are passive and legal. Active subdomain brute-forcing against domains you do not own can trigger automated abuse alerts and may cross legal boundaries in some jurisdictions.
DAY 37
Python: Building a Vulnerability Scanner
Build your own scanner. Not just use one.
#Python#VulnScanner#Day37of90
dark Project Architecture Foundation
Vulnerability Scanner: Architecture First
NVD APIModulesReports

Project goal: Combine everything learned — port scan → banner grab → identify service version → query CVE database → generate report. This is the core workflow of commercial scanners like Nessus and OpenVAS.

security_scanner/ ├── main.py # Entry point — argparse CLI ├── scanner.py # Port scanning with threading ├── banner_grab.py # Banner extraction per service ├── cve_lookup.py # NVD API integration ├── report.py # HTML/JSON report generation └── requirements.txt # pip dependencies

Design principle: Each module does one job. scanner.py only scans. banner_grab.py only grabs banners. This separation makes the code testable, maintainable, and extendable — the same principles used in professional security tools.

20 min · TCM Security Python course YouTube
red NVD CVE API CVE Database
NVD API: Free CVE Intelligence
import requests def search_cves(keyword, results_per_page=5): '''Query NIST NVD for CVEs matching keyword.''' url = 'https://services.nvd.nist.gov/rest/json/cves/2.0' params = { 'keywordSearch': keyword, 'resultsPerPage': results_per_page } r = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=10) if r.status_code == 200: data = r.json() return data.get('vulnerabilities', []) return []
# Parse CVE results: cves = search_cves('Apache 2.4.49') for cve in cves: c = cve['cve'] cve_id = c['id'] desc = c['descriptions'][0]['value'][:100] score = c.get('metrics', {}).get('cvssMetricV31', [{}])[0]\ .get('cvssData', {}).get('baseScore', 'N/A') print(f'{cve_id} | CVSS: {score} | {desc}')
25 min · Test NVD API against different service banners
dark Full Scanner Integration Integration
Complete Scanner Workflow
# main.py — connecting all modules: from scanner import port_scan from banner_grab import grab_banner from cve_lookup import search_cves from report import generate_report def run_scan(target, port_range=(1, 1025)): results = [] open_ports = port_scan(target, port_range) for port in open_ports: banner = grab_banner(target, port) cves = [] if banner: cves = search_cves(banner[:50]) # First 50 chars results.append({ 'port': port, 'banner': banner, 'cves': cves }) generate_report(target, results) return results
30 min · Build the MVP scanner — 3 modules, one clean output
white Report Generation Professional Output
Report Generation: Professional Output
import json from datetime import datetime def generate_report(target, results): report = { 'target': target, 'scan_date': datetime.now().isoformat(), 'findings': results, 'summary': { 'open_ports': len(results), 'cves_found': sum(len(r['cves']) for r in results), 'critical': sum(1 for r in results for c in r['cves'] if get_cvss(c) >= 9.0) } } filename = f'scan_{target}_{datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d")}.json' with open(filename, 'w') as f: json.dump(report, f, indent=2) print(f'Report saved: {filename}')
20 min · Add report generation to your scanner
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 37 Checklist

  • 2Query the NVD API — search for 'Apache 2.4' and print CVE IDs with CVSS scores
  • 3Connect port scanner + banner grabber into a single workflow in main.py
  • 4Add CVE lookup for each banner found — flag any CVSS >= 7.0 as HIGH or CRITICAL
  • 5Generate a JSON report — target, scan date, open ports, banners, CVEs per finding

📖 Templates Jinja2 — Python HTML Report Templating jinja.palletsprojects.com
📚 Examples GitHub — Python Security Scanner Examples github.com/topics/python-security
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 37
  • 1Build the minimum viable scanner: scan ports → grab banner → search NVD → print CVSS score
  • 2Test against scanme.nmap.org — find the real services and look up their CVEs
  • 3Generate a JSON report file with your findings — formatted with indent=2 for readability
  • 4Add to GitHub — create a repo with a basic README describing what the tool does
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 37
"Adding NVD CVE lookup to your portfolio scanner is impressive to employers. It shows you understand the complete recon → vulnerability correlation → reporting workflow that professional tools use."
The NVD API is free and requires no API key for basic queries (just respect the rate limit of 5 requests per 30 seconds without a key). Add your NVD API key in a `.env` file to get higher rate limits. Never hardcode API keys in source code — use `python-dotenv` to load them from environment files.
⚠ Common Mistake
Publishing a CVE scanner to GitHub without responsible disclosure notes. If your tool can actually identify exploitable vulnerabilities in production systems, add clear documentation: for authorised testing only, how to report vulnerabilities found, and links to responsible disclosure guidelines. This protects you legally and professionally.
DAY 38
Python Automation: Security Tasks
Automate the boring security stuff. Free up analyst time for real work.
#Python#Automation#Day38of90
dark os & subprocess Foundation
os & subprocess: Run Anything from Python
subprocessschedulealerts
import subprocess # Run Nmap from Python and capture output: result = subprocess.run( ['nmap', '-sV', '-p', '80,443,22', 'target.com'], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=60 ) print(result.stdout) # Nmap output as string print(result.stderr) # Any error messages
import os # File system operations: os.path.exists('/var/log/auth.log') # Check file exists os.listdir('/var/log/') # List directory os.makedirs('reports/scans', exist_ok=True) # Create dirs os.path.getsize('scan_results.json') # File size in bytes
20 min · realpython.com automation · Python.org subprocess docs
red Scheduled Security Tasks Automation
Scheduled Tasks: Automated Security
import schedule import time # pip install schedule def hourly_log_check(): print('Running log analysis...') # check_auth_log(), count_failures(), alert_if_high() def daily_vuln_scan(): print('Running vulnerability scan...') # scan_critical_hosts(), check_cvss(), email_report() # Schedule tasks: schedule.every(1).hour.do(hourly_log_check) schedule.every().day.at('06:00').do(daily_vuln_scan) schedule.every().monday.do(generate_weekly_report) # Run forever: while True: schedule.run_pending() time.sleep(60) # Check every minute
20 min · Build a scheduled log checker that runs every hour
dark Email & Slack Alerts Notifications
Alerts: Email & Slack Notifications
import smtplib from email.message import EmailMessage def send_alert(subject, body, to_addr): msg = EmailMessage() msg['Subject'] = f'🚨 Security Alert: {subject}' msg['From'] = 'security@company.com' msg['To'] = to_addr msg.set_content(body) with smtplib.SMTP_SSL('smtp.gmail.com', 465) as s: s.login('user', 'app_password') s.send_message(msg)
import requests # Slack webhook alert (simpler than email): def slack_alert(webhook_url, message): payload = {'text': f':rotating_light: {message}'} requests.post(webhook_url, json=payload) # Example usage: if failed_logins > 100: slack_alert(WEBHOOK, f'{failed_logins} failed SSH logins from {top_ip}')
20 min · Create a free Slack webhook and test your alert system
white Threat Intel Integration SOAR-lite
Threat Intel: Automated IOC Checking
import requests import time def check_abuseipdb(ip, api_key): url = 'https://api.abuseipdb.com/api/v2/check' headers = {'Key': api_key, 'Accept': 'application/json'} params = {'ipAddress': ip, 'maxAgeInDays': 90} r = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params) data = r.json()['data'] return data['abuseConfidenceScore'] # 0-100
# Process list of suspicious IPs from logs: with open('suspicious_ips.txt') as f: for ip in f: ip = ip.strip() score = check_abuseipdb(ip, API_KEY) if score >= 75: print(f'[MALICIOUS] {ip} — Score: {score}') block_ip(ip) # Add firewall rule slack_alert(WEBHOOK, # Alert the team f'Blocked malicious IP {ip} (score: {score})') time.sleep(1) # Respect rate limit
25 min · Build the automated IP threat checker

  • 2Schedule a log check function to run every hour using the schedule library

resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Guide Real Python — Python Automation Guide realpython.com/python-automation
🔌 API AbuseIPDB — Free IP Reputation API (1,000/day) abuseipdb.com/api
📖 Docs schedule — Python Job Scheduling Library schedule.readthedocs.io
🔧 Webhooks Slack API — Create Free Incoming Webhooks api.slack.com/messaging/webhooks
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 38
  • 1Build the automated IP threat checker — read IPs from a file, query AbuseIPDB for each
  • 2Flag any IP with confidence score above 75% as malicious — output to report.txt
  • 3Set up a free Slack workspace and webhook — send a test alert from your Python script
  • 4Schedule your log checker to run every 5 minutes — verify it fires using print statements
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 38
"Python automation is what separates a $50k analyst from a $90k analyst. The ability to automate 'check these 10,000 IPs against threat intel' saves hours daily and directly drives career advancement."
What you built today — log reading + threat intel API + alerting — is a simplified version of what commercial SOAR platforms like Palo Alto XSOAR, Splunk SOAR, and Microsoft Sentinel do at enterprise scale. Understanding the fundamentals makes you a better user and buyer of these tools, not just an operator.
⚠ Common Mistake
Ignoring API rate limits. Every free API tier has limits. AbuseIPDB allows 1,000 checks per day. VirusTotal allows 4 per minute. Without `time.sleep()` between requests, your script will exhaust the daily quota in minutes, get your API key blocked, and produce incomplete results. Always read the rate limit documentation before building.
DAY 39
Python: Working with APIs & Threat Intel
Connect your scripts to real threat intelligence. APIs make it possible.
#Python#ThreatIntel#Day39of90
dark REST APIs Foundation
REST APIs: Every Security Tool Has One
VirusTotalShodanGreyNoise

REST APIs are HTTP-based interfaces that return data in JSON format. Every modern security platform — VirusTotal, Shodan, Splunk, CrowdStrike, Palo Alto — exposes an API for automation.

import requests API_KEY = 'your_key_here' # Most APIs use one of these auth methods: # 1. Header-based (most common): headers = {'X-Apikey': API_KEY} # 2. Bearer token: headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {API_KEY}'} # 3. Query parameter: params = {'api_key': API_KEY, 'query': 'data'} r = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params) data = r.json() # Parse JSON response
20 min · realpython.com/python-api · requests docs
red VirusTotal API File & URL Intelligence
VirusTotal: 70+ AV Engines in One API
import requests import hashlib VT_KEY = 'your_virustotal_api_key' def check_file_hash(file_path): # Calculate SHA256 of file: sha256 = hashlib.sha256() with open(file_path, 'rb') as f: sha256.update(f.read()) file_hash = sha256.hexdigest() # Query VirusTotal: url = f'https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/files/{file_hash}' headers = {'x-apikey': VT_KEY} r = requests.get(url, headers=headers) if r.status_code == 200: stats = r.json()['data']['attributes']['last_analysis_stats'] malicious = stats.get('malicious', 0) total = sum(stats.values()) print(f'Malicious: {malicious}/{total} engines')
25 min · Create free VT account and test file hash check
dark Shodan & GreyNoise Network Intelligence
Shodan & GreyNoise: Network Intel
import shodan api = shodan.Shodan('YOUR_SHODAN_KEY') # Enrich an IP with Shodan intelligence: def shodan_enrich(ip): try: host = api.host(ip) return { 'org': host.get('org', 'Unknown'), 'country': host.get('country_name', 'Unknown'), 'ports': host.get('ports', []), 'cves': host.get('vulns', []) } except shodan.APIError: return None
# GreyNoise — is this IP benign scanner or real threat? import requests def check_greynoise(ip): url = f'https://api.greynoise.io/v3/community/{ip}' r = requests.get(url) # Free community tier if r.status_code == 200: data = r.json() return data.get('classification', 'unknown') # Returns: 'benign' (scanner) | 'malicious' | 'unknown'
20 min · Test GreyNoise against known scanner IPs
white IOC Enricher Project Portfolio Project
IOC Enricher: Portfolio-Worthy SOAR Tool
import requests, time, csv def enrich_ip(ip): result = {'ip': ip} # VirusTotal check: vt = check_virustotal_ip(ip) # Returns malicious count result['vt_malicious'] = vt time.sleep(15) # VT: 4 requests/minute on free tier # AbuseIPDB check: abuse = check_abuseipdb(ip) # Returns 0-100 score result['abuse_score'] = abuse time.sleep(1) # GreyNoise classification: gn = check_greynoise(ip) # benign/malicious/unknown result['greynoise'] = gn # Verdict: if vt > 3 or abuse >= 75: result['verdict'] = 'MALICIOUS' elif gn == 'benign': result['verdict'] = 'BENIGN_SCANNER' else: result['verdict'] = 'UNKNOWN' return result
30 min · Build and test the full IOC enricher

🔌 API GreyNoise Community API — Free, No Key Required greynoise.io/api

📖 Platform MISP — Open Source Threat Intelligence Platform misp-project.org
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 39
  • 1Create free accounts at VirusTotal and AbuseIPDB — get API keys for both
  • 2Build the IOC Enricher — input a file of 10 suspicious IPs from your auth.log brute force analysis
  • 3Output an enriched CSV: IP | VT Malicious Count | AbuseIPDB Score | GreyNoise | Verdict
  • 4Push to GitHub with README: Purpose, Installation, Usage, Example Output, Screenshot
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 39
"This IOC enricher is exactly what Tier 1 SOC analysts do manually for hours every day. Automate it and you have built a SOAR-lite tool. Put it on GitHub — instant portfolio point."
Never upload real customer IP addresses, file hashes from production systems, or any PII to VirusTotal. VirusTotal submissions are public — all data uploaded to the API is accessible to the entire VirusTotal community. Use a separate test account for portfolio projects and never mix production data with learning projects.
⚠ Common Mistake
Hardcoding API keys in Python files pushed to GitHub. GitHub scans public repositories for exposed API keys and so do attackers. Exposed keys get abused within minutes of being committed. Always store API keys in a `.env` file, load them with `python-dotenv`, and add `.env` to `.gitignore` before your first commit.
DAY 40
Python Pandas for Security Analytics
Analyse millions of log events with 5 lines of Python. Pandas for security.
#Python#Pandas#Day40of90
dark Pandas Basics Foundation
Pandas: DataFrames for Log Analysis
DataFramegroupbymatplotlib
import pandas as pd # pip install pandas # Load log file: df = pd.read_csv('access.log', sep=' ', names=['ip','ident','user','time','request', 'status','bytes']) print(df.head()) # First 5 rows print(df.info()) # Column types and null counts print(df.describe()) # Statistical summary print(df.shape) # (rows, columns) — how big is this?
20 min · pandas.pydata.org/docs · realpython.com
red Filtering & Grouping Analysis Operations
Filter & Group: Find the Anomalies
# Filter: only 404 errors (potential scanning): df_404 = df[df['status'] == 404] print(f'{len(df_404)} 404 errors found')
# Count requests per IP (find top talkers): ip_counts = df.groupby('ip').size()\ .sort_values(ascending=False) print(ip_counts.head(10))
# Flag suspicious IPs (>1000 requests): suspicious = ip_counts[ip_counts > 1000] print(f'Suspicious IPs: {list(suspicious.index)}')
# Multi-column analysis: df.groupby(['ip', 'status']).size()\ .unstack(fill_value=0)\ .sort_values(404, ascending=False)\ .head(10) # IPs with most 404s — likely scanning
25 min · kaggle.com/learn/pandas (free)
dark Time Series Analysis Attack Timeline
Time Series: Spot Attack Patterns
# Parse timestamps: df['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(df['time'], format='[%d/%b/%Y:%H:%M:%S') df.set_index('timestamp', inplace=True)
# Requests per hour — find attack spikes: hourly = df.resample('1H').size() print(hourly) # Output: # 2026-04-21 01:00 245 # 2026-04-21 02:00 8942 ← Attack spike!
# Requests per minute for a specific IP: attack_ip = '45.33.32.156' ip_timeline = df[df['ip'] == attack_ip].resample('1T').size() print(ip_timeline) # See exactly when the attack started
20 min · Detect the attack window in your sample log
white Visualisation with Matplotlib Executive Reporting
Matplotlib: Turn Data into Charts
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Bar chart: top 10 IPs by request count: top_ips = df.groupby('ip').size().sort_values().tail(10) top_ips.plot(kind='barh', color='red', figsize=(10,6)) plt.title('Top 10 IPs by Request Volume') plt.xlabel('Request Count') plt.savefig('top_ips.png', bbox_inches='tight') plt.show()
# Line chart: requests over time: hourly = df.resample('1H').size() hourly.plot(figsize=(12,4), color='blue') plt.axhline(y=hourly.mean()*3, color='red', linestyle='--', label='Alert threshold') plt.title('Hourly Request Volume — Attack Spike Visible') plt.legend() plt.savefig('timeline.png')
20 min · Generate 3 charts from your sample log data
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 40 Checklist
  • 1Load a sample Apache access log into a Pandas DataFrame — inspect with .head() and .info()
  • 2Use groupby + size() to find top 10 requesting IPs in one line
  • 3Filter for 404 errors only — which IPs are generating the most 404s? (likely scanners)
  • 4Parse timestamps and use resample('1H') to find the hour with the highest traffic spike
  • 5Generate 3 charts — top IPs bar chart, status code pie chart, hourly traffic line chart
Total Day 40 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Official Pandas Documentation — Official Guides pandas.pydata.org/docs
🎓 Course Kaggle — Free Python + Pandas Course kaggle.com/learn/pandas
📖 Guide Real Python — Pandas Data Exploration Guide realpython.com/pandas-python-explore-dataset
📊 Data GitHub — Sample Apache Access Log Files github.com/topics/apache-log
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 40
  • 1Download a sample Apache access log from GitHub — load it into Pandas with pd.read_csv()
  • 2Find top 5 requesting IPs, most common status codes, and the hour with most traffic
  • 3Generate 3 charts: top IPs bar chart, status code distribution, hourly traffic line with alert threshold
  • 4Save charts as PNG files — these become the visualisation section of your incident report
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 40
"Pandas + Matplotlib is a lightweight SIEM substitute for small organisations. Many Security Engineers build internal dashboards this way — saving thousands in commercial tooling costs."
For logs larger than 1GB, never use `pd.read_csv(file)` directly — it loads the entire file into RAM and will crash on a machine with 8GB. Use `pd.read_csv(file, chunksize=10000)` to process 10,000 rows at a time. Combine with `pd.concat()` to aggregate results across chunks.
⚠ Common Mistake
Presenting raw Pandas output (DataFrames printed to terminal) in reports. Convert analysis to proper charts and formatted tables for any audience above technical staff. `df.to_html()` generates HTML tables instantly. `matplotlib` generates publication-quality charts. Always present findings visually — not as Python output.
DAY 41
Python Project: Complete Security Toolkit
Day 41: Combine everything into one portfolio-worthy security toolkit.
#Python#Portfolio#Day41of90
dark Project Architecture Foundation
Security Toolkit: 5 Modules in One
argparserichGitHub
security_toolkit/ ├── main.py # CLI entry point ├── modules/ │ ├── scanner.py # Threaded port scanner │ ├── banner_grab.py # Service banner extraction │ ├── osint.py # WHOIS + DNS + subdomain enum │ ├── ioc_checker.py # VT + AbuseIPDB + GreyNoise │ └── log_analyser.py # Pandas + matplotlib ├── reports/ # Output directory ├── requirements.txt # pip dependencies ├── .env.example # Template (never commit .env!) ├── .gitignore # Exclude .env and reports └── README.md # Professional documentation
20 min · TCM Security Python course · GitHub examples
red CLI with argparse Professional Interface
argparse: Professional CLI Interface
import argparse parser = argparse.ArgumentParser( description='Security Toolkit v1.0', formatter_class=argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter ) parser.add_argument('--target', required=True, help='Target IP or domain') parser.add_argument('--mode', choices=['scan', 'osint', 'ioc', 'logs'], required=True, help='Operation mode') parser.add_argument('--ports', default='1-1024', help='Port range (default: 1-1024)') parser.add_argument('--output', choices=['json', 'html', 'csv'], default='json', help='Report format') args = parser.parse_args() print(f'Mode: {args.mode} | Target: {args.target}')
25 min · Build the argparse interface for all 5 modes
dark Rich Terminal Output Visual Polish
rich: Beautiful Terminal Output
from rich.console import Console from rich.table import Table from rich.progress import Progress console = Console() # Colour-coded status messages: console.print('[bold red]CRITICAL[/] Port 23 (Telnet) open!') console.print('[bold green]SAFE[/] SSH with key auth enabled') console.print('[yellow]WARNING[/] Default credentials detected')
# Beautiful results table: table = Table(title='Port Scan Results') table.add_column('Port', style='cyan') table.add_column('Service', style='green') table.add_column('Banner', style='yellow') table.add_column('CVEs', style='red') table.add_row('22', 'SSH', 'OpenSSH_8.9', '0') table.add_row('80', 'HTTP', 'Apache 2.4.49', '3 ⚠️') console.print(table)
20 min · Refactor all print() statements to use rich
white GitHub Portfolio Setup Career Asset
GitHub Portfolio: Recruiters Will Find You
  • 1Create repository: `security-toolkit` — public, with a clear one-line description
  • 2Write README.md: include Purpose, Features list, Installation commands, Usage examples with screenshots
  • 3Add requirements.txt: run `pip freeze > requirements.txt` — every dependency documented
  • 4Add .gitignore: exclude `.env`, `reports/`, `*.log`, `pycache` — protect sensitive data
  • 5Add LICENSE: MIT License makes your tool open source and shareable — employers love this
  • 6Tag v1.0: create a GitHub release with changelog — shows professional development practices
30 min · Push your complete toolkit to GitHub today

resources_white Where to Learn Resources
Resources for Building Your Portfolio
📖 Docs Rich — Python Terminal Formatting Library rich.readthedocs.io
📖 Guide Real Python — Python Project Structure Best Practices realpython.com/python-application-layouts
📺 Video TCM Security — Python for Hackers (YouTube) youtube.com/watch?v=egg-GoT5iVk
📦 Deps python-dotenv — Secure Environment Variable Loading github.com/theskumar/python-dotenv
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 41
  • 1Combine all scripts from Days 29–40 into the organised module structure
  • 2Add argparse CLI and rich output formatting to the main entry point
  • 3Test all 4 modes: `--mode scan`, `--mode osint`, `--mode ioc`, `--mode logs`
  • 4Push to GitHub — write a README that makes a recruiter want to star the repository
Hands-On Task: 120 – 150 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 41
"GitHub green squares plus a well-documented Python security project equals a resume multiplier. Recruiters search GitHub. A clear README with screenshots gets noticed immediately."
The README is as important as the code. Write it for a non-technical hiring manager: what problem does it solve, why does it matter for security, how do you run it, what does the output look like. Include screenshots of rich terminal output and sample report JSON. A great README turns a code project into a story.
⚠ Common Mistake
Hardcoding API keys in any file pushed to public GitHub repositories. GitHub secret scanning automatically detects common API key formats and notifies the service provider. Within minutes of a push, your VirusTotal or Shodan key will be disabled and potentially abused. Use `.env` files and python-dotenv from day one — never as an afterthought.
DAY 42
Python Mastery Recap + Challenges
6 weeks. Python week done. You are a script-writing, tool-building security dev now.
#Python#Recap#Day42of90
dark Python Phase Recap Summary
14 Days of Python: Everything You Built
RecapHackerRankNext: Wireshark
Days 29–30: Setup, data types, control flow, functions, error handling — foundations solid
Days 31–32: Socket programming (threaded port scanner, banner grabber), file I/O and log parsing
Days 33–34: requests library (directory scanner, web recon), Scapy (packet crafting, SYN scan)
Days 35–36: Cryptography (hashlib, bcrypt, Fernet, hash cracker), OSINT automation (WHOIS, DNS, Shodan)
Days 37–38: Vulnerability scanner (NVD API + CVE lookup), automation (subprocess, schedule, Slack alerts)
Days 39–41: API integration (VT, AbuseIPDB, GreyNoise, IOC enricher), Pandas log analysis, complete portfolio toolkit
30 min · Review all 14 days of notes and code
red HackerRank Python Track Skills Validation
HackerRank: Validate Your Skills

HackerRank Python Domain has 115 challenges covering every concept you learned this phase. Complete the first 30 and you have validated 80% of the Python skills needed for security scripting.

Collections.Counter — builds log frequency analysis (exactly what you built in Day 32)
re module challenges — regex pattern matching for log parsing and IOC extraction
os module challenges — file system operations for forensics scripts
Itertools — advanced iteration for wordlist processing and subdomain enumeration

Why HackerRank matters: Many companies use HackerRank in technical interviews. Your Python security projects demonstrate applied skill. HackerRank scores validate theoretical understanding. Both together = a strong technical profile.

60 min · Complete 10 HackerRank Python challenges today
dark Advanced Resources Next Level
Advanced Reading: Black Hat Python
'Black Hat Python' (No Starch Press) — the bible of Python security scripting. Chapters 2–5 cover network tools using sockets and raw packets — directly extending what you built this phase.
'Violent Python' (Syngress) — older but excellent. Covers forensics automation, web hacking scripts, and wireless attacks in Python.
awesome-python-security (GitHub) — curated list of Python security libraries, tools, and learning resources. Your next 100 project ideas live here.
TCM Security Practical Ethical Hacking — paid course but Python chapters are referenced by security professionals globally. Worth every penny when you're ready.
Read Black Hat Python chapters 2–5 over the next week
white Python Certification Path Certifications
Python Certifications for Security Roles
  • 1PCEP (Python Certified Entry Professional) — Python Institute. Validates basic Python syntax and data types. Good first certification. Online proctored exam.
  • 2PCAP (Python Certified Associate) — Python Institute. Covers OOP, exceptions, file I/O, and modules. More relevant for security scripting roles.
  • 3No official 'Python Security' certification exists — your GitHub portfolio is the certification. Employers care about working tools, not Python certificates.
  • 4CompTIA CySA+ — tests security analysis skills including Python-based analysis concepts. More valuable than a Python cert for security roles.
  • 5Strategy: Skip Python certs. Invest the time into your GitHub portfolio and HackerRank score. Those are what employers actually check.
15 min · Plan your next 3 Python portfolio projects
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 42 Checklist
  • 1Complete 10 HackerRank Python challenges — focus on collections.Counter, re module, os module
  • 2Verify your security toolkit is on GitHub with a README, requirements.txt, and .gitignore
  • 3Review your 14 days of Python notes — create a master reference cheat sheet
  • 4Order or download Black Hat Python — read the introduction and Chapter 1 tonight
  • 5Understand what Traffic Analysis with Wireshark covers — that is what starts Day 43
Total Day 42 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Resources
Free Resources for Today & Beyond

white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 42
  • 1Complete 10 HackerRank Python challenges — document your score for the profile
  • 2Verify your GitHub toolkit is public, has a README, and shows at least 5 commits
  • 3Write a one-page Python cheat sheet: 7 core modules, 5 security patterns, 3 anti-patterns
