Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
stats Phase 4 Complete Progress Stats
Phase 4 Complete — Your Progress
42
Days of consistent learning — halfway through
7+
Working Python security tools built
1
GitHub portfolio repository live and public
48
Days remaining — Wireshark starts tomorrow
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 42
"Understanding why code works is infinitely more valuable than knowing that it works. When you understand the socket API, you understand every network tool. When you understand hashlib, you understand every cryptography tool."
Traffic Analysis starting Day 43 combines everything from Phase 1 (networking theory), Phase 3 (Linux commands), and Phase 4 (Python scripting) into one unified skill. Wireshark is the visual tool. Python processes the pcap files programmatically. The combination is what professionals use in real incident response.
⚠ Common Mistake
Stopping Python practice now that the phase is complete. Python fluency requires daily use — not a 14-day sprint. Write at least one Python script per week for the rest of the 90 days, even if just to automate a task from another phase. The compounding effect of consistent daily practice is the entire point of this 90-day structure.
5
Phase 05
Traffic Analysis
Days 43–56 · 14 sessions
DAY 43
Wireshark Introduction & Setup
See every packet on your network. Wireshark is the analyst's microscope.
#Wireshark#TrafficAnalysis#Day43of90
dark What is Wireshark? Foundation
Wireshark: The Analyst's Microscope
Packet Capture.pcap filesProtocol Analysis

Wireshark is the world's most widely used free, open-source packet analyser. It captures live network traffic in real-time or reads saved `.pcap` files — showing you every single byte that crosses a network interface.

Who uses Wireshark: SOC analysts investigating incidents, penetration testers analysing traffic, network engineers troubleshooting, forensic investigators recovering evidence, and malware analysts understanding attack behaviour.

Why it matters: Every network attack — every exploit, every C2 beacon, every data exfiltration — leaves traces in network traffic. Wireshark lets you see those traces with complete detail at every layer of the OSI model.

20 min · wireshark.org/#educationalContent — Free Wireshark University
red Installation Setup
Installing Wireshark: All Platforms
# Ubuntu/Kali Linux: sudo apt install wireshark # Add user to wireshark group (capture without root): sudo usermod -aG wireshark $USER newgrp wireshark # Apply group change immediately
# Verify installation: wireshark --version # CLI alternative (no GUI required): sudo apt install tshark # Terminal version of Wireshark

Windows/Mac: Download installer from wireshark.org/download.html — includes WinPcap/Npcap driver automatically. Must have a physical or virtual network interface card to capture live traffic.

15 min · wireshark.org/download.html
dark Interface Overview Navigation
Wireshark Interface: 3 Panels
Packet List (top panel): Every captured packet in chronological order. Columns: No., Time, Source, Destination, Protocol, Length, Info. Colour-coded by protocol type.
Packet Details (middle panel): Layer-by-layer breakdown of the selected packet. Expands each protocol header — Frame (L2) → IP (L3) → TCP (L4) → Application (L7). Click any field to inspect it.
Hex Dump (bottom panel): Raw bytes of the packet in hexadecimal. When you click a field in the details panel, its corresponding bytes highlight here. Essential for malware payload analysis.

Filter bar at the top turns green for valid filters, red for invalid. The blue shark fin button starts/stops live capture. The Statistics menu provides instant traffic profiling.

15 min · Explore each panel in your first capture
white First Packet Capture Hands-On
Your First Live Capture: Step by Step
1Launch Wireshark → you see a list of network interfaces with traffic sparklines. Select your active NIC (usually eth0 or wlan0 — the one with activity).
2Click the blue fin to start capture. Open a browser and navigate to google.com. Stop capture after 30 seconds with the red square button.
3Observe the protocols: You will see DNS (resolving google.com), TCP (connection establishment), TLS (encrypted HTTPS). Layer 1–7 all visible in one capture.
4Right-click any packet field → 'Apply as Filter' → instantly filter to only packets matching that value. This is the most powerful Wireshark workflow shortcut.
20 min · Perform your first live capture right now
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 43 Checklist
  • 1Install Wireshark and verify capture works — you can see packets flowing on your interface
  • 2Start a 30-second capture — browse to 3 different websites — stop and examine the packets
  • 3Apply the `dns` filter — see every domain your computer is resolving in real-time
  • 4Expand a DNS packet in the details panel — identify the query name and response IP
  • 5Download a practice pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net — open and explore it
Total Day 43 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🎓 Official Wireshark University — Free Official Training wireshark.org/#educationalContent
📺 Video Chris Greer — Wireshark Tutorial for Beginners (YouTube) youtube.com/watch?v=NjvR4LmwcMU
🧪 PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net — Free Malware PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Wireshark: The Basics' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/wiresharkthebasics
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 43
  • 1Capture 30 seconds of traffic on your network — browse multiple sites during capture
  • 2Apply the `dns` filter — list every domain your computer contacted — note any unexpected ones
  • 3Expand one DNS packet fully — identify: query name, query type (A/AAAA/MX), response IP
  • 4Download a pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net — open it and note the first 3 protocols in Protocol Hierarchy
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 43
"The fastest way to learn Wireshark is practising with pre-made pcap files. malware-traffic-analysis.net has hundreds of real malware pcaps with answer keys for safe, legal analysis practice."
Save your first pcap file immediately after capture. File → Save As → `first_capture_YYYYMMDD.pcap`. Build a library of captures from different scenarios — normal traffic, your own Nmap scans, web browsing. Comparing normal traffic against malicious pcaps is how analysts develop intuition.
⚠ Common Mistake
Capturing on public networks, corporate networks, or any network you do not own without explicit written permission. Packet capture intercepts traffic from all devices on the network segment — not just your own. This is illegal in most jurisdictions even if you only analyse your own data. Always capture on your own lab network.
DAY 44
Wireshark Filters & Display Techniques
1,000 packets. Find the attack in 30 seconds. Wireshark filters are the key.
#Wireshark#DisplayFilters#Day44of90
dark Capture vs Display Filters Foundation
Two Filter Types: Capture vs Display
BPFDisplay FiltersFollow Stream

Capture Filters (BPF): Applied BEFORE and DURING capture. Use Berkeley Packet Filter syntax. Reduces noise at collection time — fewer packets stored. More efficient for targeted capture. Cannot be changed after capture starts.

# Capture filter examples (BPF syntax): tcp port 80 # Only HTTP traffic host 192.168.1.100 # Only traffic to/from this IP not arp # Exclude ARP broadcast noise port 53 # Only DNS traffic

Display Filters: Applied AFTER capture to an existing file. Wireshark's own filter language — more powerful and flexible than BPF. Turn green when valid, red when invalid. Can be changed freely without losing captured data.

20 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 Wireshark tutorial
red Security Filter Cheat Sheet Essential Filters
Security Filter Cheat Sheet
tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0 # SYN scan detection
icmp # Ping sweep activity
http.request.uri contains "passwd" # LFI attempt
http.request.uri contains "SELECT" # SQL injection attempt
ftp # Cleartext FTP (credentials!)
telnet # Insecure remote access
dns.qry.name contains "evil" # Suspicious DNS query
http.response.code == 404 # Directory brute-force
ip.src == 192.168.1.100 && tcp.flags.syn==1 # Specific IP scan
25 min · Apply each filter against a practice pcap
dark Follow Stream Conversation Reconstruction
Follow Stream: Reconstruct Conversations

Follow TCP Stream is one of Wireshark's most powerful features. Right-click any TCP packet → 'Follow' → 'TCP Stream'. Wireshark reassembles the entire conversation and shows it in a readable window.

HTTP sessions: See full GET/POST requests, headers, and response bodies — including credentials submitted in web forms on unencrypted sites.
FTP sessions: See complete file transfer with the username and password in cleartext — FTP never encrypts credentials.
SMTP emails: Reconstruct full email content including attachments transmitted over unencrypted mail servers.

Colour coding: Client-to-server traffic shown in red. Server-to-client shown in blue. Instantly visualise the direction of each communication in the reconstructed stream.

20 min · Follow a TCP stream in your practice pcap
white Statistics Menu Traffic Profiling
Statistics Menu: 3-Click Traffic Profile
Protocol Hierarchy: Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy. Shows percentage breakdown of all protocols in the capture. Unexpected high-volume protocols (like DNS or ICMP when you expect HTTP) = immediate red flag.
Conversations: Statistics → Conversations. Top talking IP pairs, bytes transferred, packet counts. Identifies the noisiest hosts — attackers and C2 beacons appear here.
I/O Graph: Statistics → I/O Graph. Traffic volume over time — attack spikes, beaconing intervals, and DDoS patterns visible instantly.
Endpoints: Statistics → Endpoints. All unique IPs and their traffic volume. External IPs your internal hosts contacted — identify unexpected cloud infrastructure or C2 servers.
20 min · Apply all 4 statistics views to a malware pcap
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 44 Checklist
  • 1Apply the SYN scan filter — `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` — understand what you see
  • 2Build your personal filter library — save 10 security filters using Analyze → Display Filters
  • 3Use Follow TCP Stream on an HTTP connection — identify what data was transmitted
  • 4Run all 4 Statistics views on a malware pcap — write a 3-sentence traffic profile
  • 5Download a CTF pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net and write a 5-sentence incident summary
Total Day 44 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Reference Wireshark Display Filter Reference — Complete List wireshark.org/docs/dfref
🧪 PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net — Free Malware PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net
📖 Tutorial Guru99 — Wireshark Tutorial for Beginners guru99.com/wireshark-tutorial.html
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Wireshark: Packet Operations' Room tryhackme.com/room/wiresharkpacketoperations
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 44
  • 1Download a malware pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net — open in Wireshark
  • 2Apply these 3 filters in sequence: `http` → `dns` → `tcp.flags.syn==1` — document findings each time
  • 3Follow a TCP stream — reconstruct the full HTTP conversation and describe what happened
  • 4Write a 5-sentence incident summary: what protocol, what attack type, source IP, destination IP, severity
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 44
"Build a personal filter library in Wireshark: Analyze → Display Filters → save your security filters. You will use the same 10–15 filters 90% of the time in real investigations."
Enable Coloring Rules immediately (View → Coloring Rules). Different protocol conversations get different colours — HTTP in green, TCP SYN in red, DNS in blue. Colour-coded packets let your eyes immediately spot anomalies before you apply a single filter. It is the fastest triage technique in Wireshark.
⚠ Common Mistake
Applying display filters without first running Protocol Hierarchy and Conversations statistics. Jumping straight to filtering means you might miss the actual attack — which could be in a protocol you did not think to filter for. Always start with 2 minutes of statistics to understand what is IN the capture before filtering.
DAY 45
TCP/IP Deep Dive with Wireshark
See TCP's 3-way handshake in real packets. Theory becomes reality.
#Wireshark#TCPIP#Day45of90
dark 3-Way Handshake in Wireshark Foundation
3-Way Handshake: Theory Becomes Reality
SYN/ACKTCP FlagsRetransmissions

The TCP 3-way handshake you learned in Day 2 is now visible as real packets. Filter: `tcp && ip.addr==target`. You will see the exact SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK sequence with timestamps.

# Filter to see handshake for specific connection: tcp && ip.addr==192.168.1.1 # In the packet list: # Packet 1: SYN flags=0x002 Client → Server # Packet 2: SYN-ACK flags=0x012 Server → Client # Packet 3: ACK flags=0x010 Client → Server # Packet 4+: Data exchange begins

The Delta Time column shows latency between packets. Add it via: View → Column Preferences → + → Delta Time Displayed. High latency between SYN and SYN-ACK reveals slow or distant servers.

25 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 TCP analysis
red TCP Flags in Packets Flag Analysis
TCP Flags: 6 Flags, Many Attack Patterns
# TCP Flag values (hexadecimal): SYN = 0x002 # Initiate connection ACK = 0x010 # Acknowledge FIN = 0x001 # Graceful close RST = 0x004 # Abrupt reset PSH = 0x008 # Push data immediately URG = 0x020 # Urgent data
# Wireshark filter for each: tcp.flags == 0x002 # Pure SYN (new connection or scan) tcp.flags == 0x012 # SYN-ACK (open port response) tcp.flags.reset == 1 # All RST packets (resets) tcp.flags == 0x029 # URG+SYN+ACK (unusual — investigate)

Attack detection: A single source IP sending SYN packets to hundreds of different ports = port scan. Many SYN packets with no ACK completions = SYN flood DoS. Unexpected RST packets in established sessions = TCP session injection.

20 min · Apply each flag filter against your practice pcap
dark Spotting Port Scans Attack Detection
Detecting Port Scans in Wireshark
# Filter to detect SYN scan from single IP: tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0 && ip.src==192.168.1.100 # Shows: IP sending SYN to many ports (Nmap -sS)
# See the responses — open vs closed ports: tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==1 # SYN-ACK = port OPEN tcp.flags.reset==1 # RST = port CLOSED
# Connect scan detection (full 3-way handshake): tcp.flags == 0x002 # then filter by source IP # Nmap -sT = completes handshake then sends RST

Expert tip: Run `nmap -sS localhost` in one terminal while Wireshark is capturing in another. Watch your own scan appear in real-time — you will see exactly what a victim's IDS sees when they are being scanned.

25 min · Capture your own Nmap scan and analyse it
white HTTP in Wireshark Web Traffic Analysis
HTTP Analysis: Web Attacks Visible
# HTTP request filtering: http.request # All HTTP requests http.request.method=="GET" # Only GET requests http.request.method=="POST" # Form submissions, logins http.request.uri contains "/admin" # Admin panel access
# Response filtering: http.response.code == 200 # Successful responses http.response.code == 404 # Not found (brute force!) http.response.code == 403 # Forbidden (access control)
# Credential exposure detection: http contains "password" # Password in cleartext http contains "login" # Login form submissions # Follow TCP Stream on any POST → may see credentials!
20 min · Analyse HTTP traffic in a practice pcap
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 45 Checklist
  • 1Identify the 3-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) in a live capture — expand each packet
  • 2Capture your own Nmap -sS scan on localhost and observe it in Wireshark simultaneously
  • 3Use `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` — count how many ports were scanned
  • 4Filter `http.response.code == 404` — identify any directory brute-force activity
  • 5Use Expert Information (Analyze menu) — identify all retransmissions and resets
Total Day 45 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video Chris Greer — TCP Analysis with Wireshark (YouTube) youtube.com/watch?v=NjvR4LmwcMU
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Wireshark' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/wireshark
📖 Guide Wireshark TCP Analysis Documentation wireshark.org/docs/wsug_html_chunked
🧪 PCAPs PacketLife.net — Free Sample Packet Captures packetlife.net/captures
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 45
  • 1Run `nmap -sS localhost` in one terminal while Wireshark is capturing on loopback interface
  • 2Apply `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` — count SYN packets from your scan
  • 3Compare SYN-ACK responses (open ports) vs RST responses (closed ports) — list open ports found
  • 4Use Expert Information (Analyze → Expert Information) — note all anomalies flagged automatically
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 45
"Wireshark's Expert Information automatically flags anomalies — retransmissions, resets, malformed packets. Start every pcap analysis there. It is your automated first-pass triage."
The most valuable skill in TCP analysis is knowing what NORMAL looks like. Capture your baseline traffic now — an hour of normal browsing, normal downloads, normal DNS. Save it. Every future investigation starts by comparing suspicious traffic against this baseline. Anomalies only exist relative to normal.
⚠ Common Mistake
Ignoring TCP RST storms. A flood of RST packets in a capture is almost always either malicious (TCP session injection, port scan, DoS) or severely misconfigured. RSTs are the network equivalent of a slammed phone — something went wrong. Every RST in an investigation warrants examination of the source, destination, and timing.
DAY 46
DNS Analysis & DNS Attack Detection
DNS is the most abused protocol for attacks and the most overlooked in defence.
#DNS#TrafficAnalysis#Day46of90
dark DNS in Wireshark Foundation
DNS in Wireshark: Every Query Visible
DNS TunnelingNXDomainDGA

Filter: `dns` — instantly shows every DNS query and response in the capture. DNS uses UDP port 53 for most queries (small packets) and TCP port 53 for large responses and zone transfers.

# DNS display filters: dns # All DNS traffic dns.flags.response == 0 # Only queries (QR=0) dns.flags.response == 1 # Only responses (QR=1) dns.qry.type == 1 # A record queries only dns.qry.type == 16 # TXT record queries dns.flags.rcode == 3 # NXDomain (failed lookups)

Normal DNS behaviour: UDP, small packets (< 512 bytes), fast response times (< 100ms), simple domain names, mostly A and AAAA record types. Any deviation from this pattern warrants investigation.

20 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 DNS analysis tutorial
red DNS Tunneling Attack Detection
DNS Tunneling: Data Exfiltration via DNS

DNS tunneling encodes data inside DNS queries — allowing attackers to exfiltrate sensitive information or establish C2 communications even through firewalls that block all other outbound traffic. DNS port 53 is almost never blocked.

# Example DNS tunnel query: d2hhdGV2ZXJkYXRh.evil-c2-domain.com # The subdomain is base64-encoded stolen data: # d2hhdGV2ZXJkYXRh = base64('whateverdata')
Sign 1: Unusually long subdomains (normal = < 20 chars, tunneling = 50+ chars)
Sign 2: High volume of DNS queries — tunneling tools send hundreds per minute
Sign 3: High rate of TXT record queries — used for bidirectional tunnel communication
Sign 4: Queries to uncommon TLDs or newly registered domains
25 min · Download a DNS tunneling pcap and analyse
dark Detecting DNS Tunneling Detection Filters
DNS Tunnel Detection Filters
# Detect unusually long domain names (tunneling sign): dns.qry.name.len > 52
# High volume of TXT queries (bidirectional tunnel): dns.qry.type == 16 # TXT record type
# Failed lookups for random-looking domains (DGA/tunnel): dns.flags.rcode == 3 # NXDomain responses
# Statistics → DNS shows query type distribution: # Normal: mostly A/AAAA records # Tunneling: unusually high TXT or abnormal types

Tools that create DNS tunnels: iodine, dnscat2, dns2tcp. If you see base64-looking strings in subdomain labels combined with high query volume to a single domain — that is textbook DNS tunneling. Extract the subdomains, base64 decode them, and see the exfiltrated data.

20 min · Apply tunnel detection filters to a sample pcap
white DGA & Passive DNS Advanced DNS Analysis
DGA Domains & Passive DNS Intelligence

DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm): Malware generates hundreds of pseudo-random domain names daily. Only one is actually registered — the C2 server. All others fail (NXDomain). In Wireshark, DGA creates a flood of NXDomain responses.

# DGA detection in Wireshark: dns.flags.rcode == 3 # Filter NXDomain failures # Look for: many different random-looking domains # like: xk3mp2v.com, b8jqz9n.net, mq7rk9p.org # All from the same infected internal host

Passive DNS tracks historical domain→IP mappings over time. Tools like SecurityTrails, RiskIQ, and VirusTotal passive DNS reveal if a domain has changed IPs rapidly (fast-flux) — a C2 server evasion technique. Always check suspicious domains against passive DNS during an investigation.

20 min · Search a suspicious domain in VirusTotal passive DNS
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 46 Checklist
  • 1Apply `dns.qry.name.len > 52` to a tunneling pcap — count unusually long domain names
  • 2Identify NXDomain floods with `dns.flags.rcode == 3` — potential DGA malware
  • 3Use Statistics → DNS to see the query type distribution — spot abnormal TXT record volume
  • 4Download a DNS tunneling pcap from PacketLife.net or malware-traffic-analysis.net — identify the tunnel domain
  • 5Understand why blocking DNS to unauthorised servers at the firewall is a critical control
Total Day 46 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video SANS — DNS-Based Threat Detection (YouTube, free) youtube.com
🧪 PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net — DNS Attack PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net
🔍 Intel VirusTotal — Passive DNS and Domain Intelligence virustotal.com
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Wireshark: Traffic Analysis' Room tryhackme.com/room/wiresharktrafficanalysis
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 46
  • 1Download a DNS tunneling pcap from PacketLife.net or malware-traffic-analysis.net
  • 2Apply `dns.qry.name.len > 40` — list all unusually long domain names found
  • 3Count unique domains queried — compare the ratio of NXDomain vs successful lookups
  • 4Compare to a normal DNS capture from yesterday — document 3 specific differences
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 46
"DNS is the most overlooked protocol in security monitoring. Adding a simple DNS anomaly detection rule to your SIEM catches 30% of malware C2 traffic that every other control misses."
The most powerful DNS security control is also the simplest: block outbound DNS to any server except your authorised resolvers at the firewall. Force all DNS through your monitoring resolver. This single firewall rule eliminates DNS tunneling and ensures every DNS query is visible in your logs.
⚠ Common Mistake
Overlooking DNS during traffic analysis because 'it is just DNS.' DNS carries no payload data in normal use — which makes any anomaly in DNS volume, query types, or domain name length immediately suspicious. An attacker who can exfiltrate data via DNS needs no other network path. Monitor DNS like it is your most critical protocol.
DAY 47
HTTP/HTTPS Analysis & Web Attack Detection
Web traffic is 80% of enterprise network. Learn to read and analyse it.
#HTTP#WebAttacks#Day47of90
dark HTTP in Wireshark Foundation
HTTP Structure: Every Request Visible
SQL InjectionBrute ForceTLS Decrypt
# HTTP display filters: http # All HTTP traffic http.request # Requests only http.response # Responses only http.request.method == "POST" # Form submissions, logins http.request.method == "GET" # Page/resource requests http.host == "target.com" # Specific domain only

File extraction from pcap: Wireshark can extract files transferred over HTTP. File → Export Objects → HTTP. This extracts images, documents, executables, and scripts downloaded by the victim — critical for malware analysis.

HTTP request structure visible in details panel: Method, URI, HTTP version, all headers (User-Agent, Host, Cookie, Authorization), and the request body (form data, JSON payloads).

20 min · Wireshark University · Guru99 Wireshark HTTP guide
red Web Attack Detection Attack Patterns
Web Attacks: Visible in HTTP Traffic
# SQL Injection attempts: http.request.uri contains "SELECT" http.request.uri contains "%27" # URL-encoded quote (') http.request.uri contains "UNION%20SELECT"
# Directory brute force (mass 404s): http.response.code == 404 # Combined with high request rate from single IP
# Path traversal (LFI) attempts: http.request.uri contains "../" http.request.uri contains "etc/passwd"
# Credential submission in cleartext: http.request.method == "POST" # Follow TCP Stream → search for 'password=' in body
# Command injection in parameters: http.request.uri contains ";" http.request.uri contains "|"
25 min · Apply each filter against a web attack pcap
dark HTTPS Decryption Encrypted Traffic
TLS Decryption: Decrypt Your Own HTTPS

SSLKEYLOGFILE technique: Modern browsers can log TLS session keys to a file as connections are made. Wireshark reads this file to decrypt the traffic — revealing the plaintext even though it was encrypted on the wire.

# Step 1: Set environment variable before opening browser: export SSLKEYLOGFILE=/tmp/sslkeys.log # Linux/Mac # Windows: System Properties → Environment Variables
# Step 2: Open browser AFTER setting the variable # Browse to target sites — keys are logged automatically # Step 3: In Wireshark: # Edit → Preferences → Protocols → TLS # → (Pre)-Master-Secret log filename: /tmp/sslkeys.log

Use cases: Debugging your own web applications, malware analysis in a controlled lab environment, understanding what your own browser sends in HTTPS requests. Only decrypt traffic you own or have explicit permission to decrypt.

25 min · Decrypt your own browser HTTPS traffic
white HTTP Export Objects Evidence Extraction
Export Objects: Extract Files from PCAPs

File → Export Objects → HTTP extracts all files transferred over HTTP in the capture. This is one of the most important forensic techniques for malware analysis — you can recover the exact executable or document that infected a victim.

1Open a malware pcap → File → Export Objects → HTTP → you see a list of all downloaded files
2Note the filenames, content types, and sizes — executables (.exe, .dll) and scripts (.vbs, .ps1) are immediately suspicious
3Save suspicious files → upload their SHA256 hash to VirusTotal → confirm malware family
4The file you extracted IS the malware that infected the victim — you now have your primary forensic artefact
20 min · Extract and hash files from a malware pcap
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 47 Checklist
  • 1Apply SQL injection detection filters — identify any URI containing SELECT, UNION, or URL-encoded quotes
  • 2Filter for mass 404 responses from a single IP — confirm directory brute-force activity
  • 3Use File → Export Objects → HTTP to extract files from a malware pcap — hash and check on VirusTotal
  • 4Set SSLKEYLOGFILE and decrypt your own browser HTTPS traffic — see what github.com sends in plaintext
  • 5Follow a POST request TCP stream — identify any credentials or sensitive data in the request body
Total Day 47 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Reference Wireshark HTTP Wiki — HTTP Analysis Guide wiki.wireshark.org/HTTP
🏋️ Labs PortSwigger Web Academy — Free HTTP Security Labs portswigger.net/web-security/learning-paths
🧪 PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net — HTTP Malware PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net
📖 Guide OWASP Web Security Testing Guide — Traffic Analysis owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 47
  • 1Set SSLKEYLOGFILE environment variable — open Firefox — browse to github.com — capture in Wireshark
  • 2Load the key log file in Wireshark TLS settings — confirm you can now read decrypted HTTPS
  • 3Download a web attack pcap — apply SQL injection filter — document all injection attempts found
  • 4Extract HTTP objects from a malware pcap — hash the suspicious files — check on VirusTotal
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 47
"The SSLKEYLOGFILE technique is used by analysts to investigate encrypted malware C2 traffic in controlled lab environments. Understanding it also means understanding how enterprise SSL inspection appliances work."
Mass 404 errors from a single source IP in a short time window is one of the clearest signatures of directory brute-forcing. Rate threshold: anything above 50 requests per minute from a single IP to a single server generating mostly 404s should trigger an alert. Document this threshold when you write Suricata rules in Day 49.
⚠ Common Mistake
Only decrypting your own traffic. The SSLKEYLOGFILE method requires access to the browser generating the traffic — which means it only works for traffic YOU generate or traffic from a controlled lab environment. In real incident response, you cannot retroactively decrypt enterprise HTTPS traffic without a network SSL inspection appliance that was capturing keys at the time.
DAY 48
tcpdump: Command-Line Packet Analysis
No GUI? No problem. tcpdump is the analyst's terminal sniffer.
#tcpdump#TrafficAnalysis#Day48of90
dark tcpdump Basics Foundation
tcpdump: CLI Packet Capture
BPF FiltersOne-Linerspcap workflow
# Basic capture on interface: sudo tcpdump -i eth0
# Essential flags: tcpdump -i eth0 # Specific interface tcpdump -n # Don't resolve IPs to hostnames (faster) tcpdump -v # Verbose — more packet detail tcpdump -vv # Very verbose — full packet decode tcpdump -c 100 # Capture only 100 packets then stop
# Write to file (for Wireshark analysis later): sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/capture.pcap # Read from file: tcpdump -r /tmp/capture.pcap

Pre-installed on: every Linux and macOS system. No installation required. This is why it is the answer when interviewers ask 'how would you capture traffic on a remote server with no GUI?'

20 min · danielmiessler.com/study/tcpdump — definitive guide
red Essential tcpdump Filters BPF Syntax
tcpdump Filters: BPF Syntax Mastery
# Filter by port: tcpdump port 80 # HTTP tcpdump port 443 # HTTPS tcpdump port 22 # SSH
# Filter by host: tcpdump host 192.168.1.100 # To/from specific IP tcpdump src 192.168.1.100 # From this IP only tcpdump dst 8.8.8.8 # To this IP only
# Filter by protocol: tcpdump tcp # TCP only tcpdump udp # UDP only tcpdump arp # ARP only tcpdump icmp # ICMP/ping only
# Combine filters with boolean operators: tcpdump -i eth0 port 443 and host 8.8.8.8 tcpdump -i any 'tcp dst port 22 and not src 192.168.1.1'
25 min · Practice each filter combination in your terminal
dark Security Monitoring One-Liners SOC Techniques
Security One-Liners: Real SOC Commands
# Monitor SSH brute-force in real-time: sudo tcpdump -i any -n \ 'tcp dst port 22 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0'
# Watch all DNS queries live: sudo tcpdump -i any -n port 53
# Monitor ARP activity (detect ARP spoofing): sudo tcpdump -i eth0 arp
# Capture HTTP traffic with full packet content: sudo tcpdump -i any -A port 80 # -A prints packet in ASCII — reveals cleartext data
# Capture TCP SYN packets only (new connections): sudo tcpdump -i any \ 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-ack == 0'
25 min · Run each one-liner on your Linux system
white tcpdump → Wireshark Workflow Enterprise Workflow
Remote Capture Workflow: The Enterprise Standard
1SSH into remote server where the traffic is occurring — a web server, a compromised host, or a network chokepoint.
2Start tcpdump capture: `sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/incident_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M).pcap -c 10000`
3Copy pcap to your local machine: `scp user@server:/tmp/incident_*.pcap ~/investigations/`
4Open in Wireshark on your local machine with full GUI capabilities — apply all filters, follow streams, export objects. This is how 90% of enterprise incident response traffic analysis is performed.
20 min · Practise the full remote capture → local analysis workflow
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 48 Checklist
  • 1Run `sudo tcpdump -i any -n -w /tmp/test.pcap -c 200` — capture 200 packets and open in Wireshark
  • 2Use `tcpdump port 53 -n` to watch DNS queries live for 30 seconds — document what you see
  • 3Practice the tcpdump → SCP → Wireshark workflow between two VMs in your lab
  • 4Know the interview answer for 'how do you capture traffic on a headless server?'
  • 5Understand when to use tcpdump vs Wireshark — different tools for different contexts
Total Day 48 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Guide Daniel Miessler — tcpdump Primer (definitive guide) danielmiessler.com/study/tcpdump
📄 Reference SANS DFIR — tcpdump Cheat Sheet (free PDF) sans.org/posters
📖 Man Page tcpdump Man Page — Complete Flag Reference man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/tcpdump.1.html
🧪 Lab LabEx — Linux Networking Labs with tcpdump labex.io/free-labs/linux
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 48
  • 1Run `sudo tcpdump -i any -n -w /tmp/test.pcap -c 200` — wait 30 seconds — open in Wireshark
  • 2Watch live DNS: `sudo tcpdump -i any port 53 -n` — browse 3 websites — note every domain queried
  • 3Practice `tcpdump -i any -A port 80` — visit an HTTP-only site — see the cleartext request in terminal
  • 4Write your interview answer: 'How would you capture traffic on a remote Linux server with no GUI?'
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 48
"Many hiring managers ask 'how would you capture traffic on a server with no GUI?' The answer is: tcpdump to capture, SCP to transfer, Wireshark to analyse. A practised, specific answer wins interviews."
Always document the exact tcpdump command you ran during an incident — including interface, filter, output file, and capture count. This documentation becomes part of the chain of custody in any investigation with legal implications. The command, timestamp, and who ran it must all be recorded.
⚠ Common Mistake
Running tcpdump without the `-n` flag on production systems. Without `-n`, tcpdump performs reverse DNS lookups for every IP — generating massive DNS traffic that can overwhelm DNS servers, alter the packet timing in your capture, and appear suspicious in your own logs. Always use `-n` on production captures unless hostname resolution is specifically needed.
DAY 49
Suricata IDS/IPS Setup & Rules
Automate threat detection. Suricata watches your network 24/7.
#Suricata#IDS#Day49of90
dark What is Suricata? Foundation
Suricata: Free Enterprise IDS/IPS
IDS RulesEVE JSONET Rules

Suricata is a free, open-source, high-performance Intrusion Detection System (IDS), Intrusion Prevention System (IPS), and Network Security Monitor (NSM). It inspects all network packets in real-time against a library of threat signatures.

Enterprise-grade: Processes 10Gbps+ traffic on modern hardware. Used in commercial SOC environments, cloud security platforms, and network security appliances worldwide. The same engine that powers pfSense IDS.

Three operating modes: IDS (alert only, out-of-band), IPS (inline, blocks traffic), NSM (network security monitor, full logging). Most home labs start with IDS mode — alerting without blocking.

20 min · youtube.com — Suricata IDS/IPS Tutorial
red Installation & Setup Setup
Suricata Installation and Configuration
# Install Suricata: sudo apt install suricata # Update to Emerging Threats ruleset: sudo suricata-update # Test configuration: suricata -T -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml # Output should end with: 'Configuration provided was good.'
# Start Suricata on live interface: sudo systemctl start suricata sudo systemctl enable suricata # Start on boot # Monitor live alerts: sudo tail -f /var/log/suricata/fast.log
# Run against existing pcap file: sudo suricata -r malware.pcap \ -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml \ -l /tmp/suricata-logs/ # Then check: cat /tmp/suricata-logs/fast.log
25 min · Install and run against a malware pcap
dark Suricata Rule Anatomy Rule Writing
Rule Anatomy: Every Field Explained
# Complete Suricata rule: alert tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 22 ( msg:"SSH Brute Force Attempt"; flow:established; threshold:type both, track by_src, count 5, seconds 60; classtype:attempted-admin; sid:9001; rev:1; )
alert — action (alert/drop/reject/pass). IDS mode uses alert only.
tcp any any → $HOME_NET 22 — protocol, source IP, source port, direction, dest IP, dest port
msg: — human-readable alert name shown in logs
threshold: — prevents alert storm — only fire after 5 attempts in 60 seconds
sid: — unique rule identifier. Custom rules use sid > 9000000
25 min · Write 3 custom rules for common attack patterns
white Emerging Threats Rules Rule Management
Emerging Threats: 30,000+ Free Rules

Emerging Threats (ET) by Proofpoint provides free, daily-updated Suricata rules covering known malware families, exploit kits, C2 communication, and attack techniques. Over 30,000 rules updated every 24 hours.

# Update ET rules automatically: sudo suricata-update # List available rule sources: sudo suricata-update list-sources # Enable additional sources: sudo suricata-update enable-source et/open sudo suricata-update enable-source ptresearch/attackdetection

EVE JSON log format is what SIEMs ingest. Every Suricata alert is logged to `/var/log/suricata/eve.json` as structured JSON — timestamp, alert signature, source/destination IPs and ports, protocol, packet payload. Feed this directly into Elasticsearch or Splunk for enterprise detection.

20 min · Run suricata-update and review rule categories
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 49 Checklist
  • 1Install Suricata and run `suricata -T` to validate your configuration
  • 2Run `sudo suricata-update` and count how many rules were downloaded
  • 3Run Suricata against a malware pcap — count alerts in fast.log — identify top rule names
  • 4Write one custom rule — detect SSH brute force (5+ connections from same IP in 60 seconds)
  • 5Understand EVE JSON format — open eve.json and identify the key fields for SIEM integration
Total Day 49 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Official Suricata — Official Documentation suricata.io/docs
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Suricata' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/surricata
📦 Rules Emerging Threats — Free Suricata Rules proofpoint.com/us/products/et-intelligence
📺 Video YouTube — Suricata IDS/IPS Complete Tutorial youtube.com/watch?v=S0-vsjhPDN0
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 49
  • 1Install Suricata: `sudo apt install suricata` → run `suricata-update` → test with `-T` flag
  • 2Run against a malware pcap: `sudo suricata -r malware.pcap -l /tmp/logs/` — check fast.log
  • 3Count alerts: `wc -l /tmp/logs/fast.log` — identify the top 3 rule names that fired
  • 4Write a custom SSH brute-force rule — test it by replaying a brute-force pcap
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 49
"Writing custom Suricata rules is a valued, rare skill — it is how SOC teams tune detection to their specific environment. Start by modifying existing rules before writing from scratch."
The most important Suricata tuning skill is the threshold option. Without it, a single attacker sending 10,000 SYN packets generates 10,000 alerts — burying analysts in noise. Threshold-based rules (fire only after N events in X seconds) is what makes IDS practical in real environments. Never deploy a detection rule without considering its alert volume.
⚠ Common Mistake
Enabling too many rules without tuning. A default Suricata deployment with all ET rules enabled will fire thousands of alerts per hour in any real network — most of them false positives. Start with a minimal ruleset, tune for your network baseline, then add rules incrementally. Alert fatigue is the most common reason IDS deployments fail in enterprise.
DAY 50
Analysing Malware Traffic Patterns
Day 50 of 90! Halfway there. Let us analyse real malware network traffic.
#MalwareAnalysis#TrafficAnalysis#Day50of90
dark C2 Traffic Patterns Foundation
C2 Traffic: How Malware Phones Home
BeaconingDGALateral Movement

Command & Control (C2) traffic is how malware communicates with its operator after infecting a victim. Identifying C2 traffic is the core skill of malware traffic analysis — find the C2 and you have identified the threat.

Periodic beaconing: C2 malware checks in every N seconds. Regular, clockwork-like connection intervals — not human browsing patterns. Statistics → I/O Graph reveals regular spikes.
Unusual User-Agents: Malware often sends fake User-Agent strings. Common: `Mozilla/4.0 (compatible)`, `curl/7.68.0`, random strings, or empty UA fields. Filter: `http.user_agent`.
Non-standard ports: HTTP traffic on port 8080, 8443, or completely random ports. HTTPS on non-443 ports.
Encoded/encrypted payloads: Base64 in HTTP bodies, random-looking binary data in TCP streams.
25 min · malware-traffic-analysis.net — free practice pcaps
red Beaconing Detection Detection Technique
Beaconing: Find the Heartbeat

Beaconing detection in Wireshark: Statistics → I/O Graph. Regular spikes at fixed intervals (every 30s, 60s, 5min) are the hallmark of C2 malware beaconing. Human browsing is irregular — malware is clockwork-precise.

# Wireshark filters for beaconing investigation: ip.addr == suspected_c2_ip # All traffic to C2 http.user_agent # Check all UA strings tcp.stream eq N # Follow specific stream

Statistics → Conversations for beaconing: Look for an internal host with dozens or hundreds of short connections to a single external IP, all at regular time intervals. The external IP is your C2 server.

Beacon jitter: Sophisticated malware adds random jitter to beacon intervals to evade exact-interval detection. Instead of every 60 seconds, it beacons every 57–63 seconds. Look for near-regular intervals, not just exact ones.

25 min · Identify beaconing in a malware pcap
dark DGA Domains Advanced Detection
DGA: Random Domains, Real Malware

Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA) create hundreds of pseudo-random domain names daily. The malware author registers only one — the rest generate NXDomain failures. This makes C2 infrastructure extremely resilient — block one domain and the malware tries the next 200.

# DGA detection in Wireshark: dns.flags.rcode == 3 # Filter NXDomain failures # Signs: # - Many different random-looking domains from same host # - Like: xk3mp2v.com, b8jqz9n.net, mq7rk9p.org # - All from ONE infected internal IP # - Eventually ONE domain resolves successfully = C2

DGA families: CryptoLocker, Conficker, Zeus, Dridex all use DGA. Threat intelligence feeds contain known DGA patterns. When you see a flood of NXDomain responses from a single internal host — you have found your patient zero.

20 min · Identify DGA activity in a malware pcap
white Lateral Movement Post-Compromise Activity
Lateral Movement: Inside the Network

After initial compromise, malware spreads internally. Lateral movement in Wireshark involves unusual internal-to-internal traffic patterns that do not match normal business communication.

# Lateral movement indicators: smb # SMB traffic between internal hosts tcp.port == 445 # SMB (EternalBlue territory) tcp.port == 5985 # WinRM (PowerShell remoting) tcp.port == 3389 # RDP (remote desktop) tcp.port == 135 # WMI (Windows management)

Key question: Have these two internal hosts ever communicated before? A workstation connecting to 50 other internal servers via SMB within 10 minutes = WannaCry-style worm propagation. Normal workstations only talk to a few servers. Unusual internal traffic volumes = immediate investigation priority.

20 min · Identify lateral movement in a malware pcap
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 50 Checklist — Halfway There!
  • 1Download 3 malware pcaps from malware-traffic-analysis.net — identify the C2 domain in each
  • 2Use Statistics → I/O Graph to spot beaconing — identify the beacon interval
  • 3Filter `dns.flags.rcode == 3` — count NXDomain failures — identify potential DGA activity
  • 4Filter SMB and RDP traffic — identify any internal lateral movement patterns
  • 5Write a Suricata rule that would detect the beaconing pattern you identified
Total Day 50 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes (milestone day)
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🧪 PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net — Brad Duncan's Gold Standard malware-traffic-analysis.net
🔬 Sandbox Any.run — Online Malware Sandbox with PCAP Downloads any.run
📚 Course SANS Malware Traffic Analysis — Free Online Course youtube.com
🔍 Intel MalwareBazaar — Malware Samples and Traffic Intel bazaar.abuse.ch
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 50
  • 1Download 3 exercise pcaps from malware-traffic-analysis.net — attempt them before reading the answer
  • 2Identify for each: C2 domain, malware family name (check the site's answer after your attempt)
  • 3Write what Suricata rule would detect the C2 communication in each pcap
  • 4Document your analysis using the 5-step investigation framework from Day 52 preview
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 50
"Brad Duncan's malware-traffic-analysis.net exercises come with answer keys. Do the exercise first, then check answers. This is the single best free resource for real-world malware pcap analysis training."
Day 50 is the halfway point. Pause and compare your skill level today against Day 1. You now read network packets, write detection rules, script automation, and analyse malware traffic. None of that existed 50 days ago. The second half builds on everything in the first half — the learning compounds from here.
⚠ Common Mistake
Running malware samples outside of an isolated virtual machine. Malware pcaps are safe — they are just network captures, not executables. But actual malware samples from Any.run or MalwareBazaar must ONLY be handled in a completely isolated lab VM with no network access to your host or real network. Snapshot before, restore after, every single time.
DAY 51
Zeek (Bro) Network Security Monitor
Zeek turns raw packets into structured security logs. Enterprise-level monitoring.
#Zeek#NSM#Day51of90
dark What is Zeek? Foundation
Zeek: Packets In, Structured Logs Out
conn.loghttp.logZeek scripts

Zeek (formerly Bro) is a powerful Network Security Monitor that processes raw packets and generates high-level, structured log files automatically. Rather than alerting on specific signatures, Zeek creates a complete audit trail of all network activity.

The key difference from Suricata: Suricata fires alerts on known bad patterns. Zeek creates comprehensive logs of ALL activity — letting analysts hunt for unknown threats that no signature covers yet.

Zeek automatically generates: conn.log (every connection), dns.log (all DNS), http.log (all URLs), ssl.log (all TLS certificates), files.log (all file transfers), weird.log (protocol anomalies) — without any configuration.

20 min · zeek.org/documentation
red Installation & Running Zeek Setup
Zeek Setup: Analyse Any PCAP
# Install Zeek: sudo apt install zeek # Verify installation: zeek --version
# Analyse a pcap file (no live capture needed): zeek -r malware.pcap # Generates log files in current directory: # conn.log dns.log http.log ssl.log files.log weird.log
# Run on live interface (requires root): sudo zeekctl deploy # Uses /etc/zeek/node.cfg config # Or direct interface capture: sudo zeek -i eth0
# View logs immediately: cat conn.log | head -5 cat dns.log | head -5 cat http.log | head -5
25 min · Run Zeek against a malware pcap now
dark Key Log Files Log Analysis
Zeek Logs: Complete Network Audit Trail
# conn.log — every TCP/UDP connection: # Fields: ts, uid, id.orig_h, id.resp_h, id.resp_p, # proto, duration, orig_bytes, resp_bytes # Top talkers by bytes transferred: cat conn.log | zeek-cut id.orig_h id.resp_h resp_bytes \ | sort -k3 -rn | head -10
# http.log — every HTTP request: # Fields: ts, uid, id.orig_h, host, uri, method, # status_code, user_agent, resp_mime_types # Extract all URLs visited: cat http.log | zeek-cut host uri | head -20
# dns.log — every DNS query: # Extract unique domains queried: cat dns.log | zeek-cut query qtype_name answers \ | sort | uniq | head -20
# weird.log — protocol anomalies: # If this log has content = investigate immediately
25 min · Analyse all log files from your malware pcap
white Zeek vs Wireshark Tool Comparison
Zeek vs Wireshark: Different Jobs

Wireshark: Packet-level detail. GUI-based. Best for deep protocol analysis, following individual streams, decrypting traffic, and extracting specific artefacts. Difficult to scale to large captures.

Zeek: Session-level summary logs. CLI-based. Best for large-scale traffic analysis, hunting across millions of events, long-term logging, and integration with SIEMs. Cannot show individual packet bytes.

Professional workflow: Zeek for initial triage and hunting → Wireshark for deep-dive investigation of specific sessions. A Zeek conn.log entry includes a `uid` field — use it with `zeek-cut` to extract the specific packets for Wireshark analysis.

Zeek + Elastic Stack = open-source SIEM. Many organisations run this combination instead of paid SIEM solutions — saving tens of thousands annually while maintaining enterprise-grade detection capability.

15 min · Compare Zeek and Wireshark output on the same pcap
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 51 Checklist
  • 1Install Zeek and run `zeek -r malware.pcap` — list all log files generated
  • 2Use `zeek-cut` to extract top talkers from conn.log by bytes transferred
  • 3Extract all unique DNS queries from dns.log — compare to the Wireshark DNS filter from Day 46
  • 4Check weird.log — if it has any entries, investigate every single one
  • 5Compare investigation speed: Zeek vs Wireshark for the same malware pcap — which was faster?
Total Day 51 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Official Zeek — Official Documentation zeek.org/documentation
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Zeek' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/zeek
📦 Packages Zeek Package Manager — Community Scripts packages.zeek.org
📺 Webcast SANS — Zeek for the Security Analyst (free, YouTube) youtube.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 51
  • 1Run `zeek -r malware.pcap` in your lab — list all generated log files with `ls -la`
  • 2Extract top 10 destination IPs from conn.log by connection count — identify the C2 server
  • 3Extract all unique domains from dns.log — compare to what you found in Wireshark
  • 4Check weird.log and files.log — document any suspicious findings in each
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 51
"Zeek + Elastic Stack is a powerful open-source SIEM substitute. Many organisations run this stack instead of paid SIEMs. Knowing Zeek makes you valuable in any company with an open-source security stack."
The `weird.log` is Zeek's anomaly detector. It logs any protocol behaviour that deviates from the RFC specification — unexpected flag combinations, malformed packets, protocol version mismatches. Security professionals treat every entry in weird.log as a priority investigation item. An empty weird.log means nothing unusual happened; a populated one means something did.
⚠ Common Mistake
Trying to read Zeek's tab-separated log files directly in a text editor for large captures. Zeek logs for a busy 1-hour capture can be millions of lines. Use `zeek-cut` to extract specific fields, pipe to `sort | uniq -c | sort -rn` for frequency analysis, and use `awk` for filtering. The Linux text processing tools from Phase 3 apply directly here.
DAY 52
Network Forensics Investigation Workflow
A breach happened. The pcap is your crime scene. Here is the full investigation workflow.
#NetworkForensics#DFIR#Day52of90
dark Investigation Framework Foundation
Investigation Framework: 5 Steps
5-Step ProcessIOC ExtractionIncident Report

Every professional network forensics investigation follows a consistent framework regardless of the incident type. Skipping steps leads to missed evidence, incorrect conclusions, and incomplete reports.

Step 1 — Define scope: What timeframe? Which systems? Which network segments? Narrow scope = faster analysis and more precise findings.
Step 2 — Profile normal traffic: What does baseline look like? Protocol distribution, top talkers, connection patterns. Anomalies only exist relative to normal.
Step 3 — Identify anomalies: Deviations from baseline. Unknown external IPs, unusual protocols, unexpected ports, traffic spikes.
Step 4 — Reconstruct attack timeline: First contact → exploitation → C2 → lateral movement → exfiltration. Minute-by-minute narrative.
Step 5 — Extract IOCs: All C2 IPs, domains, file hashes, URLs. Feed into threat intel for blocking and hunting.
25 min · Wireshark University · SANS Network Forensics
red Initial Triage Step 1 & 2
Initial Triage: 2 Minutes of Statistics

Before applying any filters, spend exactly 2 minutes on statistics. This gives you a complete traffic overview and prevents the tunnel-vision of jumping straight to filtering.

1Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy: What protocols are present? Any unusual ones (Tor, IRC, unusual tunnels)? What percentage is each protocol?
2Statistics → Conversations: Top IP pairs by bytes. Any internal→external connections with massive data transfer? Potential exfiltration?
3Statistics → Endpoints: All unique IPs. Any internal IPs you do not recognise? New hosts that appeared post-compromise?
4Statistics → I/O Graph: Traffic spikes at specific times. Identify the exact moment the attack began — visible as a traffic pattern change.
20 min · Run full triage on 3 malware pcaps
dark IOC Extraction Evidence Collection
IOC Extraction: Document the Evidence
# In Wireshark — export HTTP objects (malware downloads): File → Export Objects → HTTP # Hash all extracted files with sha256sum # Check hashes against VirusTotal
# Extract unique external IPs from Zeek conn.log: cat conn.log | zeek-cut id.resp_h \ | sort | uniq > external_ips.txt # Check each against VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, Shodan
# Extract all DNS queries from Zeek dns.log: cat dns.log | zeek-cut query \ | sort | uniq > domains_queried.txt # Check against VirusTotal domain intel

Final IOC list format: {type: 'ip', value: '45.33.32.156', source: 'conn.log', confidence: 'high'} — structured for import into MISP, OpenCTI, or your SIEM blocklist.

25 min · Extract and enrich IOCs from a malware pcap
white Incident Report Writing Communication
Incident Report: Communicate Your Findings

DFIR report structure — same format used by every major incident response firm:

Executive Summary: 3–5 sentences. What happened, when, what was impacted, what was done. Non-technical management reads ONLY this.
Timeline of Events: Minute-by-minute attack narrative from first packet to last. Shows the attack progression.
Technical Analysis: Detailed findings with evidence — Wireshark screenshots, Zeek log excerpts, file hashes, PCAP references.
IOC List: All IPs, domains, file hashes, URLs, email addresses identified. Table format for easy import into security tools.
Recommendations: 3–5 specific, actionable remediation steps with priority levels.
25 min · Write a report for your Day 50 malware pcap analysis
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 52 Checklist
  • 1Apply the 5-step investigation framework to a malware pcap — document each step
  • 2Run all 4 Statistics views in the first 2 minutes before any filtering — record findings
  • 3Extract all IOCs — unique external IPs, domains, file hashes from a malware pcap
  • 4Check extracted IOCs against VirusTotal and AbuseIPDB — enrich with threat intelligence
  • 5Write a 1-page incident report with all 5 sections — this is a direct career skill builder
Total Day 52 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🔧 Tool NetworkMiner — Free Network Forensics Tool netresec.com/?page=NetworkMiner
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Network Forensics' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/networkforensics
📺 Videos SANS DFIR Summit — Free Recordings (YouTube) youtube.com
🧪 PCAPs malware-traffic-analysis.net — With Answer Keys malware-traffic-analysis.net
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 52
  • 1Select a malware pcap from malware-traffic-analysis.net — one you have not analysed before
  • 2Apply the 5-step framework — spend exactly 2 minutes on statistics before any filtering
  • 3Extract all IOCs — create a structured list with type, value, and confidence level
  • 4Write a 1-page incident report with all 5 sections — executive summary through recommendations
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 52
"Practise writing incident reports. The ability to clearly communicate technical findings to non-technical management is what separates $60k from $90k+ security analysts. Technical skill alone is not enough."
The Executive Summary is the most important section of any incident report — and the hardest to write. A CTO or CEO will read only those 5 sentences and make resource allocation decisions based on them. Write it last, after you understand the full picture. Every word must earn its place: what happened, who was affected, what was done, what remains.
⚠ Common Mistake
Writing technical incident reports that only other security analysts can understand. Real incident reports serve multiple audiences: the executive summary for leadership, the technical analysis for your peers, the IOC list for security tools. Each section must be written for its specific audience. A report that only technical people can understand fails its primary purpose.
DAY 53
Python + Wireshark: Parsing PCAPs
Python meets Wireshark. Automate pcap analysis with Scapy and PyShark.
#Python#PCAP#Day53of90
dark Reading PCAPs with Scapy Foundation
Scapy: Read Any PCAP Programmatically
rdpcapPySharkAuto-Analyser
from scapy.all import rdpcap, IP, TCP, DNS, DNSQR # Load entire pcap into memory: packets = rdpcap('malware_capture.pcap') print(f'Total packets: {len(packets)}') # Print summary of each packet: for pkt in packets: print(pkt.summary())
# Filter for specific layer types: tcp_packets = [p for p in packets if p.haslayer(TCP)] dns_packets = [p for p in packets if p.haslayer(DNS)] print(f'TCP packets: {len(tcp_packets)}') print(f'DNS packets: {len(dns_packets)}')
# Extract DNS queries (what domains were queried?): for pkt in dns_packets: if pkt.haslayer(DNSQR): # DNS query record domain = pkt[DNSQR].qname.decode('utf-8') print(f'DNS Query: {domain}')
25 min · scapy.net docs · realpython.com Scapy guide
red PyShark for App-Layer Analysis Advanced Parsing
PyShark: Wireshark Dissectors in Python
import pyshark # pip install pyshark (requires Wireshark/tshark installed) # Load pcap with display filter: cap = pyshark.FileCapture( 'malware.pcap', display_filter='http' ) for pkt in cap: try: src = pkt.ip.src host = pkt.http.host uri = pkt.http.request_uri ua = pkt.http.user_agent print(f'{src} → {host}{uri} [{ua}]') except AttributeError: pass # Not all HTTP packets have all fields

PyShark vs Scapy: PyShark uses Wireshark's dissectors — it understands every protocol Wireshark understands. Scapy is faster for raw packet manipulation. PyShark is better for application-layer analysis where you need fields like `http.user_agent` or `dns.qry.name`.

25 min · Build the PyShark HTTP analyser
dark Automated PCAP Analyser Automation Project
Auto-Analyser: 30-Line Triage Script
import pyshark from collections import Counter blocklist = ['evil.com', 'malware.ru', 'c2-server.net'] def analyse_pcap(pcap_file): cap = pyshark.FileCapture(pcap_file, display_filter='dns') dns_queries = [] for pkt in cap: try: domain = pkt.dns.qry_name dns_queries.append(domain) except AttributeError: pass # Count and find suspicious domains: counter = Counter(dns_queries) print('\nTop 10 Queried Domains:') for domain, count in counter.most_common(10): flag = ' ⚠️ BLOCKLISTED' if domain in blocklist else '' print(f' {domain}: {count}{flag}')
30 min · Build and test the auto-analyser against a malware pcap
white IOC Auto-Extraction IOC Pipeline
IOC Extraction: Automated Pipeline
import pyshark, json from datetime import datetime def extract_iocs(pcap_file): iocs = {'ips': set(), 'domains': set(), 'urls': set(), 'user_agents': set()} # Extract IPs from all packets: for pkt in pyshark.FileCapture(pcap_file): try: iocs['ips'].add(pkt.ip.dst) except: pass # Extract DNS domains: for pkt in pyshark.FileCapture(pcap_file, display_filter='dns'): try: iocs['domains'].add(pkt.dns.qry_name) except: pass # Convert sets to sorted lists for JSON export: output = {k: sorted(v) for k, v in iocs.items()} output['timestamp'] = datetime.now().isoformat() with open('iocs.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(output, f, indent=2) return output
20 min · Run IOC extraction and feed results into VirusTotal API
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 53 Checklist
  • 1Use Scapy rdpcap() to load a pcap and count total packets by protocol type
  • 2Use PyShark to extract all HTTP host headers and URIs from a malware pcap
  • 3Build the auto-analyser — DNS query counter with blocklist matching
  • 4Build the IOC extraction pipeline — output all IPs, domains, URLs to a JSON file
  • 5Feed extracted IOCs into your VirusTotal API script from Day 39 — automate enrichment
Total Day 53 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Official Scapy Documentation — rdpcap and Packet Analysis scapy.net/doc
📦 Library PyShark — Python Wrapper for Wireshark (GitHub) github.com/KimiNewt/pyshark
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Python for Pentesters' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/pythonforcybersecurity
📖 Guide Real Python — Scapy for Python Guide realpython.com/python-scapy
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 53
  • 1Load a malware pcap with Scapy rdpcap() — count packets per protocol type
  • 2Build the PyShark analyser — extract all DNS queries and HTTP user agents
  • 3Check each domain against your 5-entry blocklist — print ALERT for matches
  • 4Export all IOCs to iocs.json — feed to VirusTotal API from Day 39 for enrichment
Hands-On Task: 60 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 53
"Combining Python with pcap analysis means automated malware traffic analysis. This is what SIEM products like Splunk do internally. Understanding the foundation makes you a better security engineer at every level."
The combination of Zeek logs + Python analysis is extremely powerful. Zeek processes the raw pcap into structured TSV logs. Python reads those logs with pandas, applies analysis logic, enriches with threat intel APIs, and generates reports. This pipeline processes millions of packets in seconds — no GUI required, fully automatable, infinitely scalable.
⚠ Common Mistake
Processing large pcap files by loading them entirely into RAM with rdpcap(). A 1GB pcap will consume several GB of RAM when loaded into Scapy. For large files, use Scapy's PcapReader as an iterator: `with PcapReader('large.pcap') as reader: for pkt in reader:` — this processes one packet at a time and never loads the full file into memory.
DAY 54
Security Onion: Open Source NDR Platform
Security Onion is a free enterprise network monitoring platform. Install it today.
#SecurityOnion#NDR#Day54of90
dark What is Security Onion? Foundation
Security Onion: Free Enterprise SIEM
Zeek + SuricataElasticsearchATT&CK

Security Onion is a free, open-source Linux distribution for threat hunting, enterprise security monitoring, and log management. It bundles a complete security operations stack into a single ISO — eliminating the need to configure each tool individually.

Detection: Suricata IDS — alerts on known attack signatures with ATT&CK mapping
Traffic Analysis: Zeek NSM — structured logs for every network event
Storage & Search: Elasticsearch + Kibana — search billions of events instantly
Forensics: CyberChef built-in — decode, decrypt, analyse artefacts in browser
Case Management: Built-in ticketing for tracking incidents

Cost: $0. This exact stack is used in real enterprise SOC environments. 'Security Onion' on a resume is an immediate signal of operational experience.

20 min · securityonionsolutions.com/docs
red Setup & Installation Installation
Security Onion Setup: Home Lab
1Download ISO from securityonionsolutions.com — Security Onion 2.4 (latest). File size approximately 6GB.
2Create VM: Minimum 4GB RAM (8GB recommended), 80GB disk, 2 CPUs. Add two NICs: one for management (NAT), one for monitoring (Host-Only or promiscuous mode).
3Boot from ISO → follow setup wizard → choose 'Standalone' mode for home lab → set admin credentials → wait approximately 45 minutes for full installation.
4Access web UI at https://localhost (or VM IP) → login with credentials set during install → you are now looking at a full enterprise SOC platform.
45–60 min · securityonionsolutions.com — download and install
dark Alerts & Hunt Module Core Features
Alerts & Hunt: Point-and-Click Investigation

Alerts view: Shows all Suricata detections with severity levels, source/destination IPs, ATT&CK technique mappings, and one-click pivot to the raw packet that triggered the alert. Every alert is a case you can track.

Hunt module: Query all data by IP, domain, hash, or any field across Zeek and Suricata logs simultaneously. No SIEM query language required — point-and-click pivoting between related evidence. Find all connections to a suspicious IP, all DNS queries for a domain, all files with a hash.

PCAP import: Security Onion supports direct pcap import through the interface. Upload any malware pcap → it processes through Zeek and Suricata automatically → all findings appear in Alerts and Hunt within minutes.

20 min · Import a malware pcap and explore the alerts
white MITRE ATT&CK Integration ATT&CK Mapping
ATT&CK Integration: Know Your Coverage

Every Suricata alert in Security Onion is mapped to a MITRE ATT&CK technique. When an alert fires, you can immediately see: which tactic, which technique, and how it fits in the overall attack kill chain.

ATT&CK Navigator view shows your detection coverage as a heatmap overlaid on the full ATT&CK matrix. Green cells = you have detection for that technique. Empty cells = detection gaps where an attacker could operate undetected.

Professional value: Being able to say 'our Suricata ruleset covers 67% of ATT&CK techniques in the Initial Access and Execution tactics' is exactly the kind of metric security leadership wants to see. Security Onion makes this measurable.

15 min · View the ATT&CK coverage map in Security Onion
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 54 Checklist
  • 1Install Security Onion standalone in VirtualBox — minimum 4GB RAM, 80GB disk
  • 2Import a malware pcap using the pcap import feature in the web UI
  • 3Navigate to Alerts — count how many Suricata detections fired — note the top 3 rule names
  • 4Click one alert → pivot to the raw PCAP evidence — verify the detection makes sense
  • 5Use Hunt module to search for the C2 IP across all log sources simultaneously
Total Day 54 Study Time: 120 – 180 minutes (installation day)
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Official Security Onion — Official Documentation securityonionsolutions.com/docs
📺 Videos Security Onion Solutions — YouTube Channel youtube.com/@securityonionsolutions
💾 Download Security Onion — Free ISO Download securityonionsolutions.com
🎓 Course SANS SEC503 — Network Forensics (uses Security Onion) sans.org/courses/intrusion-detection-in-depth
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 54
  • 1Download Security Onion ISO from securityonionsolutions.com — start the VirtualBox install
  • 2While installing (45 min wait) — review the Security Onion YouTube channel for feature overview
  • 3After install — import a malware pcap — navigate to Alerts — count detections
  • 4Click one alert — note the ATT&CK technique mapping — pivot to raw PCAP — document the finding
Hands-On Task: 90 – 150 minutes (includes installation time)
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 54
"Security Onion on your resume is immediately impressive to enterprise SOC hiring managers. It shows enterprise-class monitoring knowledge that most candidates only claim theoretically."
Security Onion's pcap import feature is the fastest way to demonstrate the full Zeek + Suricata pipeline to a hiring manager during an interview. Upload a malware pcap, show Suricata alerts firing with ATT&CK mappings, pivot to Zeek conn.log for context, show the raw packet evidence. That 5-minute demo replaces an hour of explaining.
⚠ Common Mistake
Trying to run Security Onion with less than 4GB RAM. Security Onion runs Elasticsearch, Zeek, Suricata, Kibana, and several other services simultaneously. Below 4GB, the system will swap constantly and become unusable. Use 8GB if possible. If RAM is limited, use Security Onion's import mode (pcap analysis only) instead of live capture mode.
DAY 55
Network Threat Hunting Techniques
Threat hunters do not wait for alerts. They go looking for hidden attackers. Here is how.
#ThreatHunting#ATTACKFramework#Day55of90
dark What is Threat Hunting? Foundation
Threat Hunting: Proactive, Not Reactive
HypothesisATT&CKLong-Tail

Threat hunting is a proactive, analyst-driven search for threats that have already bypassed automated detection. The core assumption is: 'We have already been breached somewhere — where is the attacker hiding?'

Reactive vs Proactive: A SOC analyst waits for alerts to come to them — that is reactive. A threat hunter generates hypotheses, collects data, and searches for evidence — that is proactive. Same skills, different mindset.

Why it exists: Advanced attackers specifically design their tools and techniques to evade automated detection. Signature-based IDS misses what it has never seen. A skilled human analyst hunting with the right data catches what automation cannot.

20 min · SANS Threat Hunting free intro · attack.mitre.org
red Hypothesis-Based Hunting Methodology
Hypothesis Hunting: ATT&CK → Evidence

The structured process: MITRE ATT&CK technique → form a hypothesis → identify what network evidence would confirm it → collect that data → analyse → confirm or deny. Structured curiosity, not random searching.

Technique: T1071 (Application Layer Protocol) — adversaries use standard protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMTP) for C2 to blend in with normal traffic.
Hypothesis: 'Is there malware using HTTP beaconing to a C2 server, blending in with normal web traffic?'
Evidence to collect: HTTP connections to unusual destinations at regular intervals, unusual User-Agent strings, small regular HTTP POST requests.
Filter to apply: `http.request.method==POST && http.content_length < 100` — small regular POSTs from internal hosts to external IPs.

Result: Either you confirm the hypothesis (found the threat) or deny it (confirmed that specific technique is not present) — both are valuable outcomes.

25 min · attack.mitre.org — explore the Lateral Movement tactic
dark Baseline Deviation Hunting Technique
Baseline Deviation: Spot What Changed

Know normal to spot abnormal. Establish baselines for your environment — normal DNS query rate per host, normal number of external connections, normal protocols in use, normal working hours traffic patterns.

# In Zeek conn.log analysis with Python/Pandas: # Normal: each workstation makes ~50 external connections/hour # Anomaly: one workstation making 5000/hour # = possible beaconing, DGA, or exfiltration in progress
# DNS baseline example: # Normal: 5 DNS queries per minute per host # Anomaly: 500 queries per minute = DGA malware or DNS tunnel # Action: filter dns.flags.rcode==3 for that host # — look for NXDomain flood pattern

Statistical threshold: Flag any value beyond 2 standard deviations from the mean as worthy of investigation. Not every anomaly is malicious — but every attack IS anomalous relative to baseline.

20 min · Establish and document baselines from your own captures
white Long-Tail Analysis Advanced Technique
Long-Tail Analysis: Hunt in the Rare Events

The long-tail principle: In any large dataset, the most common events are almost certainly benign — thousands of hosts make the same DNS queries, visit the same websites, use the same applications. The rare, unique events are where attackers hide.

# Long-tail analysis with Zeek + Linux tools: # Find LEAST common external IPs (attackers hide here): cat conn.log | zeek-cut id.resp_h \ | sort | uniq -c | sort -n | head -20 # The IPs at the TOP (smallest count) = investigate first
# Rarest User-Agents (malware blends in poorly): cat http.log | zeek-cut user_agent \ | sort | uniq -c | sort -n | head -10 # Agents appearing only once = immediate investigation

Why it works: Your standard browser connects to the same CDNs, ad networks, and analytics services as thousands of other machines — generating massive frequency counts. Malware C2 traffic is unique — appears only in the rarest events.

20 min · Run long-tail analysis on a Zeek conn.log
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 55 Checklist
  • 1Pick 3 ATT&CK techniques from the Lateral Movement tactic — write a hunting hypothesis for each
  • 2Identify what network evidence would confirm or deny each hypothesis
  • 3Write a Wireshark filter or Zeek query for each hypothesis
  • 4Run long-tail analysis on a Zeek conn.log — identify the rarest external IPs and User-Agents
  • 5Understand the PEAK framework (Prepare, Execute, Act, Knowledge) — the interview answer for hunting methodology
Total Day 55 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🎯 Framework MITRE ATT&CK Navigator — Free ATT&CK Visualisation attack.mitre.org
📖 Framework TaHiTI — Threat Hunting Methodology (free PDF) betaalvereniging.nl/en/safety/tahiti
📄 Poster SANS Threat Hunting Poster (free PDF) sans.org/posters
🛠️ Tool HELK — Hunting ELK Project (GitHub, free) github.com/Cyb3rWard0g/HELK
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 55
  • 1Visit attack.mitre.org → Lateral Movement tactic → pick 3 techniques
  • 2Write a hunting hypothesis for each: 'Is [technique] present in my network traffic?'
  • 3Write a Wireshark display filter OR Zeek query that would detect evidence of each
  • 4Run long-tail analysis on a Zeek conn.log — document the 5 rarest external IPs and investigate each
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 55
"Threat hunting roles pay 20–40% more than reactive SOC analyst roles. Same skills, applied proactively instead of reactively. The career ceiling is dramatically higher."
The PEAK framework is the interview answer for 'explain your hunting methodology': Prepare (define scope, collect data, identify data sources) → Execute (run the hunt, analyse data) → Act on findings (document, escalate, remediate, create detections from confirmed threats) → Knowledge (update baselines, share findings with team). Four words that demonstrate operational maturity.
⚠ Common Mistake
Hunting without a documented hypothesis. Random data exploration occasionally finds threats by luck — but it is not repeatable, not scalable, and cannot be taught. Hypothesis-driven hunting produces documented, reproducible processes that can be refined over time. If you cannot write down your hypothesis before starting, you are not hunting — you are browsing.
DAY 56
Traffic Analysis Phase Recap & Certification Path
Traffic Analysis phase complete. You are a packet-reading, threat-hunting analyst.
#TrafficAnalysis#Recap#Day56of90
dark Phase 5 Recap Summary
14 Days of Traffic Analysis: Everything Covered
WiresharkZeek + SuricataNext: Git
Days 43–44: Wireshark setup, interface navigation, capture vs display filters, Follow Stream, Statistics
Days 45–46: TCP/IP deep dive (handshake, flags, scans), DNS analysis, DNS tunneling, DGA detection
Days 47–48: HTTP/HTTPS analysis, web attack detection, HTTPS decryption, tcpdump CLI capture
Days 49–50: Suricata IDS rule writing, malware traffic patterns (C2, beaconing, DGA, lateral movement)
Days 51–52: Zeek NSM log analysis, 5-step investigation framework, IOC extraction, incident reporting
Days 53–54: Python pcap automation (Scapy + PyShark), Security Onion enterprise platform
Day 55: Network threat hunting methodology, ATT&CK-based hypotheses, long-tail analysis
30 min · Review all 14 days of notes
red Traffic Analysis Certifications Career Path
Relevant Certifications for Your Skills
CompTIA CySA+ — directly tests traffic analysis, IDS/IPS management, threat hunting, and incident response. Everything from this phase is exam content. Moderate difficulty.
GIAC GCIA (Intrusion Analyst) — the network forensics gold standard. Heavy focus on pcap analysis, Zeek, Suricata, and IDS tuning. Premium certification, respected by SOC leadership.
Wireshark Certified Network Analyst (WCNA) — vendor-neutral Wireshark certification. Validates the skills from Days 43–47 specifically. Good for network-focused roles.
GIAC GNFA (Network Forensic Analyst) — specialises in network forensics investigation — exactly the workflow from Day 52. Premium, rare, highly valued in DFIR roles.
15 min · Compare certification requirements at giac.org
dark Career Paths Career Options
Career Paths: Traffic Analysis Skills Pay
SOC Analyst (Tier 1–3): Daily pcap analysis, Suricata alert triage, Zeek log investigation. Entry level uses all skills from this phase. $55–85k range depending on tier.
Threat Hunter: Proactive ATT&CK-based hunting as a full-time role. Uses everything from Day 55 daily. Senior role, $90–130k+ range.
Incident Responder: Network forensics is a core DFIR skill. The 5-step framework from Day 52 is the daily job. $80–120k+ range.
Network Security Engineer: Designs and tunes IDS/IPS systems. Writes custom Suricata rules. Deploys Zeek and Security Onion. $90–130k+ range.
Malware Analyst: Network traffic analysis combined with reverse engineering. Uses Wireshark + Zeek to understand malware behaviour. $85–130k+ range.
15 min · Research job descriptions on LinkedIn for each role

Your Traffic Analysis Portfolio

  • 2Include for each: attack timeline, C2 domain identified, malware family, MITRE ATT&CK mapping, Wireshark screenshots

  • 4Tag the repository: `network-forensics`, `wireshark`, `malware-analysis`, `zeek`, `suricata`

stats Phase 5 Complete Progress Stats
