quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 56
"Security+ plus Wireshark skills plus Python automation equals the top 10% of entry-level candidates. Most applicants have the cert. Almost none have demonstrated practical skills backed by GitHub evidence."
Keep practising malware pcap analysis after Day 56 — at least one pcap per week from malware-traffic-analysis.net. Wireshark intuition compounds over time in a way that is impossible to rush. The analyst who has seen 200 malware pcaps recognises attack patterns in seconds. That pattern recognition is the career-defining skill.
⚠ Common Mistake
Stopping traffic analysis practice after Day 56. Unlike knowledge-based skills that can be memorised, pcap analysis is a perceptual skill — like reading music or recognising chess patterns. It requires thousands of hours of practice to become truly fast and accurate. Keep practising beyond this phase and your skills will compound dramatically over the next 12 months.
6
Phase 06
Git Version Control
Days 57–63 · 7 sessions
DAY 57
Git Basics: Why Every Security Pro Needs Git
Every script, rule, config, and policy you write needs version control. Git week starts now.
#Git#VersionControl#Day57of90
dark What is Git? Foundation
Git: Version Control for Security Work
CommitsBranchesGitHub

Git is a distributed version control system that tracks every change ever made to every file in a project. Every security script, detection rule, incident playbook, hardening configuration, and policy document should live in Git.

Why security professionals need Git: It creates an auditable history of every change — who made it, when, and why. This is non-repudiation applied to your own work. Every professional security environment uses Git without exception.

Git is not optional. In 2026, submitting scripts or tools without Git history is the equivalent of submitting work with no version history, no collaboration capability, and no audit trail. It signals junior-level thinking regardless of technical skill.

20 min · git-scm.com/book — free Pro Git book
red Core Git Concepts Key Terminology
Git Vocabulary: 6 Concepts to Know Cold
Repository (repo): The project folder with complete change history. Contains all versions of every file since the project began. Your entire project's memory.
Commit: A named snapshot of your project at a specific moment. Includes a message describing what changed and why. The fundamental unit of Git history.
Branch: A parallel version of the codebase. Create a branch to develop a new scanner feature or detection rule without touching the stable main version.
Merge: Combine two branches back together. Integrates completed work into the main codebase after review.
Remote: A hosted copy of the repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket). Where your team collaborates and where your portfolio lives publicly.
Clone: Your local copy of a remote repository. What you get when you `git clone` someone else's project.
20 min · try.github.io — GitHub's official 25-minute interactive tutorial
dark First-Time Setup Configuration
First-Time Git Setup: One Machine, Once
# Run these once per machine — stored in ~/.gitconfig: git config --global user.name "Your Name" git config --global user.email "you@email.com" git config --global core.editor "nano" # or 'vim' or 'code'
# Verify your configuration: git config --list # Output: # user.name=Your Name # user.email=you@email.com # core.editor=nano
# Generate SSH key for GitHub auth (recommended over HTTPS): ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@email.com" cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub # Copy this to GitHub Settings → SSH Keys

Skipping setup creates problems: Without name/email config, your commits have no author attribution — making git blame and audit trails useless in team environments.

15 min · Set up Git on your machine right now
white The Daily Git Workflow Core Commands
Daily Workflow: 4 Commands, Endless Power
# 1. Create a new repository: git init my-security-tools cd my-security-tools
# 2. Stage changes for commit: git add scanner.py # Stage one file git add . # Stage ALL changes git status # See what is staged
# 3. Commit staged changes with a message: git commit -m "Add threaded port scanner with banner grabbing" # Message format: verb + what + brief why
# 4. Push to GitHub remote: git remote add origin git@github.com:user/repo.git git push -u origin main # First push sets upstream git push # Every push after that
25 min · git-scm.com/book Chapter 2 — Git Basics
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 57 Checklist
  • 1Install Git and run `git config --global` setup — verify with `git config --list`
  • 2Create a GitHub account if you do not have one — set up SSH key authentication
  • 3Create a repo called `cybersecurity-tools` — push your Python port scanner from Day 30
  • 4Write a README.md — tool name, description, installation steps, usage example with output
  • 5Understand why every security file needs Git — audit trail, collaboration, non-repudiation
Total Day 57 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Book Pro Git Book — Free, Definitive Git Reference git-scm.com/book
🧪 Interactive try.github.io — GitHub's Official 25-Min Tutorial try.github.io
📺 Course Codecademy — Learn Git & GitHub (free track) codecademy.com/learn/learn-git
🧪 Labs GitImmersion.com — 53 Hands-On Git Labs gitimmersion.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 57
  • 1Create a GitHub account → generate an SSH key → add the public key to GitHub Settings
  • 2Create a new public repo: `cybersecurity-tools` — initialise with a README
  • 3Clone the repo locally → add your port scanner from Day 30 → write a proper README.md
  • 4Commit and push: `git add .` → `git commit -m 'Add port scanner with banner grabbing'` → `git push`
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 57
"Your GitHub profile IS your security portfolio. Hiring managers and recruiters check GitHub before interviews. Consistent commits over 90 days are visible proof of dedication that no cover letter can replicate."
Write commit messages that explain WHY a change was made, not just WHAT. 'Fix port scanner' is useless. 'Fix port scanner timeout causing false FILTERED results on firewalled hosts' tells a story. Good commit messages are searchable, reviewable, and demonstrate engineering maturity to every recruiter who reads your history.
⚠ Common Mistake
Committing API keys, passwords, or .env files. Create a `.gitignore` file BEFORE your very first commit on any project. GitHub secret scanning finds exposed keys within seconds of a push — and so do automated scrapers. Exposed keys get abused within minutes. Prevention is infinitely easier than rotation and damage control.
DAY 58
Branching, Merging & Pull Requests
Branches let you experiment without breaking production. The safety net every team needs.
#Git#Branching#Day58of90
dark Why Branches? Foundation
Branches: Safe Experimentation at Scale
git checkout -bgit mergePull Request

A branch is a parallel, isolated copy of your codebase. Changes on a branch do not affect any other branch until you explicitly merge them. This makes it safe to experiment, develop, and test without risk to working code.

Security engineering use: Test a new Suricata detection rule on a branch — if it causes false positives, delete the branch with zero impact on production rules.
Development use: Build a new scanner feature in isolation — commit frequently without breaking the working version that others depend on.
Emergency use: Apply a critical hotfix on a dedicated branch — merge it immediately without waiting for incomplete feature work to be ready.

The fundamental rule: `main` branch is always stable and deployable. Work happens on branches. This is universal across every professional engineering team.

20 min · learngitbranching.js.org — free visual interactive tutorial
red Branch Commands Essential Commands
Branch Commands: Create, Switch, Delete
# Create a new branch AND switch to it: git checkout -b feature-dns-scanner # Equivalent to: git branch feature-dns-scanner && git checkout feature-dns-scanner
# List all local branches (* = current): git branch # List all branches including remote: git branch -a
# Switch between existing branches: git checkout main # Back to main git checkout feature-dns-scanner # Back to feature
# Delete a branch after merging: git branch -d feature-dns-scanner # Safe delete git branch -D feature-dns-scanner # Force delete (even if unmerged)
# Push a new branch to GitHub: git push -u origin feature-dns-scanner
20 min · learngitbranching.js.org — complete levels 1–4
dark Merging Combining Work
Merging: Combining Branches Back Together
# Merge feature branch into main: git checkout main # Switch to target branch git merge feature-dns-scanner # Merge the feature in git branch -d feature-dns-scanner # Clean up after merge
# Two merge types: # Fast-forward (clean, no divergence): # main: A---B # feature: A---B---C # Result: A---B---C (linear) # 3-way merge (branches diverged): # main: A---B---D # feature: A---B---C # Result: A---B---D---E (merge commit)

Merge conflicts happen when the same line was edited differently in both branches. Git marks the conflict with `<<<<<<` and `>>>>>>>` markers. Open the file, choose the correct version, delete the markers, then `git add filename` + `git commit` to complete the merge.

25 min · Practice merging two branches with a deliberate conflict
white Pull Requests Team Collaboration
Pull Requests: Code Review Before Deployment
1Create branch — `git checkout -b feature-ioc-checker` — write your code and commit to the branch.
2Push branch to GitHub — `git push -u origin feature-ioc-checker` — the branch now exists on the remote.
3Open Pull Request on GitHub — navigate to the repo → 'Compare & pull request' → write a description of what changed and why → submit for review.
4Review & discuss — teammates review the diff, leave comments, request changes. Discussion is preserved permanently in the PR thread — full audit trail.
5Merge — once approved, merge the PR into main. In security teams, ALL changes to detection rules, scripts, and policies go through PR review. No exceptions.
20 min · Open a PR on your cybersecurity-tools repo
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 58 Checklist
  • 1Create a branch `feature-banner-grabber` — add your Day 31 banner grabber script to it
  • 2Push the branch to GitHub — open a Pull Request describing what the tool does
  • 3Review your own PR — leave a comment, then merge it into main
  • 4Practice resolving a merge conflict — edit the same line in both branches, merge, and resolve
  • 5Complete learngitbranching.js.org levels 1–6 — visual mastery of branch concepts
Total Day 58 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🧪 Visual learngitbranching.js.org — Best Visual Branch Tutorial learngitbranching.js.org
📖 Guide Atlassian Git Branching Tutorial atlassian.com/git/tutorials/using-branches
🧪 Labs GitImmersion.com — Labs 18–30 (Branching) gitimmersion.com
📖 Docs GitHub Docs — About Pull Requests docs.github.com/en/pull-requests
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 58
  • 1Create branch `feature-banner-grabber` → add banner grabber script → commit with a descriptive message
  • 2Push the branch to GitHub → open a Pull Request with title and description
  • 3Review your own PR → add one comment → merge → delete the feature branch
  • 4Practice a conflict: edit README.md in both main and a test branch → merge → resolve conflict manually
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 58
"In your first security job, every script, rule, and policy change will go through a Pull Request for peer review before it touches production. Learning this workflow now means Day 1 feels natural."
Git Flow branching strategy is the professional standard: `main` (always stable) → `develop` (integration) → `feature/*` (one branch per feature) → `hotfix/*` (emergency production fixes). When you use this naming convention in personal projects, it signals that you have worked in professional team environments — even if you have not yet.
⚠ Common Mistake
Never push directly to `main` in any team environment. Feature branches and PRs are non-negotiable professional practice. Even for solo projects, using branches and PRs trains the habit. The first time you push a breaking change directly to production main in a real job, you will understand why this rule exists.
DAY 59
GitHub Portfolio & Secret Scanning
Your GitHub profile IS your security resume. Here is how to make it impossible to ignore.
#GitHub#Portfolio#Day59of90
dark GitHub Profile Optimization Foundation
GitHub Profile: Your Security Resume
Profile READMESecret Scanning.gitignore

GitHub profile = professional identity. Security engineering hiring managers and technical recruiters check GitHub profiles before scheduling interviews. A well-crafted profile with documented projects communicates competence before you say a single word.

Profile README: Create a repository named EXACTLY the same as your username — GitHub automatically renders its README.md on your profile page. This is your professional headline.
Pinned repositories: Pin your 6 best projects. These are the first things visitors see. Choose projects that demonstrate breadth — scanner, log analyser, OSINT tool, incident report, Sigma rule, automation script.
Contribution graph: The green square grid. Consistent daily commits over 90 days creates a dense, impressive pattern. Employers see this as visible proof of sustained effort — something impossible to fake.
Professional headline: Target role + key skills. 'Security Engineer | Python | Wireshark | Suricata | CTF Player' tells the whole story in one line.
20 min · docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile
red Writing a Great README Documentation
README: The First Impression of Every Repo
Tool name + one-line description: What does this tool do? Who is it for? Make it specific — 'Threaded port scanner with service banner grabbing and NVD CVE correlation' beats 'port scanner'.
Features list: 3–5 bullet points showing specific capabilities. Use action verbs: Scans, Extracts, Correlates, Generates, Alerts.
Installation: Exact commands from zero to running. `git clone`, `pip install -r requirements.txt`, done. Never assume the reader has anything pre-installed.
Usage with real output: Show the actual terminal output. Screenshots or ASCII blocks showing real results build immediate credibility.
Ethical use disclaimer: 'For authorised security testing only. Do not use against systems you do not own or have explicit permission to test.' This signals professional maturity and legal awareness.
25 min · Write or rewrite 3 READMEs using this structure
dark GitHub Secret Scanning Security
Secret Scanning: Find Exposed Keys

GitHub automatically scans every public push for exposed secrets — AWS access keys, API tokens, private keys, database credentials. If found, GitHub notifies you and the service provider immediately.

# Enable secret scanning for private repos: # GitHub repo → Settings → Security → Secret scanning # → Enable secret scanning # → Enable push protection (blocks pushes with secrets)

Push Protection (free for public repos) blocks the push entirely before the secret is exposed. This is the correct setting — prevention over detection.

If a secret IS exposed: Rotate it IMMEDIATELY — assume it was scraped by automated bots within 60 seconds of exposure. Changing the secret in your codebase and revoking the old one are both required. The exposed secret in git history must also be removed with `git filter-repo`.

15 min · Enable secret scanning on all your repos now
white Pre-commit Hooks & .gitignore Prevention
Pre-commit Hooks: Block Secrets Before Commit
# Install detect-secrets (blocks secrets before commit): pip install detect-secrets # Scan your repo for existing secrets: detect-secrets scan > .secrets.baseline detect-secrets audit .secrets.baseline
# Essential .gitignore entries for security projects: .env *.pem *.key *.p12 config/secrets* *credentials* venv/ __pycache__/ .DS_Store *.log reports/ .secrets.baseline
# Generate complete .gitignore automatically: # Visit gitignore.io → select Python, Linux, macOS, VS Code # → copy the generated file to your project root # ALWAYS create .gitignore BEFORE the very first commit

Critical rule: `.gitignore` only prevents FUTURE commits. If a secret was already committed, it lives in git history forever until you use `git filter-repo` to surgically remove it.

20 min · Set up .gitignore and detect-secrets on all projects
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 59 Checklist
  • 1Create your GitHub Profile README — skills, current learning, top 3 projects, LinkedIn link
  • 2Pin 6 repositories on your profile — choose projects that show breadth of skills
  • 3Add .gitignore to every repo that does not have one — use gitignore.io to generate it
  • 4Enable secret scanning and push protection on all your repositories
  • 5Run `git log --all -p | grep -i 'password\|api_key\|secret'` — audit all repos for exposed secrets
Total Day 59 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🔧 Tool gitignore.io — Auto-Generate .gitignore Files gitignore.io
🏷️ Badges shields.io — Add Professional Badges to READMEs shields.io
📖 Guide makeareadme.com — README Writing Guide makeareadme.com
📖 Docs GitHub Docs — Secret Scanning Setup docs.github.com/en/code-security/secret-scanning
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 59
  • 1Create your GitHub Profile README — repository named exactly the same as your username
  • 2Audit every repo: add .gitignore, add requirements.txt (Python repos), rewrite README with usage examples
  • 3Run the secret search: `git log --all -p | grep -i 'password\|api_key\|secret'` — fix any findings
  • 4Enable push protection on all repos: Settings → Security → Secret scanning → Push protection
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 59
"If you find a secret in git history, deleting the file does not help — it is still in every commit. Use `git filter-repo` to surgically remove it, force-push, then rotate the credential immediately."
Add shields.io badges to every README — Python version badge, license badge, last-commit badge. They take 5 minutes per repo and immediately signal that the project is maintained and professional. Recruiters who see a repo with proper badges, a clear README, and a GitHub Actions status badge make an instant positive assessment before reading a single line of code.
⚠ Common Mistake
Running `git rm` to delete a file containing a secret and thinking the problem is solved. `git rm` removes the file from the working tree and stages the deletion — but the file still exists in every previous commit in the history. Anyone who clones the repo and runs `git log -p` can still see the secret. Use `git filter-repo` for real removal.
DAY 60
Advanced Git: log, bisect, stash & blame
Senior engineers use these Git commands daily. Master them and stand out in every technical interview.
#Git#AdvancedGit#Day60of90
dark git log — Audit Trail Foundation
git log: Complete Audit Trail
git loggit blamegit bisect
# Compact one-line history: git log --oneline # a3f2b1c Add DNS scanner with subdomain enumeration # 8d4e7f2 Fix timeout causing false FILTERED results # 2c1a9b8 Initial port scanner — TCP threaded version
# Visual branch tree: git log --graph --all --oneline # Every change ever made to one file: git log -p scanner.py # Filter by author and date: git log --since='1 week ago' --author='Alice' git log --since='2026-01-01' --until='2026-04-01'

Security forensic use: In incident response on a compromised codebase, `git log -p filename` shows every change ever made to a specific file — including who made it, when, and exactly what bytes changed. This is your first step when investigating suspicious code changes.

20 min · git-scm.com/book Chapter 2 · ohshitgit.com
red git blame Accountability
git blame: Who Changed This Line?
# Show who last changed every line of a file: git blame firewall_rules.py # Output format: # commit_hash (Author Date Time Timezone LineNum) Code # a3f2b1c (Alice 2026-03-15 14:22:01 +0000 1) #!/usr/bin/env python3 # 8d4e7f2 (Bob 2026-04-01 09:15:43 +0000 2) import socket
# Blame a specific range of lines: git blame -L 10,25 scanner.py # Blame with commit context: git blame -e scanner.py # Show email instead of name

Security audit use cases: 'Who changed this authentication check on November 3rd?' 'Who removed the input validation from this API endpoint?' 'Who added this suspicious function?' Answers in seconds — non-repudiation through version history, the same CIA principle from Day 8 applied to code.

20 min · Run git blame on your own security scripts
dark git stash Context Switching
git stash: Save Work, Switch Context
# Save uncommitted work to a temporary stack: git stash # Your working directory is now clean # Switch to main to apply an urgent hotfix git checkout main git checkout -b hotfix-critical-vuln
# After fixing and merging the hotfix: git checkout feature-dns-scanner # Restore your stashed work: git stash pop # Restore latest stash (removes from stack) git stash apply # Restore latest stash (keeps in stack)
# Manage multiple stashes: git stash list # See all saved stashes git stash show stash@{1} # See what is in stash 1 git stash drop stash@{0} # Delete a specific stash git stash clear # Delete ALL stashes
15 min · Practice stash during a simulated context switch
white git bisect Security Gold
git bisect: Find the Breaking Commit
# Start bisect session: git bisect start git bisect bad # Current version is broken git bisect good v1.2.0 # This version was working fine # Git automatically checks out the midpoint commit
# At each midpoint — test the code, then tell Git: git bisect good # This commit is fine git bisect bad # This commit is broken # Git halves the search space and checks out the next midpoint # Repeat until Git identifies the exact culprit commit
# Git shows the exact commit: # 'a3f2b1c is the first bad commit' # Now examine that commit: git show a3f2b1c # Clean up after bisect: git bisect reset # Returns to original HEAD position

Security use case: 'When was this backdoor introduced?' `git bisect` finds the exact commit in O(log n) steps — 1000 commits requires only ~10 bisect steps. Invaluable for security audits of suspicious repositories.

25 min · Practice git bisect on your own repo
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 60 Checklist
  • 1Run `git log --graph --all --oneline` on your cybersecurity-tools repo — understand the visual output
  • 2Use `git blame` on your port scanner — identify exactly when each section was written
  • 3Practice `git stash` — stash uncommitted changes, switch branches, pop stash on return
  • 4Practice `git bisect` — introduce a deliberate bug, make commits, find the breaking commit
  • 5Memorise 3 critical Git interview answers — reset, revert, bisect (see Pro Tip)
Total Day 60 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🆘 Reference ohshitgit.com — Fix Every Common Git Mistake ohshitgit.com
📖 Book Pro Git — Chapter 7: Git Tools (bisect, blame, stash) git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Debugging-with-Git
📖 Tutorial Atlassian Advanced Git Tutorial atlassian.com/git/tutorials/advanced-overview
🧪 Labs GitImmersion.com — Labs 40–53 (Advanced Git) gitimmersion.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 60
  • 1Introduce a deliberate bug — remove a required import — commit it — make 3–4 more commits after
  • 2Use `git bisect start` → `git bisect bad` → `git bisect good v1.0` → test → good/bad → find the culprit
  • 3Record how many bisect steps it took — run `git bisect reset` to restore
  • 4Memorise these 3 interview answers: undo last commit = `git reset HEAD~1` / undo pushed commit = `git revert HEAD` / find breaking commit = `git bisect`
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 60
"Three Git answers to memorise cold for interviews: undo last commit keeping changes = `git reset HEAD~1` | undo a pushed commit safely = `git revert HEAD` | find which commit broke things = `git bisect`."
When searching git history for historically committed secrets or suspicious code, use `git log -S 'api_key'` (capital S, called the 'pickaxe'). It finds every commit that added or removed the exact string — not just commits that mention it in the message. This is the forensic command for security audits of repositories.
⚠ Common Mistake
Using `git push --force` when you need to rewrite history. Force push overwrites the remote branch completely — destroying any commits your teammates pushed since you last pulled. Use `git push --force-with-lease` instead — it fails safely if anyone else has pushed since your last pull, preventing accidental destruction of teammates' work.
DAY 61
GitHub Actions & DevSecOps Pipelines
Every commit triggers automated security checks. This is DevSecOps — and it starts free on GitHub.
#GitHubActions#DevSecOps#Day61of90
dark What are GitHub Actions? Foundation
GitHub Actions: Automate Everything
YAML Workflowbandit SASTpip-audit

GitHub Actions are automated workflows triggered by Git events — a push, a pull request, a schedule, or a manual trigger. They run on GitHub's servers — zero infrastructure required. Free for public repos, 2,000 minutes per month free for private repos.

DevSecOps principle: Shift Left. Move security checks earlier in the development process — from 'check after deployment' to 'check at every commit.' GitHub Actions makes this free and automatic.

# Workflow files live at: .github/workflows/security.yml # Basic structure: name: Security Scan on: [push, pull_request] # Trigger on any push or PR jobs: scan: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Run security scan run: echo 'Security check runs here'
20 min · docs.github.com/en/actions
red Security Use Cases Security Automation
Security Automation: Free on Every Commit
bandit: Python SAST scanner — finds hardcoded passwords, use of `eval()`, SQL injection risks, weak cryptography usage. Runs in 10 seconds on any Python codebase.
pip-audit: Scans requirements.txt for known CVEs in Python dependencies. Alerts you when a library you use has a newly disclosed vulnerability.
truffleHog: Deep secret scanner — detects API keys, tokens, and credentials in code and git history. More thorough than GitHub's built-in scanning.
semgrep: SAST for 20+ languages — finds security bugs using pattern-based rules. Free for open source. Has pre-built rule packs for OWASP Top 10.
trivy: Container image vulnerability scanner — checks Docker images for known CVE-affected packages. Essential for any containerised security tool.
20 min · github.com/marketplace — browse free security Actions
dark Your First Security Workflow Implementation
Your First Security Workflow: bandit + pip-audit
# .github/workflows/security.yml name: Security Checks on: push: branches: [ main, develop ] pull_request: branches: [ main ] jobs: security-scan: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Set up Python uses: actions/setup-python@v4 with: python-version: '3.11' - name: Install security tools run: pip install bandit pip-audit - name: Run bandit SAST run: bandit -r . -ll -x ./tests/ # -ll = medium+ severity - name: Run pip-audit CVE check run: pip-audit -r requirements.txt
30 min · Create this workflow in your cybersecurity-tools repo
white Scheduled Scans & GitHub Secrets Advanced Configuration
Scheduled Scans & Secure API Key Storage
# Schedule weekly scans (even without new commits): on: schedule: - cron: '0 6 * * 1' # Every Monday at 6:00 AM UTC push: branches: [ main ]
# Use GitHub Secrets for API keys (NEVER hardcode in YAML): # GitHub repo → Settings → Secrets → Actions → New secret # Name: VT_API_KEY # Value: your_actual_virustotal_api_key # Reference in workflow YAML: steps: - name: Check IOCs against VirusTotal env: VT_KEY: ${{ secrets.VT_API_KEY }} run: python ioc_checker.py --vt-key $VT_KEY

Scheduled scans catch new CVEs in existing dependencies that appear AFTER your last commit. A library you installed 6 months ago may have a critical vulnerability disclosed today. Scheduled weekly scanning is the only way to know.

20 min · Add scheduled scanning to your security workflow
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 61 Checklist
  • 1Create `.github/workflows/security.yml` with bandit and pip-audit steps
  • 2Push it to your cybersecurity-tools repo → watch the Actions tab — see the workflow run
  • 3Fix any bandit findings the scan reports — understand what vulnerability each finding represents
  • 4Add a scheduled scan using cron syntax — every Monday at 6:00 AM UTC
  • 5Store your VirusTotal API key in GitHub Secrets — reference it from the workflow file
Total Day 61 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Docs GitHub Actions — Official Documentation docs.github.com/en/actions
🛒 Marketplace GitHub Marketplace — 3000+ Security Actions github.com/marketplace?type=actions&query=security
🔧 Tool bandit — Python Security Linter bandit.readthedocs.io
🔧 Tool pip-audit — Python Dependency CVE Checker github.com/pypa/pip-audit
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 61
  • 1Create `.github/workflows/security.yml` with bandit + pip-audit steps exactly as shown
  • 2Push to your cybersecurity-tools repo → go to Actions tab → watch the workflow execute
  • 3Read the bandit output — identify any security findings — fix the highest severity ones
  • 4Add your VT_API_KEY to GitHub Secrets → reference it in the workflow → push and verify
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 61
"Being able to say 'I set up a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with automated security scanning' in an interview is DevSecOps experience — even from a personal project. It demonstrates shift-left security philosophy."
The GitHub Actions marketplace has over 3,000 pre-built Actions — many from major security vendors (Snyk, Semgrep, Checkmarx, SonarCloud). Every one of them has a free tier for public repos. Adding 2–3 security Actions to your pipeline transforms it from a basic YAML file into a professional-grade DevSecOps demonstration.
⚠ Common Mistake
Storing real secrets in workflow YAML files. Even if you think the file is private, workflow files are code — they get reviewed, forked, and versioned. Always use GitHub Secrets (Settings → Secrets → Actions) and reference them as `${{ secrets.SECRET_NAME }}` in YAML. This is the only acceptable way to use credentials in CI/CD pipelines.
DAY 62
Detection-as-Code: Sigma Rules & Git
Detection rules are code. Version them, review them, deploy them — like code. This is the future of security operations.
#Sigma#DetectionEngineering#Day62of90
dark What is Detection-as-Code? Foundation
Detection-as-Code: Rules in Git
Sigma YAMLsigma-cliATT&CK Tags

Detection-as-Code applies software engineering practices to security detection rules. Write detection rules as YAML/JSON files → store in Git → review via Pull Requests → auto-convert and deploy to SIEM. Rules are versioned, auditable, peer-reviewed, and portable.

Sigma is the universal detection rule format. Write a rule once in Sigma's vendor-neutral YAML format. Convert to any supported SIEM platform — Splunk SPL, Elastic KQL, Microsoft Sentinel KQL, IBM QRadar AQL — with a single command. One source of truth, infinite platform targets.

Why Git for detection rules: Every rule change has an author, a timestamp, and a reason. A rule that generates 10,000 false positives can be reverted in seconds. Rules can be tested on branches before deployment to production. This is how mature SOC teams operate.

20 min · github.com/SigmaHQ/sigma · sigmahq.io
red Sigma Rule Anatomy Rule Structure
Sigma Rule: Every Field Explained
title: SSH Brute Force Detection id: a8f2c3e1-4b7d-4f9a-b2c1-8e3d7f2a1b4c status: stable # stable / test / experimental description: Detects SSH brute force from single IP author: Your Name date: 2026/04/23 references: - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1110/001
logsource: product: linux service: auth detection: keywords: - 'Failed password' filter_legit: - '127.0.0.1' # Exclude localhost condition: keywords and not filter_legit falsepositives: - Legitimate users with forgotten passwords - Password managers with wrong stored passwords level: medium tags: - attack.credential_access - attack.t1110.001
25 min · Analyse 5 rules from SigmaHQ/sigma — identify each field
dark Sigma Rule Example Real Detection
Real Rule: PowerShell Encoded Command (T1059.001)
title: PowerShell Encoded Command Execution id: b9d3c4f2-5e8a-4c1b-a3d2-9f4e8c3b2a5d status: stable description: Detects PowerShell execution with encoded commands. Attackers use -EncodedCommand to obfuscate malicious scripts. author: Security Team tags: - attack.execution - attack.t1059.001
logsource: category: process_creation product: windows detection: selection: Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe' CommandLine|contains: - '-EncodedCommand' - '-enc ' - '-ec ' condition: selection falsepositives: - Legitimate administrative scripts using encoding - Software installation scripts level: high fields: - CommandLine - ParentImage - User
20 min · Write this rule and convert it using uncoder.io
white Converting with sigma-cli Platform Deployment
sigma-cli: One Rule, Every Platform
# Install sigma-cli: pip install sigma-cli # List available backends (target platforms): sigma list backends
# Convert Sigma rule to different platforms: sigma convert -t splunk rules/windows/powershell_encoded.yml # Output: Splunk SPL query ready to paste sigma convert -t elasticsearch rules/windows/powershell_encoded.yml # Output: Elasticsearch query in JSON sigma convert -t sentinel rules/windows/powershell_encoded.yml # Output: Microsoft Sentinel KQL

Free online alternative: uncoder.io converts Sigma rules in the browser — no installation needed. Paste your YAML, select target platform, get the converted query instantly. Perfect for exploring conversion output without CLI setup.

3,000+ free community rules in the SigmaHQ GitHub repo — covering Windows, Linux, cloud, network, web, and application log sources. Clone the repo and you have a detection library that enterprise SOC teams pay for.

20 min · Convert your SSH brute-force rule to 3 different platforms
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 62 Checklist
  • 1Clone github.com/SigmaHQ/sigma — browse `rules/linux/` and `rules/windows/process_creation/`
  • 2Pick 3 rules mapped to ATT&CK techniques from earlier weeks — read every field carefully
  • 3Convert each rule using uncoder.io — to Elasticsearch, Splunk, and Sentinel formats
  • 4Write your own SSH brute-force rule — all required fields, proper ATT&CK tags
  • 5Store your rule in your GitHub repo in a `sigma-rules/` directory — version control your detections
Total Day 62 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📦 Rules SigmaHQ — 3000+ Free Community Detection Rules github.com/SigmaHQ/sigma
🔄 Converter uncoder.io — Free Online Sigma Rule Converter uncoder.io
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Sigma' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/sigma
📖 Docs Sigma HQ — Official Sigma Documentation sigmahq.io
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 62
  • 1Clone SigmaHQ/sigma repo — browse `rules/linux/auditd/` and `rules/windows/process_creation/`
  • 2Pick 3 rules you recognise from earlier phases — document what each detects and what log source
  • 3Convert each rule at uncoder.io — paste the Elasticsearch output and note the query structure
  • 4Write the SSH brute-force Sigma rule — store it in `sigma-rules/` in your GitHub repo with a commit
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 62
"Detection Engineering is one of the fastest-growing, highest-paying security specialisations — $100–140k entry level at top companies. It combines SIEM + Sigma/YARA + Git + MITRE ATT&CK. Contributing even one PR to the SigmaHQ repo gives you public, verifiable proof of detection engineering skill."
The SigmaHQ repository accepts community contributions. If you write a quality detection rule that is not already covered in the repo — for a new malware technique, a novel attack pattern, or a cloud service not yet represented — submit a PR. A merged PR to SigmaHQ on your GitHub profile is a credential that no certification replicates.
⚠ Common Mistake
Deploying Sigma rules to production SIEM without testing against real log samples first. An untested rule with a broad pattern can fire thousands of false positive alerts — burying legitimate detections and burning out the SOC team investigating them. Always test against a representative sample of your logs before enabling in production.
DAY 63
Git Week Recap & Portfolio Push
Git week complete. 63 days in. Your GitHub is now your loudest professional statement. Let us finalise it.
#Git#Portfolio#Day63of90
dark Git Week Full Recap Summary
7 Days of Git: Everything You Learned
Git MasteryGitHub ActionsNext: ELK Stack
Day 57: Git init, add, commit, push — the 4 core commands. First-time setup. GitHub account and SSH keys. First portfolio repo live.
Day 58: Branching (`checkout -b`), merging, conflict resolution. Full Pull Request workflow on GitHub. Git Flow branching strategy.
Day 59: GitHub profile optimisation, professional README structure, secret scanning, push protection, pre-commit hooks, .gitignore essentials.
Day 60: git log (audit trail), git blame (accountability), git stash (context switching), git bisect (binary search for breaking commits).
Day 61: GitHub Actions CI/CD — bandit SAST, pip-audit dependency scanning, scheduled weekly scans, GitHub Secrets for API keys.
Day 62: Detection-as-Code with Sigma — rule anatomy, PowerShell detection example, sigma-cli conversion to Splunk/Elasticsearch/Sentinel, SigmaHQ community repo.
30 min · Review all 7 days of notes — git-scm.com/book for lifetime reference
red Portfolio Checklist Audit
Portfolio Checklist: Audit Your GitHub
✅ 6 repos pinned on your profile — covering port scanner, banner grabber, OSINT tool, IOC enricher, log parser, Sigma rules
✅ Every repo has a README with tool description, installation, usage example, and ethical use disclaimer
✅ Every Python repo has requirements.txt — generated with `pip freeze > requirements.txt`
✅ .gitignore on every repo — no secrets, no venv, no pycache
✅ GitHub Actions security pipeline on at least one repo — bandit + pip-audit running on every push
✅ Profile README — skills, current 90-day progress, top 3 projects, LinkedIn link
✅ Consistent commit history — green squares spanning 63+ days — visible proof of dedication
30 min · Audit every repo against this checklist right now
dark What Your GitHub Should Show Projects
Your 7 Portfolio Projects: The Full Story
Port scanner (Day 30) — threaded TCP scanner with banner grabbing and service detection
Domain OSINT tool (Day 36) — WHOIS + DNS record enumeration + subdomain brute-force
IOC enricher (Day 39) — VirusTotal + AbuseIPDB + GreyNoise multi-source enrichment
Apache log parser (Day 40) — Pandas analysis with matplotlib charts and anomaly detection
System audit script (Day 28) — Bash script: hostname, IPs, open ports, auth failures, SUID binaries
Sigma rules collection (Day 62) — SSH brute-force + PowerShell encoded command rules
Network forensics reports (Days 52–56) — 3 malware pcap investigations as Markdown case studies
20 min · Ensure all 7 projects are in your GitHub with proper documentation
white Adding Badges & ELK Preview Final Polish
README Badges & ELK Stack Preview
# Add shields.io badges to every README: ![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.11-3776AB?logo=python) ![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-green) ![Last Commit](https://img.shields.io/github/last-commit/yourusername/reponame) ![GitHub Actions](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/yourusername/reponame/security.yml)

Phase 7 Preview — ELK Stack (Days 64–70): This is the convergence week. Your Python log parsing skills (Phase 4) + Linux administration (Phase 3) + Git workflow (this week) + traffic analysis (Phase 5) all combine into building a complete, professional SIEM with Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana.

ELK is the most transformative week of the entire 90-day series. You will build a real-time security monitoring platform from scratch — ingesting your own system logs, visualising attacks in Kibana dashboards, and alerting on threats. This is exactly what enterprise SOC teams use.

20 min · Add badges to top 3 repos — preview ELK documentation

  • 3Push every remaining project from this week that is not yet on GitHub
  • 4Add shields.io badges to your top 3 repos — version, license, last commit, Actions status

stats Phase 6 Complete Progress Stats

quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 63
"A GitHub profile with 6+ documented security projects, a professional README, active commit history, and a GitHub Actions pipeline is worth more in technical hiring than most entry-level certifications."
Security engineering managers at top-tier companies explicitly say this: a strong GitHub portfolio with demonstrated, working tools beats a list of certifications from candidates who cannot write a working script. The reason is simple — certifications test knowledge, GitHub shows capability. Both are needed, but capability is rarer and more immediately valuable.
⚠ Common Mistake
Quantity over quality in your GitHub portfolio. Three exceptional, fully documented, working tools beat twenty empty repos with no README every single time. Employers click through. They open the code. They run the README instructions. A broken installation process, a missing requirements.txt, or an empty README immediately signals that the tool was built for show, not for use. Build fewer things, build them properly.
7
Phase 07
ELK Stack SIEM
Days 64–70 · 7 sessions
DAY 64
ELK Stack Introduction & Architecture
The world's most popular open-source SIEM costs $0. ELK Stack week starts now.
#ELKStack#SIEM#Day64of90
dark What is the ELK Stack? Foundation
ELK Stack: Free Enterprise SIEM
ElasticsearchLogstashKibana

ELK Stack = Elasticsearch (search + storage) + Logstash (ingestion + parsing) + Kibana (visualisation + dashboards). Now called the Elastic Stack with the addition of Beats (lightweight log shippers). Used by Netflix, Uber, LinkedIn, and thousands of enterprise SOC teams worldwide.

Why ELK for security: It is the most widely deployed open-source SIEM on the planet. ELK appears in 60%+ of SOC analyst job postings. Learning it on a home lab gives you the exact operational experience enterprise roles require — at zero cost.

Elastic Security — the built-in SIEM app inside Kibana — includes 2,000+ pre-built MITRE ATT&CK-aligned detection rules, endpoint detection, case management, and timeline investigation. All included in the free basic tier.

20 min · logz.io/learn/complete-elk-stack-tutorial
red Data Flow Architecture Architecture
Data Flow: Log Source to Dashboard
Step 1 — Log Source: Linux server, Windows host, firewall, web server, or any device generating logs — the raw data origin.
Step 2 — Filebeat Agent: Ultra-lightweight log shipper installed on the source. Reads log files and ships them to Logstash or directly to Elasticsearch. Uses ~50MB RAM.
Step 3 — Logstash: Receives raw logs, parses unstructured text into named fields using grok patterns, enriches with GeoIP, and forwards structured JSON to Elasticsearch.
Step 4 — Elasticsearch: Distributed search engine that indexes and stores all structured log documents. Every field is instantly searchable. Scales from one node to thousands.
Step 5 — Kibana: Visualisation and investigation layer. Dashboards, KQL queries, detection rules, alert management, timeline investigations, and case management all live here.
20 min · elastic.co/learn — free official ELK courses
dark Elasticsearch Basics Core Component
Elasticsearch: Every Log Instantly Searchable

Elasticsearch is a distributed search engine built on Apache Lucene. All data is stored as JSON documents inside indices. Every operation uses a REST API — every query, every index operation, every configuration change is an HTTP call.

# Check cluster health: curl http://localhost:9200/_cluster/health # List all indices: curl http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices?v # Search all log indices: curl http://localhost:9200/logs-*/_search # Count documents in an index: curl http://localhost:9200/ssh-auth-logs/_count

Index naming convention: Use date suffixes — `security-logs-2026.04.23`. Daily rotation keeps search performance high and enables easy data retention policies. Old indices are deleted automatically based on age.

20 min · elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current
white Installation Options Setup
Installation: 3 Ways to Run ELK
1Docker Compose (recommended for home lab): Single command starts the entire stack. Clone elastic/stack-docker from GitHub → `docker-compose up` → Kibana at localhost:5601 in 3 minutes. Requires 6–8GB RAM allocated to Docker.
2Elastic Cloud free trial: 14-day fully managed cloud deployment. No local setup required. Ideal for testing without RAM constraints. Perfect if your machine has less than 8GB RAM.
3Manual VM install: Install each component separately on a dedicated Ubuntu VM. Most learning value — you understand every configuration file. Requires 8GB+ RAM and 50GB+ disk.
45–60 min · Install ELK via Docker Compose today
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 64 Checklist
  • 1Install ELK via Docker Compose — clone elastic/stack-docker → `docker-compose up`
  • 2Open Kibana at localhost:5601 — log in with elastic/changeme — explore the interface
  • 3Navigate to the Security app in Kibana — note the Alerts, Rules, and Cases sections
  • 4Check Elasticsearch is running: `curl http://localhost:9200` — should return cluster info JSON
  • 5Understand the 5-step data flow — log source → Filebeat → Logstash → Elasticsearch → Kibana
Total Day 64 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes (installation day)
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Guide Logz.io — Complete ELK Stack Tutorial (free) logz.io/learn/complete-elk-stack-tutorial
🎓 Courses Elastic — Free Official Training Courses elastic.co/learn
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'ELK 101' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/investigatingwithelk101
🐳 Docker elastic/stack-docker — Official Docker Compose Setup github.com/elastic/stack-docker
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 64
  • 1Clone elastic/stack-docker from GitHub → run `docker-compose up -d` → wait 3 minutes
  • 2Open localhost:5601 in browser → log in → screenshot your running Kibana home screen
  • 3Navigate Security → Detections → Rules — count how many pre-built rules are available
  • 4Run `curl http://localhost:9200/_cat/indices?v` — see the default indices created by ELK
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes (includes installation)
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 64
"Being able to say 'I built and operated a home ELK SIEM, ingested real logs, created detection rules, and investigated simulated incidents' is a genuine differentiator at the entry level — not theoretical knowledge."
ELK, Splunk, and Microsoft Sentinel are the three SIEMs that appear in 90% of SOC job descriptions. You are learning ELK this week. Splunk free training is at trailhead.splunk.com. Sentinel has free sandbox labs at learn.microsoft.com. Complete all three and you cover the entire enterprise SIEM market — a combination almost no entry-level candidate has.
⚠ Common Mistake
Starting ELK with less than 8GB RAM allocated to Docker. Elasticsearch requires significant memory — it will start without error, appear to run normally, then crash silently when it runs out of heap space. Always set Docker Desktop memory limit to at least 6GB before starting. Check with `docker stats` after startup to verify Elasticsearch is stable.
DAY 65
Logstash Pipelines & Log Ingestion
Raw logs in. Structured intelligence out. Logstash is the pipeline that makes it happen.
#Logstash#LogIngestion#Day65of90
dark Logstash Pipeline Structure Foundation
Logstash Pipeline: Input → Filter → Output
grokGeoIPmutate

Every Logstash pipeline has exactly three sections: input (where data comes from), filter (parse, transform, enrich), and output (where enriched data goes). Config files live in `/etc/logstash/conf.d/` and are automatically loaded on startup.

# Basic pipeline structure: input { file { path => "/var/log/auth.log" start_position => "beginning" sincedb_path => "/dev/null" # Re-read from start } } filter { # Parse, transform, enrich here } output { elasticsearch { hosts => ["localhost:9200"] index => "ssh-auth-logs-%{+YYYY.MM.dd}" } }
20 min · elastic.co/guide/en/logstash/current
red Input Plugins Data Sources
Input Plugins: Where Data Comes From
# Read local log files: input { file { path => "/var/log/*.log" } }
# Receive from Filebeat agents (most common in production): input { beats { port => 5044 } }
# Receive syslog from network devices: input { syslog { port => 514 } # UDP by default }
# TCP input (custom log sources): input { tcp { port => 5000 codec => json_lines } }

Production deployments almost always use Beats agents → Logstash. Filebeat runs on each monitored system, handles buffering and backpressure, and ships reliably to Logstash's beats input on port 5044.

15 min · elastic.co/guide/en/logstash/current/input-plugins.html
dark grok Filter Most Important Filter
grok: Parse Any Log into Named Fields
# grok parses unstructured text using named patterns: filter { grok { match => { "message" => "%{SYSLOGTIMESTAMP:timestamp} %{HOSTNAME:hostname} sshd\[%{NUMBER:pid}\]: Failed password for %{USERNAME:username} from %{IP:src_ip} port %{NUMBER:src_port}" } } }
# Before grok: raw string # "Apr 23 14:22:01 server sshd[1234]: Failed password for root from 45.33.32.156 port 52341" # After grok: structured fields # timestamp: "Apr 23 14:22:01" # hostname: "server" # username: "root" # src_ip: "45.33.32.156" # src_port: "52341"

200+ built-in patterns cover Apache, SSH, syslog, Cisco, Windows Event Log, and more. Always test patterns at grokdebugger.com before deploying. A broken pattern silently tags every event as `_grokparsefailure` and fills Elasticsearch with unstructured, unsearchable data.

30 min · grokdebugger.com — test your SSH pattern now
white Enrichment Filters Intelligence Addition
Enrichment: Turn IPs into Intelligence
filter { grok { # ... parse SSH failed login as above ... } # Add geographic location to every source IP: geoip { source => "src_ip" # Adds: src_ip.geo.country_name, city_name, location } # Resolve IP to hostname: dns { reverse => ["src_ip"] action => "replace" } # Rename fields to ECS standard: mutate { rename => { "src_ip" => "source.ip" "username" => "user.name" } } }
20 min · Add GeoIP enrichment to your auth.log pipeline
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 65 Checklist
  • 1Write a Logstash pipeline that reads /var/log/auth.log using the file input plugin
  • 2Use grok to parse SSH failed login events — extract timestamp, username, and source IP
  • 3Add GeoIP enrichment to the source IP field — verify geo fields appear in Elasticsearch
  • 4Output to Elasticsearch with index name `ssh-auth-logs-YYYY.MM.dd`
  • 5Verify data in Kibana Discover — search the index and confirm all fields are parsed correctly
Total Day 65 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🔧 Tool grokdebugger.com — Test grok Patterns Before Deploying grokdebugger.com
📖 Docs Elastic — Logstash Official Documentation elastic.co/guide/en/logstash/current
📖 Tutorial Logz.io — Logstash Tutorial with Examples logz.io/learn/complete-elk-stack-tutorial
📺 Videos Elastic YouTube — Logstash Deep-Dive Playlist youtube.com/@elastic
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 65
  • 1Test your SSH grok pattern at grokdebugger.com — paste a sample auth.log line and iterate until all fields parse
  • 2Write the complete Logstash pipeline config — input, grok filter, GeoIP, mutate, output
  • 3Restart Logstash: `systemctl restart logstash` — check logs for errors: `journalctl -u logstash -f`
  • 4Verify in Kibana Discover: create an index pattern for `ssh-auth-logs-*` — confirm fields appear
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 65
"Always validate grok patterns at grokdebugger.com before writing a pipeline config. A single broken pattern silently tags ALL events as `_grokparsefailure` — destroying every dashboard that depends on those fields."
Set Logstash JVM heap in `/etc/logstash/jvm.options` to `-Xmx2g` (2GB maximum). Without this cap, Logstash will consume all available RAM as it processes logs — starving Elasticsearch and Kibana of memory and causing the entire stack to become unresponsive. Always cap Logstash heap explicitly in home lab environments.
⚠ Common Mistake
Skipping the grok pattern test step and writing pipelines directly in the config file. The iteration cycle for a broken grok pattern is: edit config → restart Logstash (60+ seconds) → wait for events → check if parsed correctly → repeat. At grokdebugger.com, the same iteration takes 5 seconds. Use the debugger for every new pattern, every time.
DAY 66
Kibana: Dashboards, KQL & Visualisations
Turn millions of raw log events into actionable visual intelligence in minutes. Welcome to Kibana.
#Kibana#KQL#Day66of90
dark Kibana Discover & KQL Foundation
KQL: The Language of SOC Analysis
KQLVisualiseDashboard

Kibana Discover is your primary raw log exploration workspace. It uses KQL (Kibana Query Language) for filtering — a powerful, readable query syntax that will become second nature within a week of daily use.

# Essential KQL patterns for security analysts: event.outcome: "failure" # Failed events only source.ip: "10.0.0.*" # IP range process.name: "cmd.exe" AND user.name: "SYSTEM" # Multi-field NOT event.type: "info" # Exclusion http.response.status_code: 404 # HTTP 404s @timestamp > "now-1h" # Last hour user.name: ("admin" OR "root" OR "Administrator") # Multiple values http.request.body.content: "SELECT *" # SQL injection

KQL is what you type hundreds of times per day in a SOC role. Every investigation starts with a KQL query. Master it now and every future platform — Elastic, Splunk SPL, Microsoft Sentinel KQL — becomes easier.

20 min · elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/current/kuery-query.html
red Creating Visualisations with Lens Visualisation
Kibana Lens: Drag-and-Drop Visualisations
1Open Lens: Kibana → Visualise Library → Create visualisation → Lens (recommended for all new dashboards).
2Bar chart — Failed logins per source IP: X-axis = `source.ip` (top values) · Y-axis = Count of records · Filter: `event.outcome: failure` → shows top attacking IPs.
3Line chart — Events over time: X-axis = `@timestamp` (auto-date histogram) · Y-axis = Count → shows traffic spikes and beaconing patterns.
4Metric panel — Alert count: Single metric, Count of records, filter by `kibana.alert.severity: high OR critical` → real-time high/critical alert count for your monitoring header.
30 min · Build all 4 visualisations from your auth.log data
dark Building a SOC Dashboard Dashboard
SOC Dashboard: Your Monitoring Homepage

Kibana → Dashboards → Create dashboard. Add your saved visualisations by clicking 'Add from library'. Arrange panels by drag-and-drop. Resize each panel to fit.

Authentication failures by IP (bar chart) — who is attacking?
Events over time (line chart) — when did the spike occur?
Top 10 source IPs (data table) — exact IP addresses with counts
Geographic map of connections (Maps layer) — where in the world?
Alert count by severity (metric panel) — how many critical alerts right now?

Save the dashboard as 'SOC Monitoring — Home'. Set it as your Kibana default homepage. Every time Kibana opens, you see the current security status of your monitored systems instantly.

30 min · Build and save your 5-panel SOC dashboard
white Maps & Saved Searches Advanced Features
Maps & Saved Searches: Persistence and Geography

Kibana Maps: Analytics → Maps → Add layer → Documents → select your index. Plots source IPs on a world map using GeoIP data from your Logstash pipeline. SSH brute-force visualised geographically creates a compelling visual for incident reports and management briefings.

Saved searches in Discover: Set a KQL filter → click Save → name it 'Failed SSH logins last 24h'. Saved searches are reusable in dashboards and shareable with teammates. Build a library of saved searches for your most common investigation starting points.

# Build your saved search library: 'Failed SSH logins last 24h' → event.outcome: failure AND process.name: sshd 'HTTP 404 mass scanning' → http.response.status_code: 404 AND @timestamp > now-1h 'New user accounts created' → event.action: user-created 'Privileged command execution' → process.name: (sudo OR su) AND event.outcome: success
20 min · Create the 4 saved searches above
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 66 Checklist
  • 1Write 5 KQL queries in Kibana Discover — multi-field, exclusion, IP range, time range, multiple values
  • 2Build 4 visualisations using Lens: bar chart, line chart, metric panel, data table
  • 3Assemble a 5-panel SOC dashboard — save it as your Kibana default homepage
  • 4Create a geographic map layer from your SSH auth data — see where attacks originate
  • 5Save 4 named searches in Discover — these become your daily investigation starting points
Total Day 66 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Reference Elastic — Full KQL Query Reference elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/current/kuery-query.html
📖 Docs Elastic — Kibana Official Documentation elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/current
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'ELK 101' Room (Kibana investigations) tryhackme.com/room/investigatingwithelk101
📺 Videos Elastic YouTube — Kibana Tutorials Playlist youtube.com/@elastic
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 66
  • 1Load Kibana's built-in sample web logs: Home → Try sample data → Sample web logs → Add data
  • 2Build a 4-panel dashboard: request count over time, top 10 IPs, HTTP response code distribution, geographic map
  • 3Save it as 'Web Traffic Monitor' — use it to practise dashboard navigation
  • 4Create 3 saved searches in Discover using KQL — one for failures, one for 404s, one for POST requests
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 66
"Master KQL deeply — it is what you type hundreds of times per day in a SOC role. `field: *` checks if a field exists · `field: (val1 OR val2)` matches either · `NOT field: value` excludes · `@timestamp > now-1h` for relative time."
Never hardcode absolute timestamps in saved dashboards. Always use relative time ranges like 'last 24 hours' or 'last 7 days' so dashboards remain permanently relevant without manual updates. An absolute timestamp (`@timestamp: [2026-04-01 TO 2026-04-02]`) is useful only in historical investigations — never in operational monitoring dashboards.
⚠ Common Mistake
Building dashboards before verifying data quality in Discover. If your grok parsing failed and fields are not correctly extracted, every visualisation built on those fields will be wrong — showing raw message strings instead of parsed IPs, counts of 1 per message, or empty charts. Always verify field structure in Discover first before building any visualisation.
DAY 67
Filebeat & Log Shipping from Real Sources
Filebeat is how logs travel from 1,000 servers to your SIEM in real time. Here is how it works.
#Filebeat#LogShipping#Day67of90
dark What is Filebeat? Foundation
Filebeat: Lightweight Log Shipper
FilebeatWinlogbeatModules

Filebeat is an ultra-lightweight log shipping agent that runs on monitored systems. It reads log files, handles backpressure, retries on failure, and ships reliably to Logstash or directly to Elasticsearch. It uses approximately 50MB RAM — negligible impact on production systems.

The Beats family — a collection of lightweight shippers for different data sources:

Filebeat — reads log files (the most common Beat)
Winlogbeat — reads Windows Event Log
Auditbeat — reads Linux auditd events with file integrity monitoring
Packetbeat — reads network packets (like a lightweight Zeek)
Elastic Agent — the modern unified agent replacing all individual Beats
20 min · elastic.co/beats/filebeat
red Filebeat Modules Pre-built Parsers
Filebeat Modules: One Command Setup
# Enable pre-built modules for common log sources: filebeat modules enable system filebeat modules enable nginx filebeat modules enable apache filebeat modules enable mysql filebeat modules enable suricata filebeat modules enable zeek filebeat modules enable auditd
# Deploy all dashboards to Kibana automatically: filebeat setup # Creates index patterns, visualisations, and dashboards # for every enabled module in one command
# Start Filebeat: systemctl start filebeat systemctl enable filebeat # Verify shipping: tail -f /var/log/filebeat/filebeat.log

Why modules matter: Within 5 minutes of enabling the system module and running `filebeat setup`, you have pre-built Kibana dashboards for SSH login monitoring, sudo usage, and system authentication events — with zero manual configuration.

25 min · Enable system module and run filebeat setup
dark Winlogbeat for Windows Security Windows Monitoring
Winlogbeat: Windows Security Events

Winlogbeat ships Windows Event Log to ELK. Install on any Windows machine to get real-time Windows security monitoring in your Kibana dashboard.

# Critical Windows Security Event IDs: 4624 — Successful logon (who logged in, from where) 4625 — Failed logon (brute force detection) 4688 — New process created (execution monitoring) 4698 — Scheduled task created (persistence detection) 4776 — NTLM authentication attempt 7045 — New service installed (malware persistence)

These 6 Event IDs cover 80% of Windows attack detection. A Winlogbeat deployment shipping these events to your ELK stack — combined with Elastic Security detection rules — creates real Windows threat detection in your home lab.

Configuration: Edit winlogbeat.yml — set Elasticsearch or Logstash output — run `.\ winlogbeat.exe setup` — start the service. Takes 10 minutes from download to shipping.

20 min · Install Winlogbeat on a Windows VM if available
white Auditbeat for Linux Linux Deep Monitoring
Auditbeat: Deep Linux Monitoring

Auditbeat ships Linux auditd events to ELK — providing detailed process execution, file integrity monitoring, user session tracking, and per-process network connections. More granular than Filebeat's system module.

# auditbeat.yml — key modules: auditbeat.modules: - module: auditd audit_rules: | -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity_changes -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity_changes -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k privilege_changes -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -k exec - module: file_integrity paths: - /etc - /usr/bin - /usr/sbin - /bin - /sbin

Compliance use: Auditbeat satisfies PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC2 file integrity monitoring requirements. The file_integrity module detects changes to critical system files — the same monitoring capability that commercial FIM products charge thousands for annually.

20 min · Install and configure Auditbeat on your Linux VM
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 67 Checklist
  • 1Install Filebeat on your Linux VM — enable the `system` module — run `filebeat setup`
  • 2Start Filebeat and verify logs are flowing: open Kibana → Dashboards → search 'Filebeat System'
  • 3Understand the 6 critical Windows Event IDs — when each fires and what attack it detects
  • 4Configure Auditbeat — add audit rules to monitor /etc/passwd and /etc/sudoers for changes
  • 5Verify your dual-OS monitoring concept: Linux via Filebeat + Windows via Winlogbeat = enterprise coverage
Total Day 67 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Docs Elastic — Filebeat Official Documentation elastic.co/guide/en/beats/filebeat/current
📖 Modules Elastic — Filebeat Module Reference List elastic.co/guide/en/beats/filebeat/current/filebeat-modules.html
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'ELK 101' Room (Filebeat config) tryhackme.com/room/investigatingwithelk101
📺 Videos Elastic Blog — Getting Started with Filebeat elastic.co/blog
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 67
  • 1Install Filebeat: `sudo apt install filebeat` — enable system module: `filebeat modules enable system`
  • 2Run `sudo filebeat setup` — wait for dashboard deployment — check Kibana Dashboards
  • 3Start Filebeat: `sudo systemctl start filebeat` — verify with `journalctl -u filebeat -f`
  • 4Open Kibana → Dashboards → 'Filebeat System — SSH login attempts' — verify your own auth.log is showing
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 67
"With Filebeat on your Linux VM and Winlogbeat on a Windows VM, you now monitor both operating systems — exactly what enterprise SOC teams do at scale. This dual-OS monitoring setup is worth explicitly stating in interviews."
In filebeat.yml, always configure either `output.logstash` OR `output.elasticsearch` — never both simultaneously. Having both outputs enabled causes Filebeat to silently drop events without any error in the logs. It appears to be working normally but data never arrives. This is one of the most common and frustrating Filebeat misconfiguration issues.
⚠ Common Mistake
Not running `filebeat test config` and `filebeat test output` before starting the service. These two commands validate your configuration file syntax AND verify the connection to your output (Logstash or Elasticsearch) before any events are processed. Running them before `systemctl start filebeat` saves hours of debugging dropped events.
DAY 68
Elastic Security: Detection Rules & Alerts
Your ELK stack becomes a real SIEM the moment you turn on detection rules. Here is how.
#ElasticSecurity#DetectionRules#Day68of90
dark Elastic Security Detection Engine Foundation
Elastic Security: 2000+ Free Detection Rules
Query RulesEQL SequencesThreshold Rules

Kibana → Security → Rules contains 2,000+ pre-built detection rules included free in the Elastic Stack basic tier. Every rule is mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques. Enable with a single click — no configuration required to start detecting threats.

Rule coverage: Credential access, lateral movement, persistence, privilege escalation, exfiltration, command and control, discovery, initial access — every ATT&CK tactic has dedicated rules out of the box.

github.com/elastic/detection-rules — the community rules repository. All 2,000+ rules are open source, viewable, forkable, and contributable. The same rules that protect enterprise environments are available for your home lab and your career learning.

20 min · elastic.co/guide/en/security/current/detection-engine-overview.html
red Rule Types Detection Methods
Rule Types: 5 Ways to Detect Threats
Query (KQL/EQL): Evaluates a KQL or EQL condition on a schedule. Fires when matching events exist. Most common rule type — covers 80% of detection use cases.
Threshold: Fires when the count of matching events exceeds N within a time window, grouped by a field. Perfect for brute force detection: more than 5 failures from the same IP in 60 seconds.
Event Correlation (EQL): Detects sequences of events — the most powerful rule type for catching multi-step attacks that individual event rules miss entirely.
Machine Learning: Uses Elastic's anomaly detection on log data — identifies statistically unusual behaviour without predefined signatures. Catches novel attacks.
Indicator Match: Matches ingested logs against a threat intelligence feed (IOC list). Automatically alerts when a known-malicious IP, domain, or file hash appears in your logs.
20 min · Explore each rule type in Kibana Security → Rules
dark Creating a Custom Rule Rule Creation
Custom Rule: SSH Brute Force Detection
# Custom Threshold Rule in Kibana Security: # Security → Rules → Create Rule → Threshold Name: SSH Brute Force Detected Index: filebeat-* Query: event.category: authentication AND event.outcome: failure Threshold: Group by: source.ip Count: > 5 Within: 1 minute Severity: High Risk Score: 73 ATT&CK: Credential Access / Brute Force (T1110) Actions: Create alert + Create case automatically

Save and enable the rule. It now evaluates every minute. When more than 5 failed SSH logins from the same source IP occur within 60 seconds, it fires an alert — exactly the brute-force pattern you have been studying since Day 22.

25 min · Create this custom rule in your Kibana instance
white EQL Sequence Detection Advanced Detection
EQL: Detect Multi-Step Attack Chains

EQL (Event Query Language) is Elastic's powerful language for detecting sequences of events across time. It catches attackers that individual event rules completely miss — because attackers deliberately spread their actions across multiple events.

# EQL Sequence — Detect recon → enumeration → C2: sequence by host.name [process where process.name == "whoami"] [process where process.name in ("net.exe", "netstat.exe")] [network where destination.port == 4444 and not destination.ip: "192.168.0.0/16"]
# EQL Sequence — PowerShell spawning cmd.exe spawning network: sequence by process.entity_id [process where process.name == "powershell.exe"] [process where process.name == "cmd.exe" and process.parent.name == "powershell.exe"] [network where destination.port != 443 and not destination.ip: "10.0.0.0/8"]

EQL sequences are the most powerful Elastic Security feature and the least understood by most candidates. Demonstrate one in an interview and you stand out from 95% of applicants.

25 min · Build and test one EQL sequence rule
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 68 Checklist
  • 1Enable 10 pre-built rules in Kibana Security → filter by 'Linux' and 'Authentication'
  • 2Create the SSH brute-force threshold rule exactly as described above
  • 3Simulate a brute force: `hydra -l root -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://localhost` — watch alerts fire
  • 4Build one EQL sequence rule — recon → enumeration → outbound connection
  • 5Configure an alert action — route high-severity alerts to a Slack webhook
Total Day 68 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Docs Elastic Security — Detection Engine Documentation elastic.co/guide/en/security/current/detection-engine-overview.html
📦 Rules elastic/detection-rules — 2000+ Open Source Rules github.com/elastic/detection-rules
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Elastic SIEM' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/investigatingwithelk101
📺 Video Elastic — EQL Tutorial (YouTube) youtube.com/@elastic
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 68
  • 1Enable 10 pre-built Linux authentication rules in Security → Rules → filter and enable
  • 2Create the custom SSH brute-force threshold rule with MITRE ATT&CK tag T1110
  • 3Run `hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt ssh://localhost` on your own VM — watch Security → Alerts
  • 4Investigate one fired alert — click through to the raw event — document what you see
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 68
"EQL sequence detection is the most powerful Elastic feature and the least understood. Build and demo one EQL sequence rule in an interview and you will stand out from 95% of applicants."
Run Hydra ONLY against your own localhost or an isolated lab VM — never against any other system, even accidentally. Hydra is loud — it fires thousands of authentication attempts rapidly. Running it against any system you do not own or have explicit written permission to test is unauthorised access under cybercrime law, regardless of intent. Always verify your target IP is your own machine before running.
⚠ Common Mistake
Enabling all 2,000+ pre-built rules simultaneously on a home lab with limited RAM. Each enabled rule runs on a schedule and consumes CPU and memory. Enabling hundreds at once causes Elasticsearch to become overloaded with rule evaluations, degrading performance across the entire stack. Enable rules selectively — 20–30 at a time — and monitor resource usage with `docker stats`.
DAY 69
SIEM Investigation Workflow: Alert to Report
An alert fires at 2am. Walk through a complete SOC investigation from alert to written report — inside ELK.
#SIEM#SOCWorkflow#Day69of90
dark The SOC Alert Workflow Foundation
SOC Workflow: Alert to Case Closed
TimelinePivotingCase Management

Every SOC investigation follows the same structured workflow — regardless of company, SIEM platform, or alert type. Knowing this workflow cold, and being able to execute it entirely within Kibana, is the Tier 1 analyst's core job description.

1. Alert triggers → Kibana Security detects rule match → alert created with severity, timestamp, affected hosts
2. Triage → Tier 1 analyst reviews — is this a true positive or false positive? Is it high priority?
3. Case created → formal tracking begins — title, severity, assignee, description
4. Timeline investigation → pivot through related events — build the attack narrative
5. IOC enrichment → check IPs, hashes, domains against threat intel
6. Escalate or close → hand to Tier 2 with full context, OR close with documented disposition
20 min · elastic.co/guide/en/security/current
red Timeline Investigation Core Skill
Timeline: Your Forensic Workbench
1Open Timeline: Security → Timelines → New Timeline. Give it a descriptive name: 'SSH Brute Force — 45.33.32.156 — 2026-04-23'.
2Add events: From Alerts or Discover, click 'Add to Timeline' on relevant events. Pin the critical ones — the first failed login, the successful authentication, the first command executed after login.
3Add analyst notes: Click any pinned event → Add note. Document why this event matters: 'This is the first successful login after 847 failed attempts from this IP — 4 minutes after attacks began.'
4Save and share: Save Timeline → attach it to your Case. Every significant investigation produces a named, saved Timeline as the forensic record.
25 min · Build a Timeline from yesterday's simulated brute-force
dark Analyst Pivoting Investigation Technique
Pivoting: Connect Every Evidence Piece

Pivoting is the core daily skill of a SOC analyst — following evidence connections from one piece of data to the next. In Kibana, every field value is clickable — filter for it or filter it out with one click.

1Alert shows suspicious source IP → click the IP → 'Filter for this value' → see ALL events from that IP in the last 24 hours.
2Find associated hostname → click the destination hostname → filter → see all services that host was running when the connection occurred.
3Find processes running at that time → switch to Auditbeat data → filter by host + timestamp → see every process execution during the attack window.
4Find suspicious parent-child process chains → filter for the suspicious process → check its parent_process.name → is cmd.exe spawning from Excel? That is malicious macro execution.
20 min · Practise the 4-step pivot chain on your brute-force data
white Cases Management Documentation
Cases: Full Audit Trail per Incident

Security → Cases → Create case. Every significant alert generates a case — the formal record of the investigation from first detection to final disposition.

# Case fields to fill: Title: SSH Brute Force — 45.33.32.156 — 2026-04-23 Severity: High Assignee: Your Name Tags: brute-force, SSH, T1110, credential-access Description: Automated SSH brute-force attack detected. 847 failed attempts from 45.33.32.156 over 4 minutes. One successful login at 14:22:01.

Add evidence: Attach the Timeline, add screenshots, link the original alert. Add notes at each investigation step — what you found, what you checked, what you concluded. Close with disposition: True Positive (attack confirmed), False Positive (benign activity), or Indeterminate (insufficient evidence).

Cases create the compliance audit trail — required for PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC2. Every alert reviewed, every investigation documented, every closure with justification.

20 min · Create a complete case for your simulated brute-force
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 69 Checklist
  • 1Open the brute-force alert from Day 68 — triage it in Kibana Security
  • 2Create a new Timeline — add 5 pinned events with analyst notes at each
  • 3Pivot through the investigation: source IP → destination host → running processes → parent processes
  • 4Create a Case titled 'SSH Brute Force — Lab Simulation' — fill all fields and attach Timeline
  • 5Close the case as True Positive with a 4-sentence written summary — this is exactly what Tier 1 analysts produce every shift
Total Day 69 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Docs Elastic Security — Investigation and Cases Docs elastic.co/guide/en/security/current
📄 Guide SANS — Incident Handler's Handbook (free PDF) sans.org/white-papers/incident-handlers-handbook
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Elastic SIEM' Investigation Room tryhackme.com/room/investigatingwithelk101
🔧 Tool TheHive Project — Free Case Management Alternative thehive-project.org
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 69
  • 1Open the SSH brute-force alert in Kibana Security — triage: is this real or a false positive?
  • 2Create Timeline 'SSH Brute Force — Lab' — pin 5 events — add a note explaining each
  • 3Create Case 'SSH Brute Force — Lab Simulation' — attach Timeline — fill all fields
  • 4Write the 4-sentence incident summary: attack type, source, timeline, disposition — this is your written deliverable
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 69
"The ability to walk an interviewer through 'how I investigate a SIEM alert' step by step — with specific Kibana views, pivoting techniques, case creation, and a written summary — places you above almost every other entry-level candidate."
Practise narrating the investigation workflow out loud until it feels completely natural. Record yourself explaining it. Watch the recording. Refine until you can walk through alert → triage → timeline → pivot → case → report in under 3 minutes with specific, confident details. That narration is what separates a technically competent candidate from one who actually gets the job offer.
⚠ Common Mistake
Closing alerts as False Positive without documenting WHY they are benign. Undocumented FP closures are how real attacks get missed during incident review. Every FP closure must explain: 'This alert fired because of X. I investigated by checking Y and Z. I determined it was benign because of W.' That documentation protects you professionally and builds institutional knowledge for your team.
DAY 70
ELK Week Recap & SIEM Certifications
ELK week done. You built a real SIEM from scratch. 70 days in. Phase 8 preview: Cloud Security.
#ELKStack#Recap#Day70of90
dark ELK Week Full Recap Summary
7 Days of ELK: What You Built
SIEM BuiltCertificationsNext: Cloud
Day 64: ELK architecture — Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Beats data flow. Docker Compose deployment. Elastic Security overview.
Day 65: Logstash pipelines — input plugins, grok parsing, GeoIP enrichment, mutate transforms, Elasticsearch output with daily index rotation.
Day 66: Kibana Discover — KQL queries, Lens visualisations, 5-panel SOC dashboard, geographic maps, saved searches library.
Day 67: Filebeat modules (system, nginx, auditd), Winlogbeat for Windows Event IDs, Auditbeat for file integrity, Elastic Agent as the future.
